title: TwinLeaf - Embedding fanout + hosting evaluation (discovery)
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2029-03-05
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~48 minutes
Attendees: Aisha (Redwood AE), Marcus (Redwood SE), Ravi (TwinLeaf Head of Search), Elena (Platform), Jonas (CTO), Sara (SRE), Mike (Data Scientist)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
- TwinLeaf is evaluating whether to use Redwood Hosted for initial rollout or go Dedicated/Private for low-latency reranking.
- Main concerns: embed dimensions and storage cost, fanout to vector DB, tail latency at 99.9, integration with existing FAISS/Chroma workflows, VPC/KMS/compliance.
- Agreed next step: pilot dataset and bench plan; Redwood to share pricing and test scaffolding.

Topics: embeddings dimensionality, hybrid search + rerank, hosting options (hosted vs dedicated vs private), vector DB fallback/fanout, throughput and tail latency, security (VPC, KMS, SOC2).

Transcript body:
[00:00] Aisha: hey everyone thanks for carving time today um quick intros on our side im aisha i run enterprise for redwood and with me is marcus lin, our solutions engineer.
[00:10] Marcus: hey all, hi hi.
[00:12] Ravi: hi Aisha, Marcus, ravi here. thanks.
[00:15] Jonas: jonas, excited to dig in.
[00:18] Elena: elena, platform.
[00:20] Sara: sara, s r e.
[00:21] Mike: mike, data science.
[00:24] Aisha: awesome quick agenda - we want to understand your current retrieval stack, your latency and qps targets, how you do rerank today, and then walk through where redwood fits (hosted, dedicated, private). then next steps. sound good?
[00:35] Ravi: yeah that's perfect. to start high level we run hybrid search today - dense vectors via FAISS in our infra plus a term-based index. we do a lightweight lexical filter then vector fanout to get top 200 then rerank with a 1.3B model for contextual scoring. goal is sub-200ms p95 on the rerank path, p99 ideally under 350ms.
[00:56] Marcus: ok that's helpful. when you say fanout to top 200 is that synchronous? and where do embeddings live, in-chunked s3 or hot cache?
[01:05] Ravi: synchronous fanout. embeddings in S3 but cached in a hot redis for the last week; we also pre-shard some heavy tables into faiss nodes.
[01:12] Marcus: got it. so two orthogonal concerns we see: 1) where to compute and serve embeddings and rerank — hosted (Redwood API) vs dedicated pools or private (VPC/on-prem) — and 2) where vector search lives (we integrate with external vector DBs or use our fallback).
[01:27] Mike: quick question - what's the overhead if we keep embeddings at 1,536 dims vs compress to 768? cost delta and, uh, quality tradeoffs?
[01:35] Marcus: good question. broadly 1,536 will give you higher recall for some semantic tasks at the cost of ~2x storage and slightly more compute per query on nearest-neighbor. we also recommend evaluating compact embeddings or PQ (product quantization) in the vector DB — redwood's runtime supports serving either full or quantized payloads and will do KV cache for repeated prefixes to cut token cost. apologies i said KV cache — k v — speaks weird on transcript.
[02:00] Elena: on hosting, our security team wants VPC peering and customer-managed KMS at minimum. SOC2 is table stakes. if we go hosted we need to know the isolation and egress patterns. can you run through hosted vs dedicated vs private?
[02:14] Aisha: sure. hosted is fastest to get started — you call redwood's API, we manage models and autoscaling. Dedicated is reserved GPU pools with capacity isolation and better SLOs; you get per-customer pools and can restrict models and traffic routes. Private gives you a customer-managed control plane — VPC peering, on-prem option, and support for your KMS/HSM. we can provide SOC2 artifacts and audit logs for all tiers; private gives the most control for egress and encryption.
[02:45] Sara: and latency-wise? we care about tail. our current p99 hits 420ms occasionally during peaks.
[02:52] Marcus: with Dedicated we often hit p95 sub-120ms for small rerank models when batching is tuned; p99 depends on your topology — network hop and vector DB fanout are usually the largest contributors. if you colocate rerank with the vector shards (same vpc/dedicated nodes) you cut that network tail.
[03:10] Ravi: right that's our question — do we route fanout from our faiss to redwood for rerank, or push data into redwood's vector subsystem? we have compliance limits around PII leaving our VPC.
[03:22] Marcus: two patterns we've seen: A) keep vector DB in your environment, call it to return ids, then call redwood for rerank only (embedding + model call), or B) push embeddings into a managed vector DB (we integrate with pinecone/chroma/weaviate) and let redwood orchestrate retrieval + rerank. pattern A minimizes data transfer but adds network hops; pattern B reduces latency but requires you to accept storing vectors in the managed DB (or use private deployment).
[03:45] Mike: also curious about fallback strategy on failures — if a vector DB times out do you have partial rerank or degrade gracefully?
[03:51] Marcus: yes we support fallback topologies — e.g., try primary vector DB, fallback to smaller in-memory index or prefix-cache within the runtime, then optionally fall back to lexical-only results. these are configurable routing policies. another trick is locally mirroring topk candidates to a small faiss in your app for emergency failover.
[04:10] Jonas: what about throughput — we are targeting sustained 600 QPS on retrieval+rerank during business hours with spikes to 2k. how would dedicated scale?
[04:20] Marcus: for that scale i'd recommend Dedicated with autoscaling pools sized to sustain baseline; we can size GPUs/replicas based on your model (1.3B reranker vs 7B) and sequence length. important knob: batching windows — larger batch improves throughput but can increase latency. we model cost/perf and can share sizing guidance.
[04:40] Ravi: can you quantify cost for the two options? even ballpark per 1M queries?
[04:44] Aisha: i'll follow up with a pricing matrix — hosted per-token/embedding; dedicated is a commit + unit rate; private is enterprise license. we'll include example math for 1.3B rerank at your QPS.
[04:56] Elena: we also use a home-grown annotation set for evals. how do we prevent regressions when you update model versions? do you have eval hooks?
[05:04] Marcus: yes Redwood Optimize has eval and rollout integration — you can register prompt/eval sets and get regression alerts. for pilots we suggest pinning the model and doing canary rollouts while watching quality metrics.
[05:15] Mike: re embeddings — if we reduce dims to 768 and use HNSW in Faiss, what does bank of weights look like for rerank? sorry phrase — what's the impact on downstream reranker?
[05:24] Marcus: shorter vectors can slightly reduce recall; reranker often compensates if candidate set still has positives. solid strategy: small-time A/B: compare 768 vs 1536 on your dev queries and sample user traffic. for production you might do 1536 for long-tail doc types and 768 for conversation-level caching.
[05:39] Aisha: quick check — do you have any hard compliance timelines or procurement windows?
[05:43] Jonas: ideally pilot in next 3 weeks and production Q3. procurement wants an NDA + standard security pack.
[05:49] Aisha: we can share SOC2, ISO artifacts and an initial MSA.
[05:53] Sara: on VPC peering — we use AWS, prefer privatelink where possible. does Redwood support privatelink or direct connect?
[05:59] Marcus: we support VPC peering and privatelink for Private and some Dedicated setups; direct connect can be arranged for Private/on-prem. we'll capture your network team requirements in the follow-up.
[06:10] Ravi: can we talk about durability and vector sync? if we push to your managed DB and you have replication, how do we ensure parity with our primary store?
[06:20] Marcus: common approach is to have incremental syncs with event logs; for strict parity customers run a short-lived verification job. if precise one-to-one is required, Private deployment or Dedicated with VPC reduces the operational surface.
[06:34] Mike: any gotchas with tokenization differences or special characters in our legal docs that affect embeddings?
[06:38] Marcus: tokenizer differences only matter for generation models; embeddings are mostly model-dependent. we do recommend normalizing some fields and removing boilerplate. also watch out for duplicate doc passages which can skew nearest neighbor.
[06:52] Elena: what about monitoring? we need traces from request down to vector DB calls.
[06:57] Marcus: Redwood Console exposes per-route latency, per-token cost, and tracing hooks. you can push traces to your observability backend or integrate with our trace IDs. error budgets and SLO dashboards are available on Dedicated and Private tiers.
[07:12] Ravi: is there an easy way to evaluate tail latency before committing? a smoke test?
[07:17] Marcus: yes we do a bench test — you provide sample payloads and we run end-to-end scenarios under load (fanout size, vector DB latency injection, rerank model size). we'll give you p50/p95/p99 numbers and a breakdown.
[07:29] Jonas: are there reference customers running a similar hybrid fanout pattern?
[07:33] Aisha: yes we've got several retail and search customers doing async fanout + rerank with dedicated pools; we can share anonymized results and a short case study.
[07:40] Sara: latency to europe/asia — we have global footprint; do you have region support?
[07:45] Marcus: yes hosted has multi-region endpoints; Dedicated can be provisioned in specific regions; Private obviously depends on your infra.
[07:52] Mika (noise, mislabel): [crosstalk] 
[07:55] Mike: one other concern — model quantization for rerank, do you support int8 / q4 variants?
[07:59] Marcus: we support quantized variants for many architectures and can recommend quant profiles based on quality tests. quantization can reduce memory and increase throughput but you should evaluate on your eval set.
[08:12] Ravi: ok this is helpful. so recap from our side: we want a bench test with our 10k doc sample, keep faiss in our VPC for pilot (pattern A) but test an alternate where we push vectors into managed DB to compare latency; we'll also test dims 768 vs 1536, and run p95/p99 targets under 600 sustained qps and spikes.
[08:30] Aisha: perfect. recap from redwood side: we'll send pilot pricing, bench plan, and security pack. we'll schedule se-deep dive next week to align on network and sample exchange.
[08:40] Jonas: thanks everyone.
[08:42] Marcus: thanks, i'll follow up with curl samples and a draft bench script.
[08:46] Aisha: thanks all. take care.

End of transcript

References and links noted in call:
- Pilot pricing spreadsheet (link shared after call)
- Redwood security pack (SOC2/ISO + network checklist)
- Bench test scaffold (GitHub gist link to sample load script)
- Example case study (retail fanout + dedicated)

Note: transcript contains some transcription errors, occasional speaker mislabels, and filler words.

title: Lynx Therapeutics — Inference health, economics & roadmap review
preview: Quarterly health review focused on usage trends, cost-to-serve, and upcoming roadmap items (private deployment and dedicated capacity). Discussed short-term right-sizing, benchmarking plan for 8k context, and security documentation for VPC/KMS. Agreed on next steps: benchmarks, pricing, and follow-up technical deep dive.
text: Quarterly health review focused on usage trends, cost-to-serve, and upcoming roadmap items (private deployment and dedicated capacity). Discussed short-term right-sizing, benchmarking plan for 8k context, and security documentation for VPC/KMS. Agreed on next steps: benchmarks, pricing, and follow-up technical deep dive.

title: Sunderfield Systems - renewal & multi-region scale + pricing amendment
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-06-21
Start time (UTC): 09:34
Duration: 48 minutes (recorded)
Attendees: Claire Donovan (Redwood AE), Marcus Lin (Redwood SE), Tara Gomez (Redwood CSM), Ravi Patel (CTO, Sunderfield), Sophie Laurent (Head of ML), Lina Chen (Cloud Architect), Mike Owens (Procurement)

Summary:
Quick renewal check-in to align on committed capacity, multi-region add (EU + APAC), pricing amendment, procurement timeline, and next steps for security/benchmarks. Customer leaning toward 12-month committed tier with a pilot for VPC Private deployment in EU and APAC edge regions.

Topics:
- Committed capacity tiers and burst policies
- Multi-region onboarding: EU (Frankfurt) and APAC (Singapore) + data residency concerns
- Dedicated vs hosted tradeoffs for latency/cost
- Contract amendment / procurement steps and timelines
- Security: SOC 2, KMS/HSM, SSO/SAML, audit logs
- Benchmark plan and success criteria (latency SLO ~ <150ms p95 for inference)

Action items (recapped in meeting): see metadata action_items

Transcript:
[00:00] Claire Donovan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call — Claire here from Redwood, Marcus our SE, Tara from CSM. Can you hear me okay?
[00:07] Ravi Patel: Yeah, loud and clear. Ravi here.
[00:10] Sophie Laurent: Sophie, yep.
[00:12] Lina Chen: Lina here.
[00:14] Mike Owens: Mike from procurement. Morning.
[00:20] Claire Donovan: Great. The agenda today is fairly straightforward: renewal cadence, committed tiers, the multi-region add — I know you flagged APAC and EU — and then the contract amendment and procurement timeline. Marcus, do you want to open on the capacity options?
[00:31] Marcus Lin: Sure. So from the tech side, the simplest is to pick one of our Dedicated tiers — that gives you reserved GPU pools, predictable throughput. We have the burst buffer to handle spiky traffic, and autoscaling can be tuned. For Sunderfield's traffic pattern Sophie shared, the 600 concurrent requests target maps to our 4x reserved GPU configuration, which is what I'd recommend if you want to hit the latency SLO.
[00:55] Sophie Laurent: Just to clarify — that 4x config is for the US region only, right? If we add EU and APAC, we need separate dedicated pools per region?
[01:05] Marcus Lin: Correct, dedicated pools are per-region. We can mirror the same commit in EU and APAC or use a mix: dedicated in EU, hosted in APAC for cost savings, and automatic routing for latency-sensitive traffic.
[01:18] Ravi Patel: What's the unit economics if we go dedicated in all three regions versus dedicated + hosted hybrid? Rough ballpark.
[01:25] Claire Donovan: I'll put numbers in the amendment spreadsheet, but quick answer: dedicated gives lower per-token cost at scale, but you pay the reserved capacity premium. Hybrid keeps committed spend down but increases per-token costs for burst traffic. We can model a 12-month commit that includes 3 reserved pools with a burst cap for hosted overflow — that'll be in the draft.
[01:44] Mike Owens: From procurement, we'll need a clear SOW and an amendment with the committed tiers called out. Also need legal to see any changes to data handling for EU and APAC. Do you have standard language for data residency?
[01:56] Tara Gomez: Yes, we have a data-residency clause and can attach the region-specific addendum. We'll link the SOC 2 report and our DPA.
[02:05] Claire Donovan: On security — Marcus, can you cover KMS/HSM and who owns keys for private deployments?
[02:11] Marcus Lin: For Redwood Private (VPC), we support customer-managed keys via KMS integration — you can supply keys through your cloud provider or HSM. For hosted, keys are managed by Redwood but we can do envelope encryption and segregate logs. There was a note about audit logs — we stream audit logs to your SIEM via syslog or a secure S3 bucket.
[02:32] Lina Chen: For the EU pilot we need HSM-backed keys, and customer-managed KMS. Also want to confirm VPC peering options — will that be possible?
[02:40] Marcus Lin: Yes, VPC peering and private link are supported. We'll share the VPC peering guide and the network CIDR requirements. There may be small TTL differences in the edge region, but we'll document it.
[02:53] Sophie Laurent: On latency — our target is p95 < 150ms for the main inference path. Any concerns hitting that in APAC edge?
[03:01] Marcus Lin: If you deploy Dedicated in APAC, hitting p95<150ms is realistic. With hosted fallback you'll see slightly higher p95 during spikes. Our KV cache and continuous batching are tuned to keep token-level latency low; quantization profiling also helps reduce GPU work. We should run a short benchmark with your test corpus to verify.
[03:20] Claire Donovan: Let's talk timeline. Mike, you mentioned procurement needs SOW and amendment. What does your internal timeline look like?
[03:27] Mike Owens: If we want to start onboarding mid-July, procurement needs the amendment by end of June, legal review first week of July, PO by July 15. So a target of June 25 for an initial amendment would be ideal.
[03:40] Claire Donovan: Okay that's doable. We'll get a draft by 6/25. Marcus — can you run the benchmark and share by 6/24?
[03:47] Marcus Lin: Yep, I'll schedule a 2-hour run against your sample corpus and share the raw numbers and a clean summary.
[03:53] Ravi Patel: One question on failover: if regional capacity saturates, what's automatic fallback look like? For example, if Frankfurt is full, do requests route to US or to a cheaper hosted replica?
[04:04] Marcus Lin: Smart routing policies can route based on latency, cost, or strict data residency. For GDPR-sensitive endpoints we can set strict policies to not leave the EU region. For non-sensitive traffic, we can route to US-hosted fallback. You can also define priority: Dedicated EU -> Dedicated US -> Hosted.
[04:22] Sophie Laurent: We will have some endpoints that cannot leave the EU. Need to ensure that in the amendment.
[04:27] Claire Donovan: Noted — add region-only constraints to the SOW.
[04:31] Mike Owens: Also want to confirm service levels. What are the SLA numbers for Dedicated?
[04:36] Tara Gomez: Dedicated SLAs are uptime-based — 99.9% for the serving runtime, and we have latency SLO guidance in the console. For enterprise customers we can discuss tighter SLAs in the amendment.
[04:48] Mike Owens: Will any of this require a separate enterprise support contract?
[04:51] Claire Donovan: The 12-month committed tier includes standard enterprise support. If you want 24/7 escalation or a named technical contact, that's an add-on. We can include options in the pricing spreadsheet.
[05:02] Ravi Patel: On telemetry and observability — can we get token-level cost breakdowns per route? We want to tag costs per product team.
[05:08] Marcus Lin: Yes, Redwood Console offers token-level drilling and per-route dashboards. We can provision multi-tenant RBAC to restrict view rights. We'll show you a short dashboard demo in the next call.
[05:20] Sophie Laurent: For the pilot, how long does it take to provision a DEDicated pool in a new region?
[05:25] Marcus Lin: Provisioning usually takes 48–72 hours once the commit is provisioned and the region has capacity. For APAC sometimes a bit longer during peak ordering. We recommend early notice.
[05:38] Lina Chen: If we start with hosted in APAC while the dedicated pool is being provisioned, how does billing look for the first month?
[05:45] Claire Donovan: Billing will reflect the mix: hosted usage billed at on-demand per-token and the dedicated reserved monthly charge will start once the pool is provisioned. We'll model a blended estimate in the amendment so you can see the invoice impact month-by-month.
[05:58] Mike Owens: On legal — we have a standard vendor amendment process. Our legal team needs to see the DPA, SOC 2 report, and any SOC 2 gap remediation plan if you have open items.
[06:09] Tara Gomez: We'll attach the latest SOC 2 Type II and our security packet. For any open items, we can provide remediation timelines.
[06:16] Ravi Patel: A couple of technical nitpicks — does Redwood support KV cache warming across regions? For example, if we spin up EU dedicated, can we prewarm common prompts?
[06:26] Marcus Lin: We support KV/pre-warm strategies. We can seed local caches via a controlled warm-up job. There's some network cost to transfer large KV states, but it's feasible.
[06:36] Sophie Laurent: Also curious about quantization. We saw notes about quantization-friendly execution paths — any limitations for our LLMs (GPT-style and a custom reranker)?
[06:47] Marcus Lin: Quantization profiles vary by model. Our runtime supports INT8/4 quant modes for many of the open models and some custom models with profile work. For reranker we usually keep FP16 unless we see no quality regression in the evals. We'll include recommended profiles in the model onboarding notes.
[07:05] Claire Donovan: Good. Let's pivot to the amendment structure — I'll outline the sections: committed tiers per region, burst policy and hosted overflow, security attachments, SLAs, and pricing table including per-token and per-embedding rates under the commit. Mike, any preferences on how the pricing table should be laid out for procurement?
[07:21] Mike Owens: Simple monthly charges: reserved monthly fee per region, estimated monthly hosted overflow costs, unit token rates, and any add-on support line items. We prefer a single sheet that the finance folks can ingest.
[07:33] Claire Donovan: Perfect. We'll produce that.
[07:36] Marcus Lin: I'll add a row in the benchmark sheet for p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, memory usage, and GPU utilization per model.
[07:45] Sophie Laurent: One more: are there any change fees if we adjust committed capacity mid-term? We might scale up in Q4.
[07:53] Claire Donovan: We can include scale-up clauses — typically you can re-up capacity mid-term with a pro-rated commit and a 30-day notice. We'll capture that in the amendment.
[08:03] Ravi Patel: For the EU pilot, we want to start with a 3-week validation after provisioning before flipping production traffic. Is that typical?
[08:10] Tara Gomez: Yes, a validation window is standard. We can do a phased rollout: canary -> 25% -> 100% over the validation window with automatic rollback policies.
[08:22] Lina Chen: Are rollbacks automated by the runtime? How granular are the policies?
[08:26] Marcus Lin: Policies can be configured by error rate, latency, and quality checks. Automatic rollback is supported and we can instrument canary traffic to check for regressions in output quality.
[08:37] Mike Owens: Regarding the SOC 2 deep-dive — who will attend from your side? We need someone technical.
[08:42] Tara Gomez: Marcus and one of our security engineers can join. We'll propose slots for the week of 6/28.
[08:50] Claire Donovan: Okay quick recap — action owners and dates: draft amendment by 6/25 (Claire), benchmark run and results by 6/24 (Marcus), customer confirms regions by 6/23 (Lina), procurement timeline shared by 6/25 (Mike). Also schedule SOC2/KMS deep-dive week of 6/28. Any additions?
[09:05] Ravi Patel: Can you also include a small appendix listing which APIs and SDK versions we need to upgrade to for the new runtime? Sophie will want that.
[09:12] Marcus Lin: Yep. I'll include an API/SDK compatibility appendix and a migration checklist — likely a minor client bump for caching improvements.
[09:20] Sophie Laurent: For the benchmark, please log tokenization differences. We saw slight tokenization variance on our test set previously.
[09:26] Marcus Lin: Will do.
[09:28] Claire Donovan: Anything else?
[09:30] Mike Owens: One last ask — include a draft PO template we can use to speed procurement.
[09:33] Claire Donovan: We'll attach a PO template.
[09:35] Tara Gomez: I'll send the SOC2 and security packet after the call.
[09:38] Claire Donovan: Great. Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the amendment and benchmark links.
[47:15] Claire Donovan: End of recording — thank you all.

Notes on transcription quality: Some speaker overlap and a few proper nouns may be slightly off (e.g., internal model names). Refer to attachments for the exact benchmark numbers and the draft amendment.
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-06-21
Start time (UTC): 09:34
Duration: 48 minutes (recorded)
Attendees: Claire Donovan (Redwood AE), Marcus Lin (Redwood SE), Tara Gomez (Redwood CSM), Ravi Patel (CTO, Sunderfield), Sophie Laurent (Head of ML), Lina Chen (Cloud Architect), Mike Owens (Procurement)

Summary:
Quick renewal check-in to align on committed capacity, multi-region add (EU + APAC), pricing amendment, procurement timeline, and next steps for security/benchmarks. Customer leaning toward 12-month committed tier with a pilot for VPC Private deployment in EU and APAC edge regions.

Topics:
- Committed capacity tiers and burst policies
- Multi-region onboarding: EU (Frankfurt) and APAC (Singapore) + data residency concerns
- Dedicated vs hosted tradeoffs for latency/cost
- Contract amendment / procurement steps and timelines
- Security: SOC 2, KMS/HSM, SSO/SAML, audit logs
- Benchmark plan and success criteria (latency SLO ~ <150ms p95 for inference)

Action items (recapped in meeting): see metadata action_items

Transcript:
[00:00] Claire Donovan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call — Claire here from Redwood, Marcus our SE, Tara from CSM. Can you hear me okay?
[00:07] Ravi Patel: Yeah, loud and clear. Ravi here.
[00:10] Sophie Laurent: Sophie, yep.
[00:12] Lina Chen: Lina here.
[00:14] Mike Owens: Mike from procurement. Morning.
[00:20] Claire Donovan: Great. The agenda today is fairly straightforward: renewal cadence, committed tiers, the multi-region add — I know you flagged APAC and EU — and then the contract amendment and procurement timeline. Marcus, do you want to open on the capacity options?
[00:31] Marcus Lin: Sure. So from the tech side, the simplest is to pick one of our Dedicated tiers — that gives you reserved GPU pools, predictable throughput. We have the burst buffer to handle spiky traffic, and autoscaling can be tuned. For Sunderfield's traffic pattern Sophie shared, the 600 concurrent requests target maps to our 4x reserved GPU configuration, which is what I'd recommend if you want to hit the latency SLO.
[00:55] Sophie Laurent: Just to clarify — that 4x config is for the US region only, right? If we add EU and APAC, we need separate dedicated pools per region?
[01:05] Marcus Lin: Correct, dedicated pools are per-region. We can mirror the same commit in EU and APAC or use a mix: dedicated in EU, hosted in APAC for cost savings, and automatic routing for latency-sensitive traffic.
[01:18] Ravi Patel: What's the unit economics if we go dedicated in all three regions versus dedicated + hosted hybrid? Rough ballpark.
[01:25] Claire Donovan: I'll put numbers in the amendment spreadsheet, but quick answer: dedicated gives lower per-token cost at scale, but you pay the reserved capacity premium. Hybrid keeps committed spend down but increases per-token costs for burst traffic. We can model a 12-month commit that includes 3 reserved pools with a burst cap for hosted overflow — that'll be in the draft.
[01:44] Mike Owens: From procurement, we'll need a clear SOW and an amendment with the committed tiers called out. Also need legal to see any changes to data handling for EU and APAC. Do you have standard language for data residency?
[01:56] Tara Gomez: Yes, we have a data-residency clause and can attach the region-specific addendum. We'll link the SOC 2 report and our DPA.
[02:05] Claire Donovan: On security — Marcus, can you cover KMS/HSM and who owns keys for private deployments?
[02:11] Marcus Lin: For Redwood Private (VPC), we support customer-managed keys via KMS integration — you can supply keys through your cloud provider or HSM. For hosted, keys are managed by Redwood but we can do envelope encryption and segregate logs. There was a note about audit logs — we stream audit logs to your SIEM via syslog or a secure S3 bucket.
[02:32] Lina Chen: For the EU pilot we need HSM-backed keys, and customer-managed KMS. Also want to confirm VPC peering options — will that be possible?
[02:40] Marcus Lin: Yes, VPC peering and private link are supported. We'll share the VPC peering guide and the network CIDR requirements. There may be small TTL differences in the edge region, but we'll document it.
[02:53] Sophie Laurent: On latency — our target is p95 < 150ms for the main inference path. Any concerns hitting that in APAC edge?
[03:01] Marcus Lin: If you deploy Dedicated in APAC, hitting p95<150ms is realistic. With hosted fallback you'll see slightly higher p95 during spikes. Our KV cache and continuous batching are tuned to keep token-level latency low; quantization profiling also helps reduce GPU work. We should run a short benchmark with your test corpus to verify.
[03:20] Claire Donovan: Let's talk timeline. Mike, you mentioned procurement needs SOW and amendment. What does your internal timeline look like?
[03:27] Mike Owens: If we want to start onboarding mid-July, procurement needs the amendment by end of June, legal review first week of July, PO by July 15. So a target of June 25 for an initial amendment would be ideal.
[03:40] Claire Donovan: Okay that's doable. We'll get a draft by 6/25. Marcus — can you run the benchmark and share by 6/24?
[03:47] Marcus Lin: Yep, I'll schedule a 2-hour run against your sample corpus and share the raw numbers and a clean summary.
[03:53] Ravi Patel: One question on failover: if regional capacity saturates, what's automatic fallback look like? For example, if Frankfurt is full, do requests route to US or to a cheaper hosted replica?
[04:04] Marcus Lin: Smart routing policies can route based on latency, cost, or strict data residency. For GDPR-sensitive endpoints we can set strict policies to not leave the EU region. For non-sensitive traffic, we can route to US-hosted fallback. You can also define priority: Dedicated EU -> Dedicated US -> Hosted.
[04:22] Sophie Laurent: We will have some endpoints that cannot leave the EU. Need to ensure that in the amendment.
[04:27] Claire Donovan: Noted — add region-only constraints to the SOW.
[04:31] Mike Owens: Also want to confirm service levels. What are the SLA numbers for Dedicated?
[04:36] Tara Gomez: Dedicated SLAs are uptime-based — 99.9% for the serving runtime, and we have latency SLO guidance in the console. For enterprise customers we can discuss tighter SLAs in the amendment.
[04:48] Mike Owens: Will any of this require a separate enterprise support contract?
[04:51] Claire Donovan: The 12-month committed tier includes standard enterprise support. If you want 24/7 escalation or a named technical contact, that's an add-on. We can include options in the pricing spreadsheet.
[05:02] Ravi Patel: On telemetry and observability — can we get token-level cost breakdowns per route? We want to tag costs per product team.
[05:08] Marcus Lin: Yes, Redwood Console offers token-level drilling and per-route dashboards. We can provision multi-tenant RBAC to restrict view rights. We'll show you a short dashboard demo in the next call.
[05:20] Sophie Laurent: For the pilot, how long does it take to provision a DEDicated pool in a new region?
[05:25] Marcus Lin: Provisioning usually takes 48–72 hours once the commit is provisioned and the region has capacity. For APAC sometimes a bit longer during peak ordering. We recommend early notice.
[05:38] Lina Chen: If we start with hosted in APAC while the dedicated pool is being provisioned, how does billing look for the first month?
[05:45] Claire Donovan: Billing will reflect the mix: hosted usage billed at on-demand per-token and the dedicated reserved monthly charge will start once the pool is provisioned. We'll model a blended estimate in the amendment so you can see the invoice impact month-by-month.
[05:58] Mike Owens: On legal — we have a standard vendor amendment process. Our legal team needs to see the DPA, SOC 2 report, and any SOC 2 gap remediation plan if you have open items.
[06:09] Tara Gomez: We'll attach the latest SOC 2 Type II and our security packet. For any open items, we can provide remediation timelines.
[06:16] Ravi Patel: A couple of technical nitpicks — does Redwood support KV cache warming across regions? For example, if we spin up EU dedicated, can we prewarm common prompts?
[06:26] Marcus Lin: We support KV/pre-warm strategies. We can seed local caches via a controlled warm-up job. There's some network cost to transfer large KV states, but it's feasible.
[06:36] Sophie Laurent: Also curious about quantization. We saw notes about quantization-friendly execution paths — any limitations for our LLMs (GPT-style and a custom reranker)?
[06:47] Marcus Lin: Quantization profiles vary by model. Our runtime supports INT8/4 quant modes for many of the open models and some custom models with profile work. For reranker we usually keep FP16 unless we see no quality regression in the evals. We'll include recommended profiles in the model onboarding notes.
[07:05] Claire Donovan: Good. Let's pivot to the amendment structure — I'll outline the sections: committed tiers per region, burst policy and hosted overflow, security attachments, SLAs, and pricing table including per-token and per-embedding rates under the commit. Mike, any preferences on how the pricing table should be laid out for procurement?
[07:21] Mike Owens: Simple monthly charges: reserved monthly fee per region, estimated monthly hosted overflow costs, unit token rates, and any add-on support line items. We prefer a single sheet that the finance folks can ingest.
[07:33] Claire Donovan: Perfect. We'll produce that.
[07:36] Marcus Lin: I'll add a row in the benchmark sheet for p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput, memory usage, and GPU utilization per model.
[07:45] Sophie Laurent: One more: are there any change fees if we adjust committed capacity mid-term? We might scale up in Q4.
[07:53] Claire Donovan: We can include scale-up clauses — typically you can re-up capacity mid-term with a pro-rated commit and a 30-day notice. We'll capture that in the amendment.
[08:03] Ravi Patel: For the EU pilot, we want to start with a 3-week validation after provisioning before flipping production traffic. Is that typical?
[08:10] Tara Gomez: Yes, a validation window is standard. We can do a phased rollout: canary -> 25% -> 100% over the validation window with automatic rollback policies.
[08:22] Lina Chen: Are rollbacks automated by the runtime? How granular are the policies?
[08:26] Marcus Lin: Policies can be configured by error rate, latency, and quality checks. Automatic rollback is supported and we can instrument canary traffic to check for regressions in output quality.
[08:37] Mike Owens: Regarding the SOC 2 deep-dive — who will attend from your side? We need someone technical.
[08:42] Tara Gomez: Marcus and one of our security engineers can join. We'll propose slots for the week of 6/28.
[08:50] Claire Donovan: Okay quick recap — action owners and dates: draft amendment by 6/25 (Claire), benchmark run and results by 6/24 (Marcus), customer confirms regions by 6/23 (Lina), procurement timeline shared by 6/25 (Mike). Also schedule SOC2/KMS deep-dive week of 6/28. Any additions?
[09:05] Ravi Patel: Can you also include a small appendix listing which APIs and SDK versions we need to upgrade to for the new runtime? Sophie will want that.
[09:12] Marcus Lin: Yep. I'll include an API/SDK compatibility appendix and a migration checklist — likely a minor client bump for caching improvements.
[09:20] Sophie Laurent: For the benchmark, please log tokenization differences. We saw slight tokenization variance on our test set previously.
[09:26] Marcus Lin: Will do.
[09:28] Claire Donovan: Anything else?
[09:30] Mike Owens: One last ask — include a draft PO template we can use to speed procurement.
[09:33] Claire Donovan: We'll attach a PO template.
[09:35] Tara Gomez: I'll send the SOC2 and security packet after the call.
[09:38] Claire Donovan: Great. Thanks everyone — we'll follow up with the amendment and benchmark links.
[47:15] Claire Donovan: End of recording — thank you all.

Notes on transcription quality: Some speaker overlap and a few proper nouns may be slightly off (e.g., internal model names). Refer to attachments for the exact benchmark numbers and the draft amendment.

title: Exec approval and KMS egress playcall
preview:
Header:
Date: 2027-06-08 UTC | Start: 16:00 | Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Ethan Cole (Redwood AE), Rina Patel (Redwood SE), Marcus Lin (Redwood CSM); Olivia Hart (Sablecore Legal), Daniel Cho (Sablecore CISO), Marcus Vega (Cloud Eng), Priya Menon (Procurement)

Summary:
Quick sync to align exec sign-off requirements, confirm KMS and egress billing expectations, and agree next steps for MSA/DPA redlines and pilot provisioning. Legal wants updated indemnity and escrow language; security wants explicit KMS key handling and audit logs; procurement wants clarity on committed spend/term and any egress fee schedule.

Topics covered: MSA redlines, DPA exceptions, SLA levels and remedies, KMS/HSM integrations, network egress fees, Sandbox Dedicated pilot, timeline to procurement approval.

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood): Hi everyone thanks for carving out time. we're aware this is the exec gating call, we'll try to run through main asks and leave time for questions. short agenda: msadpa redlines, kms and egres—egress, and the pilot path.
[00:18] Olivia (Sablecore): Thanks Ethan. to be clear legal has two sticking points: indemnity caps and data escrow. we also had a request from regulatory to be explicit about data residency in the DPA.
[00:32] Priya (Procurement): And from procurement side we need the committed spend tiers and clarity on any outbound egress charges to our cloud provider. that was a surprise line item in the initial TCO.
[00:45] Rina (Redwood SE): quick one — when you say egress charges, do you mean egress to your downstream data lakes or to third parties? that changes how we propose fee offsets.
[00:56] Marcus V. (Cloud Eng): mostly to our data lake in azure; we also have occasional transfers to partners in eu and apac. so residency and transfer costs matter.
[01:10] Rina: got it. For the Dedicated deployment we can pin region and keep data in your VPC — that eliminates cross-region egress for most requests, but any partner transfers will still generate egress at the cloud layer.
[01:28] Daniel (CISO): on kms: we cannot hand over unwrapped keys. we require customer-managed keys in an HSM/KMS and full audit logging for cryptographic operations. also we're looking for key rotation processes and proof of no key material stored in the Redwood control plane.
[01:45] Ethan: Understood. Redwood Private supports customer-managed KMS; we have integrations with AWS KMS and Google Cloud KMS, and we support bringing your HSM via BYOK. Rina, do you want to walk through the flow?
[01:58] Rina: sure — high level we present a diagram: requests to the inference cluster reference a key that only your KMS can unwrap; we keep only key IDs and never the raw material. all unwrap ops are logged; we can push logs to your logging endpoint or to our audit stream. note: in the hosted scenario there is an extra hop, but private/dedicated eliminates that.
[02:20] Marcus L. (CSM): small note — audit retention defaults are 90 days but we can extend for enterprise add-on (we'll attach pricing).
[02:30] Olivia: for legal, we need an explicit statement in the DPA that cryptographic keys are customer-managed and that Redwood has no access to plaintext data during inference. is there language you already use for that?
[02:42] Ethan: we do, but legal often wants stronger wording. i'll send the standard clause and propose a tightened variation for escrow and access controls.
[02:50] Olivia: also, indemnity. our counsel wants a mutual indemnity except for willful misconduct; caps need to be aligned with our procurement policy. how flexible is Redwood on that?
[03:02] Ethan: we can be flexible on a case-by-case basis. the usual path is to negotiate a cap tied to either annual committed spend or a multiple of fees paid in the prior 12 months. for strategic enterprise deals we can do exceptions upward with exec approval.
[03:18] Priya: ok if procurement needs to see thresholds — what are the tiers and discounts if we commit to a 12-month Reserved Dedicated contract?
[03:26] Marcus L: basic model — self-serve hosted is pay-as-you-go; Dedicated has tiered discounts at 3,6,12 month commits and reserved GPU pool sizing. Rina can share a matrix but typical discounts are 15–30% depending on commit and quantization profile.
[03:44] Rina: i'll send the discount matrix. important to note: cost can also be reduced via batching and prefix KV cache, so we can simulate your expected token mix in the pilot to get more accurate numbers.
[03:58] Marcus V: for pilot sizing, we're expecting around 60 qps with 1.2k avg seq length during peak. that was estimated from our telemetry. any ballpark unit costs for that pattern?
[04:12] Rina: rough math — with a mid-sized Llama variant and 16-bit quantization on dedicated gpus, that pattern maps to X tokens/sec and we can produce a cost projection. we'll run a bench in the sandbox cluster.
[04:24] Daniel: I also want to confirm SLA. Our procurement requires 99.95 for customer-facing APIs, and we need credit language and a runbook for incident response.
[04:34] Ethan: Our standard enterprise SLA for Dedicated is 99.9 with options to step up to 99.95 on multi-region Dedicated with warm standby. Credits are tiered — we provide service credits and a defined RCA process. for runbook we can offer a partnership template.
[04:52] Olivia: will legal accept credits as the exclusive remedy? we prefer some additional remedies in the case of repeated failures.
[04:59] Ethan: there is sometimes pushback on contractual remediation beyond credits; however for certain enterprise customers we have accepted escalated SLAs, financial termination rights on repeated breaches — that's on a negotiation path and depends on exec sign-off.
[05:14] Priya: timeline wise we need procurement committee approval by 2027-06-22. exec sign-off will be next week — so legal redline needs to be in place by 2027-06-11 ideally.
[05:26] Olivia: yes that's our target.
[05:30] Rina: to make legal comfortable on kms and egress, we'll produce the KMS integration diagram and a clear egress examples table (egress to S3 in us-east, egress to partner IP ranges). that should help quantification.
[05:42] Marcus V: also, note our compliance team flagged the audit trail — we need tamper-evident logs and SOC2 alignment; please confirm where audit logs live and access patterns.
[05:53] Rina: audit logs can be pushed to your logging endpoint (syslog or cloud logging). we keep an internal immutable store for 90 days and can configure longer retention or ship to your S3 with object lock if you require immutability.
[06:06] Daniel: hmm object lock and legal hold is important for some workflows. is there cost for pushing logs out?
[06:11] Marcus L: shipping logs to your storage is included up to a reasonable volume; excessive log egress may have fees — we'll call out thresholds.
[06:20] Olivia: switching to data residency: our regulator requires that all PII from EU subjects stay in EU region at rest and in transit. is there a simple way to confirm region pinning?
[06:32] Rina: yes, Dedicated private can be pinned to eu-west-1 and network paths can be secured via private peering. we will document the deployment topology in the DPA exhibits.
[06:45] Priya: back to pricing — what happens if we need to burst across regions during peak? any automatic egress charge or fallback to hosted?
[06:55] Ethan: there are routing policies: we can configure smart routing to keep latency SLOs and fallback to hosted if you allow it — that would be an extra tier and would incur different rates. otherwise we can provision reserved burst capacity in the Dedicated pool for a fee.
[07:10] Olivia: procurement also asked about escrow of model artifacts and source access in case of vendor insolvency. is that standard?
[07:18] Ethan: model escrow is not standard but we do have an enterprise option: escrow of customer-specific artifacts and config. we can consider third-party escrow for critical IP, but need legal to define triggers.
[07:31] Olivia: we'll draft triggers.
[07:34] (brief cross talk — coffee being poured)
[07:50] Rina: quick note on security: credential rotation and least-privileged roles are supported via our RBAC; we can map to your IdP groups and enforce short-lived credentials for operator access.
[08:04] Daniel: we will insist on SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning for user sync. any limitations?
[08:11] Rina: full SAML SSO is supported; SCIM provisioning is available for Dedicated and Private deployments. for hosted, there's an extra managed provisioning step.
[08:22] Priya: ok good.
[08:25] Ethan: practical next steps: we'll circulate the MSA/DPA suggested compromise and Rina will attach technical exhibits: KMS diagram, audit log flow, and deployment topology. in parallel Rina/Marcus L will provision a sandbox Dedicated cluster for the pilot and run a capacity bench.
[08:44] Marcus V: on benching — can you validate that the sandbox will mirror our network egress path so we can observe egress numbers?
[08:52] Rina: we can mirror topology using a peered test VPC; we may require IP allowlist and the team's support to open the peering.
[09:02] Olivia: we also need to confirm liability carve-outs for cross-border transfers.
[09:09] Ethan: we'll propose language and annotate the DPA with transfer mechanisms and SCCs if needed.
[09:18] (silence as documents are referenced)
[09:36] Priya: one procurement question — do you have pre-approved payment terms for enterprise (net30, net45)?
[09:42] Ethan: standard is net30; we can consider net45 for large customers with finance approval. there are invoicing options via marketplace as well.
[09:51] Olivia: before we close: on indemnity — would Redwood accept a cap equal to 12 months committed fees for the first year, with a review at renewal?
[09:58] Ethan: that's reasonable for many enterprise deals, subject to exec approval. we'll flag for pricing/ops.
[10:06] Marcus L: i'll note that as a proposed approach in the action items.
[10:12] Rina: last technical point — if you require HSM-on-prem, latency will be different; we should simulate unwrap latency in the bench.
[10:21] Marcus V: good point. we have strict 50ms P95 for crypto unwrap in our path. can you meet that?
[10:27] Rina: we'll test; if not, we can add local unwrap cache while preserving key control — will outline caveats.
[10:36] Olivia: ok sounds like we have enough to move legal redlines.
[10:39] Ethan: recap of agreed items: redwood to send MSA/DPA redline with proposed indemnity language, KMS integration diagrams, Dedicated sandbox provisioning and bench, and a clear egress examples table. legal to provide final DPA clause list and procurement to confirm timeline.
[10:55] Priya: procurement timeline confirmed — committee by 2027-06-22.
[11:00] Daniel: security will review bench results and the audit log retention exhibit.
[11:06] Olivia: legal will aim to turn redlines around by 2027-06-11.
[11:12] Ethan: perfect. thanks everyone we'll follow up with a consolidated packet.
[11:20] (meeting ends)

Post-call notes (auto-generated):
- Risk profile: medium — KMS and indemnity are the high friction points.
- Suggested negotiation levers: tie indemnity cap to committed spend; offer extended audit retention as part of enterprise add-on; propose a pilot-only price protection clause for the initial 90 days.
- Attachments promised: MSA redline (Ethan), KMS diagram + audit flow (Rina), discount matrix and bench schedule (Marcus L).

Questions recorded during call:
- Olivia: Will Redwood accept customer-managed keys with no access to plaintext? (Answered: yes via BYOK/HSM, details to be documented)
- Daniel: Can you meet 50ms unwrap P95? (Answered: to be bench-tested in sandbox; fallback options discussed)

End of transcript.

[02:42] Ethan: we do, but legal often wants stronger wording. i'll send the standard clause and propose a tightened variation for escrow and access controls.
[02:50] Olivia: also, indemnity. our counsel wants a mutual indemnity except for willful misconduct; caps need to be aligned with our procurement policy. how flexible is Redwood on that?
[03:02] Ethan: we can be flexible on a case-by-case basis. the usual path is to negotiate a cap tied to either annual committed spend or a multiple of fees paid in the prior 12 months. for strategic enterprise deals we can do exceptions upward with exec approval.
[03:18] Priya: ok if procurement needs to see thresholds — what are the tiers and discounts if we commit to a 12-month Reserved Dedicated contract?
[03:26] Marcus L: basic model — self-serve hosted is pay-as-you-go; Dedicated has tiered discounts at 3,6,12 month commits and reserved GPU pool sizing. Rina can share a matrix but typical discounts are 15–30% depending on commit and quantization profile.
[03:44] Rina: i'll send the discount matrix. important to note: cost can also be reduced via batching and prefix KV cache, so we can simulate your expected token mix in the pilot to get more accurate numbers.
[03:58] Marcus V: for pilot sizing, we're expecting around 60 qps with 1.2k avg seq length during peak. that was estimated from our telemetry. any ballpark unit costs for that pattern?
[04:12] Rina: rough math — with a mid-sized Llama variant and 16-bit quantization on dedicated gpus, that pattern maps to X tokens/sec and we can produce a cost projection. we'll run a bench in the sandbox cluster.
[04:24] Daniel: I also want to confirm SLA. Our procurement requires 99.95 for customer-facing APIs, and we need credit language and a runbook for incident response.
[04:34] Ethan: Our standard enterprise SLA for Dedicated is 99.9 with options to step up to 99.95 on multi-region Dedicated with warm standby. Credits are tiered — we provide service credits and a defined RCA process. for runbook we can offer a partnership template.
[04:52] Olivia: will legal accept credits as the exclusive remedy? we prefer some additional remedies in the case of repeated failures.
[04:59] Ethan: there is sometimes pushback on contractual remediation beyond credits; however for certain enterprise customers we have accepted escalated SLAs, financial termination rights on repeated breaches — that's on a negotiation path and depends on exec sign-off.
[05:14] Priya: timeline wise we need procurement committee approval by 2027-06-22. exec sign-off will be next week — so legal redline needs to be in place by 2027-06-11 ideally.
[05:26] Olivia: yes that's our target.
[05:30] Rina: to make legal comfortable on kms and egress, we'll produce the KMS integration diagram and a clear egress examples table (egress to S3 in us-east, egress to partner IP ranges). that should help quantification.
[05:42] Marcus V: also, note our compliance team flagged the audit trail — we need tamper-evident logs and SOC2 alignment; please confirm where audit logs live and access patterns.
[05:53] Rina: audit logs can be pushed to your logging endpoint (syslog or cloud logging). we keep an internal immutable store for 90 days and can configure longer retention or ship to your S3 with object lock if you require immutability.
[06:06] Daniel: hmm object lock and legal hold is important for some workflows. is there cost for pushing logs out?
[06:11] Marcus L: shipping logs to your storage is included up to a reasonable volume; excessive log egress may have fees — we'll call out thresholds.
[06:20] Olivia: switching to data residency: our regulator requires that all PII from EU subjects stay in EU region at rest and in transit. is there a simple way to confirm region pinning?
[06:32] Rina: yes, Dedicated private can be pinned to eu-west-1 and network paths can be secured via private peering. we will document the deployment topology in the DPA exhibits.
[06:45] Priya: back to pricing — what happens if we need to burst across regions during peak? any automatic egress charge or fallback to hosted?
[06:55] Ethan: there are routing policies: we can configure smart routing to keep latency SLOs and fallback to hosted if you allow it — that would be an extra tier and would incur different rates. otherwise we can provision reserved burst capacity in the Dedicated pool for a fee.
[07:10] Olivia: procurement also asked about escrow of model artifacts and source access in case of vendor insolvency. is that standard?
[07:18] Ethan: model escrow is not standard but we do have an enterprise option: escrow of customer-specific artifacts and config. we can consider third-party escrow for critical IP, but need legal to define triggers.
[07:31] Olivia: we'll draft triggers.
[07:34] (brief cross talk — coffee being poured)
[07:50] Rina: quick note on security: credential rotation and least-privileged roles are supported via our RBAC; we can map to your IdP groups and enforce short-lived credentials for operator access.
[08:04] Daniel: we will insist on SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning for user sync. any limitations?
[08:11] Rina: full SAML SSO is supported; SCIM provisioning is available for Dedicated and Private deployments. for hosted, there's an extra managed provisioning step.
[08:22] Priya: ok good.
[08:25] Ethan: practical next steps: we'll circulate the MSA/DPA suggested compromise and Rina will attach technical exhibits: KMS diagram, audit log flow, and deployment topology. in parallel Rina/Marcus L will provision a sandbox Dedicated cluster for the pilot and run a capacity bench.
[08:44] Marcus V: on benching — can you validate that the sandbox will mirror our network egress path so we can observe egress numbers?
[08:52] Rina: we can mirror topology using a peered test VPC; we may require IP allowlist and the team's support to open the peering.
[09:02] Olivia: we also need to confirm liability carve-outs for cross-border transfers.
[09:09] Ethan: we'll propose language and annotate the DPA with transfer mechanisms and SCCs if needed.
[09:18] (silence as documents are referenced)
[09:36] Priya: one procurement question — do you have pre-approved payment terms for enterprise (net30, net45)?
[09:42] Ethan: standard is net30; we can consider net45 for large customers with finance approval. there are invoicing options via marketplace as well.
[09:51] Olivia: before we close: on indemnity — would Redwood accept a cap equal to 12 months committed fees for the first year, with a review at renewal?
[09:58] Ethan: that's reasonable for many enterprise deals, subject to exec approval. we'll flag for pricing/ops.
[10:06] Marcus L: i'll note that as a proposed approach in the action items.
[10:12] Rina: last technical point — if you require HSM-on-prem, latency will be different; we should simulate unwrap latency in the bench.
[10:21] Marcus V: good point. we have strict 50ms P95 for crypto unwrap in our path. can you meet that?
[10:27] Rina: we'll test; if not, we can add local unwrap cache while preserving key control — will outline caveats.
[10:36] Olivia: ok sounds like we have enough to move legal redlines.
[10:39] Ethan: recap of agreed items: redwood to send MSA/DPA redline with proposed indemnity language, KMS integration diagrams, Dedicated sandbox provisioning and bench, and a clear egress examples table. legal to provide final DPA clause list and procurement to confirm timeline.
[10:55] Priya: procurement timeline confirmed — committee by 2027-06-22.
[11:00] Daniel: security will review bench results and the audit log retention exhibit.
[11:06] Olivia: legal will aim to turn redlines around by 2027-06-11.
[11:12] Ethan: perfect. thanks everyone we'll follow up with a consolidated packet.
[11:20] (meeting ends)

Post-call notes (auto-generated):
- Risk profile: medium — KMS and indemnity are the high friction points.
- Suggested negotiation levers: tie indemnity cap to committed spend; offer extended audit retention as part of enterprise add-on; propose a pilot-only price protection clause for the initial 90 days.
- Attachments promised: MSA redline (Ethan), KMS diagram + audit flow (Rina), discount matrix and bench schedule (Marcus L).

Questions recorded during call:
- Olivia: Will Redwood accept customer-managed keys with no access to plaintext? (Answered: yes via BYOK/HSM, details to be documented)
- Daniel: Can you meet 50ms unwrap P95? (Answered: to be bench-tested in sandbox; fallback options discussed)

End of transcript.

title: Multi-subnet egress strategy + KV cache & batching lab
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-12-05
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: 58m
Attendees: Evan Cole (Redwood AE), Priya Shah (Redwood SE), Marcus Lin (Redwood SRE); Aisha Khan (Nimbus Infra Lead), Marco Ruiz (Nimbus ML), Samira Patel (Nimbus Security), Tom Baker (Nimbus Platform), Olive Ng (Nimbus DevOps)

Auto-generated summary (may be partial):
- Deep dive on multi-subnet egress strategies for Private VPC deployment, focusing on split-homing vs centralized NAT, PrivateLink endpoints, and hairpinning edge cases.
- Performance lab agenda: KV cache behavior, batching tradeoffs, quantization impact on latency, and end-to-end egress path latency variance.
- Action items: share diagrams, run KV traces, schedule benchmark session, provide pilot pricing.

Topics covered:
- Multi-subnet egress topology options (dedicated NAT vs egress VPC vs transit gateway)
- PrivateLink endpoint placement and cross-account routing
- DNS behavior and resolver placement for low-latency KV cache hits
- Hairpin/NAT reflection scenarios and MTU/path MTU discovery impacts
- Batching window tuning vs latency SLOs and token-cost tradeoffs
- Quantization considerations for model size and memory-bound inference

Transcript body:
[00:00:03] Evan Cole: All right thanks everyone for joining. we'll run about an hour. Quick intros — Evan (AE), Priya (SE), Marcus (SRE) on the Redwood side. 
[00:00:11] Aisha Khan: Hi all, Aisha from Nimbus infra. We have Marco, Samira, Tom and Olive on the call. We sent the subnet map earlier, hope you got it.
[00:00:20] Priya Shah: Yep got the map, thanks. Objective: understand egress patterns and how those interact with KV cache locality and batching so you don't see weird latency spikes.
[00:00:28] Marco Ruiz: Main concern: our inference pods live in multiple AZ subnets across two VPCs for isolation. We want requests to go out to Redwood Private deployment without adding 20-100ms jitter.
[00:00:38] Marcus Lin: And we'll talk about PRIVAteLink placement — if you put endpoints in each subnet vs central egress it changes the hairpinning and MTU.
[00:00:46] Samira Patel: Also need to confirm audit/logging and KMS flow, SOC2 controls. Can't have secrets leaving our managed KMS.
[00:00:53] Evan Cole: Totally. We'll cover KMS integration notes and what the Private control plane needs versus your on-prem HSM. 
[00:01:02] [00:01:15] Tom Baker: Quick question: is split-homing supported, meaning some subnets route to PrivateLink and others go through a NAT gateway? We need to route sensitive workloads via a dedicated path.
[00:01:25] Priya Shah: Yes split-homing is supported. We recommend pinning security-sensitive traffic to a PrivateLink + dedicated private deployment subnet that is behind customer-managed egress. The tricky bit is DNS and source-dest-checks — you have to ensure resolver answers PrivateLink A records and not public ones.
[00:01:40] Olive Ng: Do you have a recommended DNS pattern? We're using Route53 inbound endpoints + forwarding rules.
[00:01:46] Priya Shah: Route53 resolver inbound endpoints are fine. Typical pattern: local resolver in each AZ with conditional forwarder for redwood.internal (example) to the PrivateLink resolver IP. That keeps the KV cache key locality tight — fewer cross-AZ DNS lookups.
[00:02:04] Aisha Khan: We're worried about hairpinning: if a pod in Subnet A calls PrivateLink in Subnet A but routing sends it through a central NAT in Subnet B, do we get hairpin/reflect issues?
[00:02:14] Marcus Lin: Hairpin NAT can cause increased path length and double MTU fragmentation. For large prompt payloads we've seen 1-2% extra latency tail — that's the scenario to watch. If you avoid hairpin by placing PrivateLink endpoints closer to caller subnets, you reduce tail latency.
[00:02:30] Marco Ruiz: How about KV cache? If we have prefix caching on the Redwood runtime, do we get better hit rates if traffic stays within AZ?
[00:02:38] Priya Shah: Yes. KV cache locality matters. The cache is sharded by model shard and by runtime node; cross-AZ calls will often miss local cache and need remote fetch or duplicate compute. So keeping affinity — same AZ or same subnet — improves hits and reduces fanout.
[00:02:55] Evan Cole: We'll show a quick lab sketch in a minute. Also a note on batching: longer batching windows increase throughput but worsen p50/p95. We'll demo the trade-offs for your 50ms SLO.
[00:03:08] [00:03:22] Priya Shah: Ok I'm sharing the multi-subnet diagram (link in chat: https://drive.google.com/diagram-nimbus-egress) — feel free to annotate.
[00:03:31] Olive Ng: Got it. The diagram shows two VPCs peered, Transit gateway, and PrivateLink endpoints in two AZs.
[00:03:36] Tom Baker: What about MTU mismatches across VPN tunnels? In our on-prem stretch we've seen PMTU discovery timeouts cause 200-300ms stalls for large streaming responses.
[00:03:46] Marcus Lin: Good callout. PMTU path discovery failures do show up as sporadic large token-latency. Our runtime supports smaller transport MTU settings, and on the platform side we recommend enabling path MTU discovery and consistent MTU across your edge NAT and PrivateLink endpoints.
[00:03:59] Samira Patel: From security side: will logs include request-id and token-level cost? We need to reconcile for audits.
[00:04:06] Evan Cole: Yes, Private deployments include audit logs with per-request IDs, model version, cost estimates and optional payload redaction. We can integrate those logs into your Splunk or SIEM via our Fluentd sidecar.
[00:04:18] [00:04:30] Marco Ruiz: Can you run us through a sample latency bench? We have a 4K token worst-case prompt and a 512 token typical.
[00:04:38] Priya Shah: For 4k worst-case we advise using a quantized model variant if latency matters. Quantization reduces memory bandwidth but can increase compute, sometimes raising p50 but decreasing cost. We should test quantization 4-bit vs 8-bit on your workload.
[00:04:53] Olive Ng: Any gotchas with quantization and KV cache?
[00:04:58] Priya Shah: KV cache is architecture-level; quantization affects model compute, not the KV cache keys. But memory footprint changes can affect how many cache shreds fit per GPU, indirectly affecting cache hit rates across co-located models.
[00:05:14] Aisha Khan: We're also interested in batching windows. Marco, what's your current p95 requirement?
[00:05:18] Marco Ruiz: p95 should be under 220ms for interactive features. We're ok with p99 up to 500ms for heavy jobs but prefer lower.
[00:05:25] Evan Cole: That sets constraints. Lower batching windows (e.g., 5-10ms) give better p95 but cost more tokens. For your traffic shape we recommend adaptive batching with dynamic cutoff: short window on small requests, a slightly longer one during bursts.
[00:05:40] [00:05:55] Priya Shah: Now demo-lab: we'll simulate three topologies: 1) PrivateLink endpoints per AZ and local egress, 2) central NAT gateway egress with PrivateLink in one subnet, 3) split-homing where sensitive subnets use dedicated PrivateLink and others use shared egress. We'll compare KV cache hit rate, mean latency, and tail.
[00:06:15] Evan Cole: For transparency we'll capture tcpdump-level traces. Please remember to mask PII and we only need metadata and timing, not payloads.
[00:06:24] Tom Baker: Will you need IAM changes for PrivateLink cross-account?
[00:06:28] Priya Shah: Yes you must accept a VPC endpoint service and add the AWS principal for the Redwood service. We'll provide a CloudFormation snippet you can run to automate the endpoint creation and route tables.
[00:06:40] Marcus Lin: Also watch security groups — ensure the endpoint SG allows traffic from your subnets; no source NAT needed when calling PrivateLink.
[00:06:47] [00:06:59] Marco Ruiz: In scenario 2, if the central NAT adds 8-15ms each way, is that per-request or per-token?
[00:07:04] Priya Shah: Mostly per-request and per-chunk — each network roundtrip adds overhead. For streaming responses network cost per chunk can accumulate. So central NAT often hurts streaming use-cases more than single-request non-stream use.
[00:07:17] Olive Ng: Any network tuning tips? We run EKS with ENIs and CNI iptables.
[00:07:22] Marcus Lin: We suggest ENI trunking or AWS vpc-cni enhanced mode, larger ENI MTU (9001) where possible, and set kube-proxy to ipvs mode for stable routing. Also avoid hairpin NAT on high-throughput pods.
[00:07:36] Samira Patel: For compliance we'll need SAML-based SSO for the console; is that supported in Private?
[00:07:42] Evan Cole: Yes SAML/SSO, RBAC and audit retention options are configurable in Redwood Private control plane. We'll include the SAML metadata exchange doc.
[00:07:52] [00:08:05] Priya Shah: Ok demo step 1: local PrivateLink per AZ. Synthetic: 1000 requests across 3 AZs, 50% small prompts, 50% large. Observed p50 = 60ms, p95 = 170ms, KV local hit rate 78%.
[00:08:23] Marco Ruiz: That's promising. What's the cost delta vs central NAT?
[00:08:26] Priya Shah: Slightly higher networking costs for endpoint per AZ, but compute cost lower because of cache locality — net savings on token-cost after about 200k requests/day in our model. We'll share the spreadsheet.
[00:08:36] Evan Cole: Link to benchmark sheet in chat: https://sheets.redwood/egress-kv-benchmarks-nimbus (mock)
[00:08:42] [00:08:55] Priya Shah: Step 2: central NAT, PrivateLink single AZ, same workload. Observed p50 = 82ms, p95 = 320ms, KV hit rate 41% because of cross-AZ misses. Streaming tail worse.
[00:09:09] Aisha Khan: That p95 is concerning. We use streaming for chat.
[00:09:12] Evan Cole: That's why split-homing is valuable — route chat traffic through local PrivateLink and batch-heavy offline jobs through central NAT.
[00:09:21] Olive Ng: For split-homing how to route per-app? iptable rules?
[00:09:26] Marcus Lin: Use route tables per subnet or application-level egress via ENI attachments. You can also use service mesh egress policies to steer traffic.
[00:09:35] Samira Patel: Any differences for on-prem connectors? We plan an on-prem DR site.
[00:09:40] Priya Shah: On-prem adds WAN variability; consider a local edge proxy that terminates TLS and forwards to PrivateLink over a dedicated transit. That keeps TLS termination in your control and reduces path variance.
[00:09:52] [00:10:05] Marco Ruiz: Quick question re: cache warmup across scaleouts. If the runtime autoscaling adds a new node, is there cache rehydration costs?
[00:10:12] Priya Shah: New nodes start with empty KV caches; rehydration is lazy — first requests repopulate cache. To reduce cold-start penalty we do prewarm hooks and can seed caches from snapshot if you have a stable working set. We'll propose a prewarm plan in the pilot.
[00:10:26] Evan Cole: Also model pinning matters: if you force multiple model variants across nodes, cache fragmentation increases. Try to limit variant fanout for production traffic.
[00:10:36] [00:10:50] Aisha Khan: What telemetry should we send back for debugging?
[00:10:54] Marcus Lin: We recommend collecting: request-id, client-subnet, path taken (PrivateLink vs NAT), KV-cache-hit boolean, batch-size, token-count, model-variant-id, and timestamps for request-start, kv-check, model-infer-start, model-infer-end, response-send.
[00:11:11] Marco Ruiz: We'll map that to our observability — Splunk fields.
[00:11:14] Priya Shah: Perfect. We'll share a JSON schema for the telemetry.
[00:11:18] [00:11:30] Demo intermediate: show cumulative distribution graphs (Priya describes):
- local endpoints: tight CDF; central NAT: long tail; split: hybrid where critical flows match local behavior.
[00:11:47] Tom Baker: About PrivateLink and TLS: do you terminate TLS at Redwood or is it end-to-end?
[00:11:53] Evan Cole: Private deployments allow end-to-end TLS where you keep keys. For hosted-managed PrivateLink we can accept mutual TLS if you prefer mutual auth; we can also broker TLS if you provide certs. For compliance many customers bring their own keys via KMS/HSM.
[00:12:08] Samira Patel: Need an HSM integration diagram and contact for your security SME.
[00:12:13] Evan Cole: We'll include that in the security pack.
[00:12:17] [00:12:30] Olive Ng: Back to batching: how do you tune batching to avoid request-level starvation when you have mix of 10 token and 2k token requests?
[00:12:38] Priya Shah: Use size-aware batching and priority lanes. Small requests go to a low-latency lane with max batch size small and a short timeout; long requests go to through-put lane. Also prefix caching reduces work on long requests if repeated prefixes exist.
[00:12:55] Marco Ruiz: Prefix caching — does it store KV per prompt prefix?
[00:12:59] Priya Shah: It stores decoder KV cache for repeated prefixes, yes. It's keyed by model, prompt prefix hash, and position. Good hit rates if you have many similar prompts (e.g., system prompts).
[00:13:12] [00:13:25] Aisha Khan: We'll be sending you three representative traces: chat, summarization, and batch embed job. Expect them by Friday.
[00:13:31] Evan Cole: Great. We'll run them through our lab and give you per-topology recommendations.
[00:13:36] [00:13:50] Priya Shah: A note on circuit breakers and DNS: if your resolver fails and falls back to public DNS, traffic could egress over the internet. Use conditional forwarding and circuit-breaker rules so resolvers return NODATA instead of public A records when PrivateLink is required.
[00:14:07] Olive Ng: Good point, we've seen that once.
[00:14:12] Marcus Lin: Also add egress-policy enforcement at the VPC level — deny public 443 except for approved egress endpoints.
[00:14:20] [00:14:34] Marco Ruiz: Any specific packet capture markers you want us to include?
[00:14:38] Priya Shah: Capture SYN/ACK timings and TLS handshake RTTs, and mark which local endpoint was hit; we correlate that to batch timestamps.
[00:14:48] Tom Baker: Let's talk about failure modes: if an endpoint fails, does Redwood auto-failover to hosted or other region?
[00:14:57] Evan Cole: For Private deployments you control fallback policies. Redwood supports automatic fallback to a configured secondary endpoint or model variant; we recommend a canary policy that prefers local endpoints and falls back to remote only when capacity is exhausted. We'll help you set the policy.
[00:15:12] [00:15:25] Priya Shah: Now I'll run a short synthetic where we simulate cache warmup and a node replacement. Observed transient p95 spike for ~12s during rehydration, then stable.
[00:15:38] Marco Ruiz: Can we avoid the spike?
[00:15:41] Priya Shah: Prewarm snapshots reduce it to near-zero. Also staggered scale events help.
[00:15:47] Aisha Khan: What about cost transparency during pilot? We need token-level and net-network cost breakdown.
[00:15:53] Evan Cole: Redwood Console exposes token-level cost, per-route network egress, and per-model compute. For Private you can configure cost tags aligned with your internal billing. We'll share a sample report.
[00:16:08] [00:16:20] Samira Patel: Audit question: are payloads persisted in logs? We need payload redaction by default.
[00:16:26] Evan Cole: Default is metadata-only logs; payload retention is opt-in and can be disabled. For compliance we recommend never storing full payloads and using deterministic hashing if needed.
[00:16:37] Olive Ng: Let's cover the pilot scope.
[00:16:41] Evan Cole: Pilot: two AZs, PrivateLink endpoints in each AZ, split-homing for chat traffic, run 48h benchmark with your three traces, enable telemetry, KMS integration test, and SAML SSO for console.
[00:16:56] Marco Ruiz: Sounds good. Any perf SLOs to lock in?
[00:17:00] Marcus Lin: We'll propose p50 < 80ms, p95 < 220ms for interactive flow as a target for pilot.
[00:17:06] Priya Shah: We'll also include a config for KV cache size and batch window defaults we used in the lab.
[00:17:12] [00:17:25] Aisha Khan: Anything else we should prepare?
[00:17:27] Evan Cole: Please share the subnet exports, routing tables, and the three trace files. Also confirm if you want HSM integration in pilot or later.
[00:17:36] Aisha Khan: We'll confirm HSM later; for pilot we can use managed KMS.
[00:17:40] [00:17:55] Priya Shah: Quick Q&A rapid fire:
[00:17:58] Marco Ruiz: Q: If we enable per-AZ endpoints, is there an impact on autoscaling costs?
[00:18:03] Priya Shah: Minimal — endpoints are cheap compared to GPU cost. The bigger cost is duplicate cache footprint across nodes.
[00:18:09] Tom Baker: Q: Any recommened encryption-in-transit settings?
[00:18:13] Evan Cole: Use mTLS for control plane; TLS1.3 for data plane. We support client certs if needed.
[00:18:20] Samira Patel: Q: Does Redwood support VPC flow logs ingestion?
[00:18:23] Marcus Lin: Yes we can forward flow logs and correlate them with request ids.
[00:18:29] [00:18:40] Olive Ng: Q: How do you handle regional failover?
[00:18:44] Evan Cole: Route-based failover by policy — prefer local region, fallback to secondary region with capacity checks. We can orchestrate graceful fallback with stateful KV replication if needed.
[00:18:56] [00:19:10] Priya Shah: Ok wrapping up the technical recap:
- Recommended pilot topology: PrivateLink per AZ + split-homing for interactive chat
- Batch tuning: adaptive batching lanes, short timeouts for low-latency flows
- KV cache: ensure AZ affinity, prewarm snapshots for scale events
- DNS: conditional forwarding to resolver endpoints, guardrails to prevent public fallback
- Security: KMS/HSM options, SAML SSO, audit log integration

[00:19:40] Aisha Khan: Recap sounds right. For next steps we'll get the exports and traces over.
[00:19:47] Evan Cole: Great. We'll send the CFN snippet, bench spreadsheet, security pack and a proposed pilot plan.
[00:19:54] [00:20:05] Final notes (rambling and crosstalk):
[00:20:10] Marco Ruiz: one more tiny thing, how about egress tags to chargeback?
[00:20:14] Evan Cole: We emit cost tags and can map to your internal cost centers.
[00:20:18] Priya Shah: I'll follow up with a prioritized list of config changes for your infra team.
[00:20:22] Aisha Khan: Thanks everyone's been super helpful.
[00:20:25] Evan Cole: Thanks, we'll follow up with the artifacts.

(Transcript truncated for brevity; full recording kept in Fireflies and linked assets)

References & links mentioned in-chat:
- Multi-subnet diagram: https://drive.google.com/diagram-nimbus-egress
- Benchmark sheet (sample): https://sheets.redwood/egress-kv-benchmarks-nimbus
- CloudFormation snippet (private link): https://redwood-internal/cfn/nimbus-private-endpoint-template
- Security pack placeholder: https://redwood-internal/security/private-deploy-pack.pdf

End of transcript.

[00:00:03] Evan Cole: All right thanks everyone for joining. we'll run about an hour. Quick intros — Evan (AE), Priya (SE), Marcus (SRE) on the Redwood side. 
[00:00:11] Aisha Khan: Hi all, Aisha from Nimbus infra. We have Marco, Samira, Tom and Olive on the call. We sent the subnet map earlier, hope you got it.
