[00:00:28] Marco Ruiz: Main concern: our inference pods live in multiple AZ subnets across two VPCs for isolation. We want requests to go out to Redwood Private deployment without adding 20-100ms jitter.
[00:00:38] Marcus Lin: And we'll talk about PRIVAteLink placement — if you put endpoints in each subnet vs central egress it changes the hairpinning and MTU.
[00:00:46] Samira Patel: Also need to confirm audit/logging and KMS flow, SOC2 controls. Can't have secrets leaving our managed KMS.
[00:00:53] Evan Cole: Totally. We'll cover KMS integration notes and what the Private control plane needs versus your on-prem HSM. 
[00:01:02] [00:01:15] Tom Baker: Quick question: is split-homing supported, meaning some subnets route to PrivateLink and others go through a NAT gateway? We need to route sensitive workloads via a dedicated path.
[00:01:25] Priya Shah: Yes split-homing is supported. We recommend pinning security-sensitive traffic to a PrivateLink + dedicated private deployment subnet that is behind customer-managed egress. The tricky bit is DNS and source-dest-checks — you have to ensure resolver answers PrivateLink A records and not public ones.
[00:01:40] Olive Ng: Do you have a recommended DNS pattern? We're using Route53 inbound endpoints + forwarding rules.
[00:01:46] Priya Shah: Route53 resolver inbound endpoints are fine. Typical pattern: local resolver in each AZ with conditional forwarder for redwood.internal (example) to the PrivateLink resolver IP. That keeps the KV cache key locality tight — fewer cross-AZ DNS lookups.
[00:02:04] Aisha Khan: We're worried about hairpinning: if a pod in Subnet A calls PrivateLink in Subnet A but routing sends it through a central NAT in Subnet B, do we get hairpin/reflect issues?
[00:02:14] Marcus Lin: Hairpin NAT can cause increased path length and double MTU fragmentation. For large prompt payloads we've seen 1-2% extra latency tail — that's the scenario to watch. If you avoid hairpin by placing PrivateLink endpoints closer to caller subnets, you reduce tail latency.
[00:02:30] Marco Ruiz: How about KV cache? If we have prefix caching on the Redwood runtime, do we get better hit rates if traffic stays within AZ?
[00:02:38] Priya Shah: Yes. KV cache locality matters. The cache is sharded by model shard and by runtime node; cross-AZ calls will often miss local cache and need remote fetch or duplicate compute. So keeping affinity — same AZ or same subnet — improves hits and reduces fanout.
[00:02:55] Evan Cole: We'll show a quick lab sketch in a minute. Also a note on batching: longer batching windows increase throughput but worsen p50/p95. We'll demo the trade-offs for your 50ms SLO.
[00:03:08] [00:03:22] Priya Shah: Ok I'm sharing the multi-subnet diagram (link in chat: https://drive.google.com/diagram-nimbus-egress) — feel free to annotate.
[00:03:31] Olive Ng: Got it. The diagram shows two VPCs peered, Transit gateway, and PrivateLink endpoints in two AZs.
[00:03:36] Tom Baker: What about MTU mismatches across VPN tunnels? In our on-prem stretch we've seen PMTU discovery timeouts cause 200-300ms stalls for large streaming responses.
[00:03:46] Marcus Lin: Good callout. PMTU path discovery failures do show up as sporadic large token-latency. Our runtime supports smaller transport MTU settings, and on the platform side we recommend enabling path MTU discovery and consistent MTU across your edge NAT and PrivateLink endpoints.
[00:03:59] Samira Patel: From security side: will logs include request-id and token-level cost? We need to reconcile for audits.
[00:04:06] Evan Cole: Yes, Private deployments include audit logs with per-request IDs, model version, cost estimates and optional payload redaction. We can integrate those logs into your Splunk or SIEM via our Fluentd sidecar.
[00:04:18] [00:04:30] Marco Ruiz: Can you run us through a sample latency bench? We have a 4K token worst-case prompt and a 512 token typical.
[00:04:38] Priya Shah: For 4k worst-case we advise using a quantized model variant if latency matters. Quantization reduces memory bandwidth but can increase compute, sometimes raising p50 but decreasing cost. We should test quantization 4-bit vs 8-bit on your workload.
[00:04:53] Olive Ng: Any gotchas with quantization and KV cache?
[00:04:58] Priya Shah: KV cache is architecture-level; quantization affects model compute, not the KV cache keys. But memory footprint changes can affect how many cache shreds fit per GPU, indirectly affecting cache hit rates across co-located models.
[00:05:14] Aisha Khan: We're also interested in batching windows. Marco, what's your current p95 requirement?
[00:05:18] Marco Ruiz: p95 should be under 220ms for interactive features. We're ok with p99 up to 500ms for heavy jobs but prefer lower.
[00:05:25] Evan Cole: That sets constraints. Lower batching windows (e.g., 5-10ms) give better p95 but cost more tokens. For your traffic shape we recommend adaptive batching with dynamic cutoff: short window on small requests, a slightly longer one during bursts.
[00:05:40] [00:05:55] Priya Shah: Now demo-lab: we'll simulate three topologies: 1) PrivateLink endpoints per AZ and local egress, 2) central NAT gateway egress with PrivateLink in one subnet, 3) split-homing where sensitive subnets use dedicated PrivateLink and others use shared egress. We'll compare KV cache hit rate, mean latency, and tail.
[00:06:15] Evan Cole: For transparency we'll capture tcpdump-level traces. Please remember to mask PII and we only need metadata and timing, not payloads.
[00:06:24] Tom Baker: Will you need IAM changes for PrivateLink cross-account?
[00:06:28] Priya Shah: Yes you must accept a VPC endpoint service and add the AWS principal for the Redwood service. We'll provide a CloudFormation snippet you can run to automate the endpoint creation and route tables.
[00:06:40] Marcus Lin: Also watch security groups — ensure the endpoint SG allows traffic from your subnets; no source NAT needed when calling PrivateLink.
[00:06:47] [00:06:59] Marco Ruiz: In scenario 2, if the central NAT adds 8-15ms each way, is that per-request or per-token?
[00:07:04] Priya Shah: Mostly per-request and per-chunk — each network roundtrip adds overhead. For streaming responses network cost per chunk can accumulate. So central NAT often hurts streaming use-cases more than single-request non-stream use.
[00:07:17] Olive Ng: Any network tuning tips? We run EKS with ENIs and CNI iptables.
[00:07:22] Marcus Lin: We suggest ENI trunking or AWS vpc-cni enhanced mode, larger ENI MTU (9001) where possible, and set kube-proxy to ipvs mode for stable routing. Also avoid hairpin NAT on high-throughput pods.
[00:07:36] Samira Patel: For compliance we'll need SAML-based SSO for the console; is that supported in Private?
[00:07:42] Evan Cole: Yes SAML/SSO, RBAC and audit retention options are configurable in Redwood Private control plane. We'll include the SAML metadata exchange doc.
[00:07:52] [00:08:05] Priya Shah: Ok demo step 1: local PrivateLink per AZ. Synthetic: 1000 requests across 3 AZs, 50% small prompts, 50% large. Observed p50 = 60ms, p95 = 170ms, KV local hit rate 78%.
[00:08:23] Marco Ruiz: That's promising. What's the cost delta vs central NAT?
[00:08:26] Priya Shah: Slightly higher networking costs for endpoint per AZ, but compute cost lower because of cache locality — net savings on token-cost after about 200k requests/day in our model. We'll share the spreadsheet.
[00:08:36] Evan Cole: Link to benchmark sheet in chat: https://sheets.redwood/egress-kv-benchmarks-nimbus (mock)
[00:08:42] [00:08:55] Priya Shah: Step 2: central NAT, PrivateLink single AZ, same workload. Observed p50 = 82ms, p95 = 320ms, KV hit rate 41% because of cross-AZ misses. Streaming tail worse.
[00:09:09] Aisha Khan: That p95 is concerning. We use streaming for chat.
[00:09:12] Evan Cole: That's why split-homing is valuable — route chat traffic through local PrivateLink and batch-heavy offline jobs through central NAT.
[00:09:21] Olive Ng: For split-homing how to route per-app? iptable rules?
[00:09:26] Marcus Lin: Use route tables per subnet or application-level egress via ENI attachments. You can also use service mesh egress policies to steer traffic.
[00:09:35] Samira Patel: Any differences for on-prem connectors? We plan an on-prem DR site.
[00:09:40] Priya Shah: On-prem adds WAN variability; consider a local edge proxy that terminates TLS and forwards to PrivateLink over a dedicated transit. That keeps TLS termination in your control and reduces path variance.
[00:09:52] [00:10:05] Marco Ruiz: Quick question re: cache warmup across scaleouts. If the runtime autoscaling adds a new node, is there cache rehydration costs?
[00:10:12] Priya Shah: New nodes start with empty KV caches; rehydration is lazy — first requests repopulate cache. To reduce cold-start penalty we do prewarm hooks and can seed caches from snapshot if you have a stable working set. We'll propose a prewarm plan in the pilot.
[00:10:26] Evan Cole: Also model pinning matters: if you force multiple model variants across nodes, cache fragmentation increases. Try to limit variant fanout for production traffic.
[00:10:36] [00:10:50] Aisha Khan: What telemetry should we send back for debugging?
[00:10:54] Marcus Lin: We recommend collecting: request-id, client-subnet, path taken (PrivateLink vs NAT), KV-cache-hit boolean, batch-size, token-count, model-variant-id, and timestamps for request-start, kv-check, model-infer-start, model-infer-end, response-send.
[00:11:11] Marco Ruiz: We'll map that to our observability — Splunk fields.
[00:11:14] Priya Shah: Perfect. We'll share a JSON schema for the telemetry.
[00:11:18] [00:11:30] Demo intermediate: show cumulative distribution graphs (Priya describes):
- local endpoints: tight CDF; central NAT: long tail; split: hybrid where critical flows match local behavior.
[00:11:47] Tom Baker: About PrivateLink and TLS: do you terminate TLS at Redwood or is it end-to-end?
[00:11:53] Evan Cole: Private deployments allow end-to-end TLS where you keep keys. For hosted-managed PrivateLink we can accept mutual TLS if you prefer mutual auth; we can also broker TLS if you provide certs. For compliance many customers bring their own keys via KMS/HSM.
[00:12:08] Samira Patel: Need an HSM integration diagram and contact for your security SME.
[00:12:13] Evan Cole: We'll include that in the security pack.
[00:12:17] [00:12:30] Olive Ng: Back to batching: how do you tune batching to avoid request-level starvation when you have mix of 10 token and 2k token requests?
[00:12:38] Priya Shah: Use size-aware batching and priority lanes. Small requests go to a low-latency lane with max batch size small and a short timeout; long requests go to through-put lane. Also prefix caching reduces work on long requests if repeated prefixes exist.
[00:12:55] Marco Ruiz: Prefix caching — does it store KV per prompt prefix?
[00:12:59] Priya Shah: It stores decoder KV cache for repeated prefixes, yes. It's keyed by model, prompt prefix hash, and position. Good hit rates if you have many similar prompts (e.g., system prompts).
[00:13:12] [00:13:25] Aisha Khan: We'll be sending you three representative traces: chat, summarization, and batch embed job. Expect them by Friday.
[00:13:31] Evan Cole: Great. We'll run them through our lab and give you per-topology recommendations.
[00:13:36] [00:13:50] Priya Shah: A note on circuit breakers and DNS: if your resolver fails and falls back to public DNS, traffic could egress over the internet. Use conditional forwarding and circuit-breaker rules so resolvers return NODATA instead of public A records when PrivateLink is required.
[00:14:07] Olive Ng: Good point, we've seen that once.
[00:14:12] Marcus Lin: Also add egress-policy enforcement at the VPC level — deny public 443 except for approved egress endpoints.
[00:14:20] [00:14:34] Marco Ruiz: Any specific packet capture markers you want us to include?
[00:14:38] Priya Shah: Capture SYN/ACK timings and TLS handshake RTTs, and mark which local endpoint was hit; we correlate that to batch timestamps.
[00:14:48] Tom Baker: Let's talk about failure modes: if an endpoint fails, does Redwood auto-failover to hosted or other region?
[00:14:57] Evan Cole: For Private deployments you control fallback policies. Redwood supports automatic fallback to a configured secondary endpoint or model variant; we recommend a canary policy that prefers local endpoints and falls back to remote only when capacity is exhausted. We'll help you set the policy.
[00:15:12] [00:15:25] Priya Shah: Now I'll run a short synthetic where we simulate cache warmup and a node replacement. Observed transient p95 spike for ~12s during rehydration, then stable.
[00:15:38] Marco Ruiz: Can we avoid the spike?
[00:15:41] Priya Shah: Prewarm snapshots reduce it to near-zero. Also staggered scale events help.
[00:15:47] Aisha Khan: What about cost transparency during pilot? We need token-level and net-network cost breakdown.
[00:15:53] Evan Cole: Redwood Console exposes token-level cost, per-route network egress, and per-model compute. For Private you can configure cost tags aligned with your internal billing. We'll share a sample report.
[00:16:08] [00:16:20] Samira Patel: Audit question: are payloads persisted in logs? We need payload redaction by default.
[00:16:26] Evan Cole: Default is metadata-only logs; payload retention is opt-in and can be disabled. For compliance we recommend never storing full payloads and using deterministic hashing if needed.
[00:16:37] Olive Ng: Let's cover the pilot scope.
[00:16:41] Evan Cole: Pilot: two AZs, PrivateLink endpoints in each AZ, split-homing for chat traffic, run 48h benchmark with your three traces, enable telemetry, KMS integration test, and SAML SSO for console.
[00:16:56] Marco Ruiz: Sounds good. Any perf SLOs to lock in?
[00:17:00] Marcus Lin: We'll propose p50 < 80ms, p95 < 220ms for interactive flow as a target for pilot.
[00:17:06] Priya Shah: We'll also include a config for KV cache size and batch window defaults we used in the lab.
[00:17:12] [00:17:25] Aisha Khan: Anything else we should prepare?
[00:17:27] Evan Cole: Please share the subnet exports, routing tables, and the three trace files. Also confirm if you want HSM integration in pilot or later.
[00:17:36] Aisha Khan: We'll confirm HSM later; for pilot we can use managed KMS.
[00:17:40] [00:17:55] Priya Shah: Quick Q&A rapid fire:
[00:17:58] Marco Ruiz: Q: If we enable per-AZ endpoints, is there an impact on autoscaling costs?
[00:18:03] Priya Shah: Minimal — endpoints are cheap compared to GPU cost. The bigger cost is duplicate cache footprint across nodes.
[00:18:09] Tom Baker: Q: Any recommened encryption-in-transit settings?
[00:18:13] Evan Cole: Use mTLS for control plane; TLS1.3 for data plane. We support client certs if needed.
[00:18:20] Samira Patel: Q: Does Redwood support VPC flow logs ingestion?
[00:18:23] Marcus Lin: Yes we can forward flow logs and correlate them with request ids.
[00:18:29] [00:18:40] Olive Ng: Q: How do you handle regional failover?
[00:18:44] Evan Cole: Route-based failover by policy — prefer local region, fallback to secondary region with capacity checks. We can orchestrate graceful fallback with stateful KV replication if needed.
[00:18:56] [00:19:10] Priya Shah: Ok wrapping up the technical recap:
- Recommended pilot topology: PrivateLink per AZ + split-homing for interactive chat
- Batch tuning: adaptive batching lanes, short timeouts for low-latency flows
- KV cache: ensure AZ affinity, prewarm snapshots for scale events
- DNS: conditional forwarding to resolver endpoints, guardrails to prevent public fallback
- Security: KMS/HSM options, SAML SSO, audit log integration

[00:19:40] Aisha Khan: Recap sounds right. For next steps we'll get the exports and traces over.
[00:19:47] Evan Cole: Great. We'll send the CFN snippet, bench spreadsheet, security pack and a proposed pilot plan.
[00:19:54] [00:20:05] Final notes (rambling and crosstalk):
[00:20:10] Marco Ruiz: one more tiny thing, how about egress tags to chargeback?
[00:20:14] Evan Cole: We emit cost tags and can map to your internal cost centers.
[00:20:18] Priya Shah: I'll follow up with a prioritized list of config changes for your infra team.
[00:20:22] Aisha Khan: Thanks everyone's been super helpful.
[00:20:25] Evan Cole: Thanks, we'll follow up with the artifacts.

(Transcript truncated for brevity; full recording kept in Fireflies and linked assets)

References & links mentioned in-chat:
- Multi-subnet diagram: https://drive.google.com/diagram-nimbus-egress
- Benchmark sheet (sample): https://sheets.redwood/egress-kv-benchmarks-nimbus
- CloudFormation snippet (private link): https://redwood-internal/cfn/nimbus-private-endpoint-template
- Security pack placeholder: https://redwood-internal/security/private-deploy-pack.pdf

End of transcript.

title: Acorn Financial - Security review: auth handling in Redwood SDKs (env vars, redaction, auditability)
preview:
Meeting: Acorn Financial - Security review: auth handling in Redwood SDKs (env vars, redaction, auditability)
Recorded: 2026-02-12 10:03 AM PT
Duration: 53 minutes

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Monica Patel, Marcus Lin, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Ethan Park
- Acorn Financial: Priya Desai, Tom Randall, Elena Kovacs, Jake Morrison

Summary (auto)
Acorn asked how Redwood’s Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs resolve credentials (explicit parameter vs environment variables), how secrets are redacted in logs/errors, and how requests can be audited (request IDs, headers, and logging hooks). Redwood walked through current behavior and the cross-language parity work underway, including standardized env var names, safe error messages, and request_id propagation. Acorn requested written confirmation that SDKs do not leak secrets in debug logs/stack traces and asked for recommended deployment patterns using secret managers.

Topics (auto)
- Env var usage + precedence order
- Header format and token types
- Secret redaction in logs and exceptions
- Auditability: request_id, correlation IDs, and log fields
- Key rotation and operational guidance

Action items (auto)
- Redwood: Send SDK auth handling guidance (env vars, precedence, redaction) (Monica / Marcus)
- Redwood: Provide sample code snippets for safe logging hooks with request_id (Kaitlyn)
- Acorn: Confirm preferred auth pattern in Kubernetes (env vars vs mounted files vs secret manager injection) (Tom / Priya)


[00:00] Monica Patel: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for making time. This is the security review you asked for on SDK auth handling, so env vars, secret redaction, and audit stuff like request IDs.

[00:12] Priya Desai: Yep. We’re evaluating Redwood for a couple internal apps. We’ve been burned by SDKs printing tokens in exceptions, so we want to be very explicit.

[00:21] Marcus Lin: Totally fair. I’m Marcus, security at Redwood. I’ll focus on secret handling, safe error messages, and what we do and don’t log.

[00:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: And I’m Kaitlyn, I work on the auth integration side with the gateway and SDKs.

[00:33] Tom Randall: Tom here, platform. We run everything in Kubernetes, secrets via Vault, injected as env vars typically. But we can also mount files if you prefer.

[00:41] Elena Kovacs: Elena, compliance. I’ll mostly ask auditability questions and what evidence you can provide.

[00:46] Jake Morrison: Jake, SRE. I care about correlation IDs, retry storms, but today it’s mostly auth and logging.

[00:54] Monica Patel: Great. Maybe let’s start with the simple question: how do the SDKs pick up credentials.

[01:02] Priya Desai: Yes. Specifically: do you support environment variables, what are they called, and what happens if both env var and code param are set.

[01:13] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yep. So across our SDKs we support passing credentials explicitly when you create the client, and we also support reading from env vars.

[01:21] Kaitlyn Nguyen: The precedence order we’re standardizing is: explicit parameter wins, then environment variable, then error if nothing is provided. We do not attempt any “magic” beyond that like reading from random config files.

[01:34] Tom Randall: When you say explicit parameter, you mean like `api_key=` in Python, same thing in Go/TS?

[01:41] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Exactly. Python `Redwood(api_key=...)`, TypeScript constructor option like `{ apiKey: ... }`, Go option like `WithAPIKey(...)`.

[01:51] Priya Desai: What are the env vars.

[01:54] Kaitlyn Nguyen: The current standardized names are `REDWOOD_API_KEY` for API key auth. For bearer tokens, we support `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN`.

[02:04] Priya Desai: You support both API keys and bearer tokens?

[02:06] Ethan Park: Yeah. Same header format, but we treat the token type differently depending on which credential you provide. Most customers just use API keys.

[02:13] Priya Desai: Header is `Authorization: Bearer <token>` I assume.

[02:17] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Correct. For API keys, it is still the Bearer header; the server recognizes it as a Redwood API key format.

[02:24] Tom Randall: Any older env var names? We’ve seen SDKs use like `REDWOOD_KEY` or something.

[02:31] Monica Patel: Good question. Historically we had some drift across languages, which is part of why we’re doing the parity initiative. Some versions accepted older names; those are being deprecated with warnings, not silently ignored. We’ll provide the exact mapping in writing.

[02:48] Priya Desai: We need to know if old names still work because we’d like to standardize our secret injection templates.

[02:53] Monica Patel: Understood.

[02:56] Elena Kovacs: From an audit standpoint, we need evidence that there’s a consistent, documented way the SDK resolves credentials.

[03:05] Marcus Lin: We have a security-reviewed guidance doc on auth resolution and secret handling. We can share that plus release notes once the parity versions are GA.

[03:15] Priya Desai: Okay. Next: do you ever log the key by default.

[03:20] Marcus Lin: No. By default the SDKs do not log request headers, and they do not print the API key in errors. If you enable debug logging, we still redact the Authorization header.

[03:32] Priya Desai: Redact how? Like completely removed, or last 4.

[03:36] Marcus Lin: We treat secrets as fully sensitive. In any structured logs, it’s either omitted or replaced with `[REDACTED]`. In a couple places we may show a fingerprint like last 4 characters for supportability, but only if it’s explicitly enabled and it is never the full token.

[03:54] Jake Morrison: Is that consistent across Python and TypeScript? Because Node libraries sometimes dump request objects.

[04:02] Monica Patel: That’s one of the parity requirements. The changes we’re shipping specifically prohibit dumping headers. Also we standardized safe error messages so stack traces don’t include the token.

[04:14] Priya Desai: Can you confirm exceptions won’t embed the request object.

[04:18] Marcus Lin: Yes. Errors include safe fields like status code, request_id, error code, and a human-readable message. They do not include raw headers. If a customer wraps the HTTP client and logs the request themselves, that’s outside the SDK, but our default and our error types do not embed secrets.

[04:39] Tom Randall: What about proxies? Some HTTP libs put the URL with credentials in it. Do you support credentials in URL.

[04:46] Kaitlyn Nguyen: We do not support credentials in the URL. It’s Authorization header only.

[04:51] Priya Desai: Great.

[04:53] Elena Kovacs: Auditability. If an API key is used, can we see which key in your logs? And can we correlate a customer request to an access event.

[05:05] Ethan Park: On our side, we associate usage to your account and key, and we can surface usage analytics. For raw “audit logs”, it depends on deployment mode. In hosted, we can provide request IDs and usage reports. In private deployments, we can provide audit logs integrated into the control plane.

[05:23] Elena Kovacs: But specifically for our SIEM: we need a request identifier we can log on our side, and the same ID on your side.

[05:31] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes. Every response includes a `request_id` (and header `x-request-id`). The SDK also exposes that request_id on errors. You can log it per request. Support will ask for it.

[05:43] Jake Morrison: On streaming too? Because sometimes stream errors don’t have a response body.

[05:49] Ethan Park: We’ve been improving that. The gateway and SDKs now propagate request_id consistently for streaming and non-streaming paths. If the connection dies mid-stream, you’ll often still have the request_id from the initial response headers.

[06:05] Jake Morrison: “Often” makes me nervous.

[06:08] Monica Patel: Totally. In the new SDK versions, the streaming iterator captures the initial headers and stores the request_id. If the stream disconnects later, the raised exception includes the request_id and a disconnect reason when available.

[06:25] Priya Desai: Is that exception message safe? No tokens.

[06:28] Monica Patel: Correct.

[06:30] Priya Desai: Ok. Back to env vars. If we run in Kubernetes and a pod has `REDWOOD_API_KEY` set, any library in that process can read it. That’s fine, but we like “explicit only” sometimes. Do you allow disabling env var lookup.

[06:47] Monica Patel: Today the default is to allow env var lookup if you don’t pass an explicit key. We have discussed adding a “requireExplicitAuth” option, but I don’t want to promise it’s in the current stable. We can take that as a request.

[07:02] Marcus Lin: From a security perspective, we generally recommend explicit injection via a secret manager into env vars, but if you want to enforce “explicit only” in production, we can document a pattern: provide a dummy env var and require the client initializer to pass the token. But a first-class knob is cleaner.

[07:19] Tom Randall: We would probably set it explicitly anyway through our bootstrapping code, but we want to avoid accidental “picks up some old env var” behavior.

[07:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s why precedence is explicit param first. If your code always provides it, env var doesn’t matter.

[07:35] Priya Desai: Unless a developer forgets and it silently uses env var.

[07:38] Monica Patel: Right. We can add guidance: treat missing explicit auth as a configuration error in your app, and have CI checks. But we’ll also look at adding the stricter mode.

[07:53] Elena Kovacs: Key rotation. How fast can we rotate keys without downtime.

[07:59] Ethan Park: Keys can be rotated in the console. The SDK just sends whatever token you provide. Operationally, you’d deploy the new key, then revoke the old. For long-running processes, you’d restart or reload config.

[08:13] Jake Morrison: Any support for multiple keys at once? Like fallback.

[08:17] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Not at the SDK level today. Some customers implement their own wrapper that tries a second key on 401, but we generally don’t recommend retrying auth failures automatically.

[08:28] Priya Desai: Yeah, we wouldn’t want automatic retries on auth.

[08:33] Priya Desai: Another thing: do you ever include the env var name in errors. Like “missing REDWOOD_API_KEY”. That’s okay. But not the value.

[08:43] Marcus Lin: We include the variable name in guidance messages, yes. Not the value.

[08:48] Priya Desai: Good.

[08:50] Tom Randall: TypeScript question: do you support browser usage? We don’t want devs shipping keys to the client.

[08:57] Monica Patel: We strongly discourage running the SDK with secret keys in browsers. The TS SDK will work in Node environments; browser bundling can expose secrets. We’ll add explicit docs that it’s server-side only unless you’re using a safe proxy.

[09:13] Priya Desai: Can you make it fail hard in browser.

[09:16] Monica Patel: We can’t reliably detect all bundlers, but we can document and potentially add a runtime warning when `window` is present.

[09:26] Jake Morrison: Logging hooks: do you provide a way to capture request_id and latency without capturing headers.

[09:34] Monica Patel: Yes. Each SDK has some form of hook or middleware. We’re standardizing the logging fields: request_id, status_code, retry_count, timeout_ms, and for streaming disconnect reason.

[09:49] Jake Morrison: That’s good. We can plumb those into our logs.

[09:52] Elena Kovacs: For audit: do you support customer-provided correlation IDs.

[09:57] Kaitlyn Nguyen: You can send `x-correlation-id` as a header; we’ll echo it back where possible. The canonical ID remains our request_id.

[10:07] Priya Desai: Is that header allowed in all SDKs.

[10:10] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes, there is a way to pass custom headers. We’ll confirm the exact option names across languages; part of parity work is consistent naming.

[10:20] Marcus Lin: One caveat: we redact Authorization header always. Custom headers are not automatically redacted unless you mark them as sensitive in your own logs. But our SDKs also don’t log them by default.

[10:35] Priya Desai: We have a standard list: Authorization, Cookie, X-Api-Key type fields. We can handle.

[10:42] Monica Patel: Great.

[10:44] Elena Kovacs: Do you have SOC 2 and do you have policies around secrets.

[10:49] Marcus Lin: Yes we are SOC 2 Type II. We have secure SDLC policies, and specifically for SDKs we have guidelines: no secrets in logs, no secrets in exception messages, and tests to ensure redaction.

[11:04] Priya Desai: Tests? Like automated tests.

[11:06] Monica Patel: Yes. As part of the conformance suite, we check “golden” error shapes and ensure that sensitive values aren’t present.

[11:16] Priya Desai: That’s good.

[11:18] Tom Randall: On env vars: do you read from .env files.

[11:22] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Not directly. If your application uses dotenv and sets env vars, then sure, but we don’t parse .env ourselves.

[11:32] Tom Randall: Good. Less magic.

[11:34] Jake Morrison: When errors happen, do you include request_id for 401 and 403 too?

[11:39] Ethan Park: Yes, request_id should be on all responses. There was some inconsistency historically, but we’ve fixed the gateway behavior so it’s always present.

[11:52] Jake Morrison: Great.

[11:54] Priya Desai: Another concern: do you ever send the key to third parties, like telemetry.

[12:00] Monica Patel: No. The SDK doesn’t send your key anywhere besides the Redwood API endpoint you configure. We don’t have client-side analytics that exfiltrate secrets.

[12:09] Marcus Lin: And if you enable any optional telemetry hooks, they are designed to never include Authorization or prompt data. But again, that’s opt-in.

[12:19] Priya Desai: Please put that in writing.

[12:22] Monica Patel: Yep.

[12:24] Elena Kovacs: Data retention is separate, but from audit perspective: can we get a list of API keys and when used.

[12:33] Ethan Park: In the console you can see keys and usage aggregated. For per-request audit logs, hosted has limits; private deployment provides more direct audit log access. We can discuss requirements.

[12:48] Elena Kovacs: Okay, we’re likely hosted initially.

[12:50] Monica Patel: Makes sense.

[12:52] Priya Desai: Can you walk through an example of an error. Like invalid key. What does the SDK show.

[13:01] Monica Patel: Sure. You’d get a typed error like `AuthenticationError` with fields: `status_code=401`, `request_id=...`, `code=...` maybe `invalid_api_key`, and message like “Authentication failed. Check REDWOOD_API_KEY or client configuration.” No token.

[13:21] Priya Desai: Ok.

[13:23] Jake Morrison: Can you include the host and path? That’s useful for debugging.

[13:27] Monica Patel: Host and path, yes. But still no headers.

[13:32] Priya Desai: That’s fine.

[13:34] Tom Randall: How do you handle multiple auth sources if both API key and bearer token env vars are set.

[13:41] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Good catch. The rule is: if explicit auth is provided, that wins. If not, we check for bearer token first only if configured to do so; but the simplest is: prefer API key if `REDWOOD_API_KEY` is set, otherwise bearer token. We’re finalizing that exact precedence to avoid surprises.

[14:02] Priya Desai: We want deterministic, documented.

[14:05] Monica Patel: Agreed. Our standard doc will specify it.

[14:08] Marcus Lin: And we generally recommend not setting both simultaneously.

[14:12] Priya Desai: Right.

[14:14] Elena Kovacs: Are there any scenarios where a secret could appear in a stack trace indirectly, like through HTTP client exceptions.

[14:22] Monica Patel: In some languages, the underlying HTTP library can include the request object in certain exceptions if you log it. We wrap those and produce our own typed errors; we do not propagate the raw request dump.

[14:38] Marcus Lin: We also have guardrails: if an exception message contains something that matches token patterns, we scrub it. Not perfect, but it’s an additional layer.

[14:50] Priya Desai: That’s interesting. How do you match.

[14:53] Marcus Lin: Pattern-based. Like typical key prefixes, length, and entropy-ish checks. Again, the main defense is: don’t include headers in the first place.

[15:05] Jake Morrison: That’s reasonable.

[15:07] Tom Randall: On our side, we may run with `HTTP_PROXY` set. Any risk the proxy sees the header obviously.

[15:15] Ethan Park: If you route through a corporate proxy, yes the proxy will see outbound traffic including Authorization. That’s your network path. If that’s a concern, we can discuss Private/VPC options later, but that’s separate.

[15:29] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:31] Monica Patel: I want to make sure we hit your checklist. Priya, anything else on env vars.

[15:37] Priya Desai: Two more. One: do you ever cache the token anywhere, like write to disk.

[15:43] Kaitlyn Nguyen: No. We keep it in memory as part of the client configuration.

[15:47] Priya Desai: Two: do you support reading from a file path env var. Like `REDWOOD_API_KEY_FILE`.

[15:54] Monica Patel: Not today. Some customers wrap that themselves, reading the file and passing the string into the client.

[16:02] Tom Randall: That’s fine.

[16:05] Elena Kovacs: We’ll need a written statement about secret handling in SDK logging. Also evidence of secure development practice.

[16:14] Marcus Lin: We can provide our SOC 2 report under NDA and the specific SDK secret handling guidance.

[16:20] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[16:22] Jake Morrison: Another audit point: request_id — is it returned in a response header for all endpoints including errors.

[16:29] Ethan Park: Yes. `x-request-id` header. And the body `request_id` field for JSON errors.

[16:37] Jake Morrison: For streaming, is it in headers only.

[16:40] Ethan Park: Typically yes. The SDK will capture it.

[16:44] Jake Morrison: Okay.

[16:45] Priya Desai: Can you provide sample logging snippet across languages. We want our devs to log request_id on failures.

[16:53] Monica Patel: Yes, we can send snippets for Python, TS, Go.

[17:00] Priya Desai: Also, clarify that they should never log the client config.

[17:03] Monica Patel: Yep.

[17:05] Tom Randall: One more: We use OpenAI and Anthropic. Their SDKs sometimes have weird env var names. We want one consistent pattern. Your `REDWOOD_API_KEY` is fine.

[17:17] Monica Patel: Great.

[17:19] Priya Desai: But please make sure there aren’t like three different env vars in different SDKs.

[17:23] Monica Patel: Understood. That’s literally the parity initiative.

[17:28] Marcus Lin: And from security, we’re enforcing the same redaction behavior across all three.

[17:34] Elena Kovacs: This call is being recorded by your tool, right.

[17:37] Monica Patel: Yes, Fireflies.

[17:39] Elena Kovacs: Ok.

[17:41] Jake Morrison: Can we talk about what you log server-side. Do you log Authorization.

[17:47] Ethan Park: We do not log Authorization headers in application logs. At the edge, some infrastructure can see it transiently for request processing, but it’s not stored in standard logs.

[17:59] Marcus Lin: That’s correct. We have controls to avoid storing secrets.

[18:03] Priya Desai: And support won’t ask for it.

[18:06] Monica Patel: Correct. Support asks for request_id, SDK version, and timestamps.

[18:12] Jake Morrison: That’s good.

[18:14] Tom Randall: In the SDK, if debug is enabled, does it print curl commands. Some libs do.

[18:21] Monica Patel: No curl command printing by default. If you implement your own interceptor and print curl, that’s on you.

[18:30] Priya Desai: Okay.

[18:32] Elena Kovacs: Next steps: we’ll need the doc, plus confirmation on env var precedence especially when multiple are set.

[18:42] Monica Patel: Yep. We’ll send a follow-up email with: the auth resolution order, env var names, redaction guarantees, and sample safe logging snippets.

[18:55] Priya Desai: Can you also include guidance on using Vault injection. Like “set env var at runtime and restart on rotation”.

[19:03] Monica Patel: Sure.

[19:05] Jake Morrison: And from SRE, we’d like to see request_id included in every error object, not only thrown exceptions.

[19:12] Monica Patel: It will be.

[19:14] Ethan Park: Also, if you send `x-correlation-id`, log it on your side; we’ll echo where possible.

[19:22] Tom Randall: Ok.

[19:24] Priya Desai: One more clarifier: do you ever accept auth in query params.

[19:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: No.

[19:30] Priya Desai: Great.

[19:32] Monica Patel: Okay, if there are no more questions, we can end a bit early. Anything else from Acorn.

[19:39] Elena Kovacs: Nothing else.

[19:41] Jake Morrison: I’m good.

[19:42] Tom Randall: Yep.

[19:44] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[19:46] Monica Patel: Perfect. We’ll send the docs and propose a follow-up for request_id and log correlation plumbing. Thanks everyone.

Call ended (auto)

Notes
- Several moments of light crosstalk; speaker attribution may be imperfect.
- Customer requested written confirmation of: env var names/precedence, no secret logging, and request_id propagation for auditing.
text:
Meeting: Acorn Financial - Security review: auth handling in Redwood SDKs (env vars, redaction, auditability)
Recorded: 2026-02-12 10:03 AM PT
Duration: 53 minutes

Attendees
- Redwood Inference: Monica Patel, Marcus Lin, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Ethan Park
- Acorn Financial: Priya Desai, Tom Randall, Elena Kovacs, Jake Morrison

Summary (auto)
Acorn asked how Redwood’s Python/TypeScript/Go SDKs resolve credentials (explicit parameter vs environment variables), how secrets are redacted in logs/errors, and how requests can be audited (request IDs, headers, and logging hooks). Redwood walked through current behavior and the cross-language parity work underway, including standardized env var names, safe error messages, and request_id propagation. Acorn requested written confirmation that SDKs do not leak secrets in debug logs/stack traces and asked for recommended deployment patterns using secret managers.

Topics (auto)
- Env var usage + precedence order
- Header format and token types
- Secret redaction in logs and exceptions
- Auditability: request_id, correlation IDs, and log fields
- Key rotation and operational guidance

Action items (auto)
- Redwood: Send SDK auth handling guidance (env vars, precedence, redaction) (Monica / Marcus)
- Redwood: Provide sample code snippets for safe logging hooks with request_id (Kaitlyn)
- Acorn: Confirm preferred auth pattern in Kubernetes (env vars vs mounted files vs secret manager injection) (Tom / Priya)


[00:00] Monica Patel: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for making time. This is the security review you asked for on SDK auth handling, so env vars, secret redaction, and audit stuff like request IDs.

[00:12] Priya Desai: Yep. We’re evaluating Redwood for a couple internal apps. We’ve been burned by SDKs printing tokens in exceptions, so we want to be very explicit.

[00:21] Marcus Lin: Totally fair. I’m Marcus, security at Redwood. I’ll focus on secret handling, safe error messages, and what we do and don’t log.

[00:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: And I’m Kaitlyn, I work on the auth integration side with the gateway and SDKs.

[00:33] Tom Randall: Tom here, platform. We run everything in Kubernetes, secrets via Vault, injected as env vars typically. But we can also mount files if you prefer.

[00:41] Elena Kovacs: Elena, compliance. I’ll mostly ask auditability questions and what evidence you can provide.

[00:46] Jake Morrison: Jake, SRE. I care about correlation IDs, retry storms, but today it’s mostly auth and logging.

[00:54] Monica Patel: Great. Maybe let’s start with the simple question: how do the SDKs pick up credentials.

[01:02] Priya Desai: Yes. Specifically: do you support environment variables, what are they called, and what happens if both env var and code param are set.

[01:13] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yep. So across our SDKs we support passing credentials explicitly when you create the client, and we also support reading from env vars.

[01:21] Kaitlyn Nguyen: The precedence order we’re standardizing is: explicit parameter wins, then environment variable, then error if nothing is provided. We do not attempt any “magic” beyond that like reading from random config files.

[01:34] Tom Randall: When you say explicit parameter, you mean like `api_key=` in Python, same thing in Go/TS?

[01:41] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Exactly. Python `Redwood(api_key=...)`, TypeScript constructor option like `{ apiKey: ... }`, Go option like `WithAPIKey(...)`.

[01:51] Priya Desai: What are the env vars.

[01:54] Kaitlyn Nguyen: The current standardized names are `REDWOOD_API_KEY` for API key auth. For bearer tokens, we support `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN`.

[02:04] Priya Desai: You support both API keys and bearer tokens?

[02:06] Ethan Park: Yeah. Same header format, but we treat the token type differently depending on which credential you provide. Most customers just use API keys.

[02:13] Priya Desai: Header is `Authorization: Bearer <token>` I assume.

[02:17] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Correct. For API keys, it is still the Bearer header; the server recognizes it as a Redwood API key format.

[02:24] Tom Randall: Any older env var names? We’ve seen SDKs use like `REDWOOD_KEY` or something.

[02:31] Monica Patel: Good question. Historically we had some drift across languages, which is part of why we’re doing the parity initiative. Some versions accepted older names; those are being deprecated with warnings, not silently ignored. We’ll provide the exact mapping in writing.

[02:48] Priya Desai: We need to know if old names still work because we’d like to standardize our secret injection templates.

[02:53] Monica Patel: Understood.

[02:56] Elena Kovacs: From an audit standpoint, we need evidence that there’s a consistent, documented way the SDK resolves credentials.

[03:05] Marcus Lin: We have a security-reviewed guidance doc on auth resolution and secret handling. We can share that plus release notes once the parity versions are GA.

[03:15] Priya Desai: Okay. Next: do you ever log the key by default.

[03:20] Marcus Lin: No. By default the SDKs do not log request headers, and they do not print the API key in errors. If you enable debug logging, we still redact the Authorization header.

[03:32] Priya Desai: Redact how? Like completely removed, or last 4.

[03:36] Marcus Lin: We treat secrets as fully sensitive. In any structured logs, it’s either omitted or replaced with `[REDACTED]`. In a couple places we may show a fingerprint like last 4 characters for supportability, but only if it’s explicitly enabled and it is never the full token.

[03:54] Jake Morrison: Is that consistent across Python and TypeScript? Because Node libraries sometimes dump request objects.

[04:02] Monica Patel: That’s one of the parity requirements. The changes we’re shipping specifically prohibit dumping headers. Also we standardized safe error messages so stack traces don’t include the token.

[04:14] Priya Desai: Can you confirm exceptions won’t embed the request object.

[04:18] Marcus Lin: Yes. Errors include safe fields like status code, request_id, error code, and a human-readable message. They do not include raw headers. If a customer wraps the HTTP client and logs the request themselves, that’s outside the SDK, but our default and our error types do not embed secrets.

[04:39] Tom Randall: What about proxies? Some HTTP libs put the URL with credentials in it. Do you support credentials in URL.

[04:46] Kaitlyn Nguyen: We do not support credentials in the URL. It’s Authorization header only.

[04:51] Priya Desai: Great.

[04:53] Elena Kovacs: Auditability. If an API key is used, can we see which key in your logs? And can we correlate a customer request to an access event.

[05:05] Ethan Park: On our side, we associate usage to your account and key, and we can surface usage analytics. For raw “audit logs”, it depends on deployment mode. In hosted, we can provide request IDs and usage reports. In private deployments, we can provide audit logs integrated into the control plane.

[05:23] Elena Kovacs: But specifically for our SIEM: we need a request identifier we can log on our side, and the same ID on your side.

[05:31] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes. Every response includes a `request_id` (and header `x-request-id`). The SDK also exposes that request_id on errors. You can log it per request. Support will ask for it.

[05:43] Jake Morrison: On streaming too? Because sometimes stream errors don’t have a response body.

[05:49] Ethan Park: We’ve been improving that. The gateway and SDKs now propagate request_id consistently for streaming and non-streaming paths. If the connection dies mid-stream, you’ll often still have the request_id from the initial response headers.

[06:05] Jake Morrison: “Often” makes me nervous.

[06:08] Monica Patel: Totally. In the new SDK versions, the streaming iterator captures the initial headers and stores the request_id. If the stream disconnects later, the raised exception includes the request_id and a disconnect reason when available.

[06:25] Priya Desai: Is that exception message safe? No tokens.

[06:28] Monica Patel: Correct.

[06:30] Priya Desai: Ok. Back to env vars. If we run in Kubernetes and a pod has `REDWOOD_API_KEY` set, any library in that process can read it. That’s fine, but we like “explicit only” sometimes. Do you allow disabling env var lookup.

[06:47] Monica Patel: Today the default is to allow env var lookup if you don’t pass an explicit key. We have discussed adding a “requireExplicitAuth” option, but I don’t want to promise it’s in the current stable. We can take that as a request.

[07:02] Marcus Lin: From a security perspective, we generally recommend explicit injection via a secret manager into env vars, but if you want to enforce “explicit only” in production, we can document a pattern: provide a dummy env var and require the client initializer to pass the token. But a first-class knob is cleaner.

[07:19] Tom Randall: We would probably set it explicitly anyway through our bootstrapping code, but we want to avoid accidental “picks up some old env var” behavior.

[07:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s why precedence is explicit param first. If your code always provides it, env var doesn’t matter.

[07:35] Priya Desai: Unless a developer forgets and it silently uses env var.

[07:38] Monica Patel: Right. We can add guidance: treat missing explicit auth as a configuration error in your app, and have CI checks. But we’ll also look at adding the stricter mode.

[07:53] Elena Kovacs: Key rotation. How fast can we rotate keys without downtime.

[07:59] Ethan Park: Keys can be rotated in the console. The SDK just sends whatever token you provide. Operationally, you’d deploy the new key, then revoke the old. For long-running processes, you’d restart or reload config.

[08:13] Jake Morrison: Any support for multiple keys at once? Like fallback.

[08:17] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Not at the SDK level today. Some customers implement their own wrapper that tries a second key on 401, but we generally don’t recommend retrying auth failures automatically.

[08:28] Priya Desai: Yeah, we wouldn’t want automatic retries on auth.

[08:33] Priya Desai: Another thing: do you ever include the env var name in errors. Like “missing REDWOOD_API_KEY”. That’s okay. But not the value.

[08:43] Marcus Lin: We include the variable name in guidance messages, yes. Not the value.

[08:48] Priya Desai: Good.

[08:50] Tom Randall: TypeScript question: do you support browser usage? We don’t want devs shipping keys to the client.

[08:57] Monica Patel: We strongly discourage running the SDK with secret keys in browsers. The TS SDK will work in Node environments; browser bundling can expose secrets. We’ll add explicit docs that it’s server-side only unless you’re using a safe proxy.

[09:13] Priya Desai: Can you make it fail hard in browser.

[09:16] Monica Patel: We can’t reliably detect all bundlers, but we can document and potentially add a runtime warning when `window` is present.

[09:26] Jake Morrison: Logging hooks: do you provide a way to capture request_id and latency without capturing headers.

[09:34] Monica Patel: Yes. Each SDK has some form of hook or middleware. We’re standardizing the logging fields: request_id, status_code, retry_count, timeout_ms, and for streaming disconnect reason.

[09:49] Jake Morrison: That’s good. We can plumb those into our logs.

[09:52] Elena Kovacs: For audit: do you support customer-provided correlation IDs.

[09:57] Kaitlyn Nguyen: You can send `x-correlation-id` as a header; we’ll echo it back where possible. The canonical ID remains our request_id.

[10:07] Priya Desai: Is that header allowed in all SDKs.

[10:10] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yes, there is a way to pass custom headers. We’ll confirm the exact option names across languages; part of parity work is consistent naming.

[10:20] Marcus Lin: One caveat: we redact Authorization header always. Custom headers are not automatically redacted unless you mark them as sensitive in your own logs. But our SDKs also don’t log them by default.

[10:35] Priya Desai: We have a standard list: Authorization, Cookie, X-Api-Key type fields. We can handle.

[10:42] Monica Patel: Great.

[10:44] Elena Kovacs: Do you have SOC 2 and do you have policies around secrets.

[10:49] Marcus Lin: Yes we are SOC 2 Type II. We have secure SDLC policies, and specifically for SDKs we have guidelines: no secrets in logs, no secrets in exception messages, and tests to ensure redaction.

[11:04] Priya Desai: Tests? Like automated tests.

[11:06] Monica Patel: Yes. As part of the conformance suite, we check “golden” error shapes and ensure that sensitive values aren’t present.

[11:16] Priya Desai: That’s good.

[11:18] Tom Randall: On env vars: do you read from .env files.

[11:22] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Not directly. If your application uses dotenv and sets env vars, then sure, but we don’t parse .env ourselves.

[11:32] Tom Randall: Good. Less magic.

[11:34] Jake Morrison: When errors happen, do you include request_id for 401 and 403 too?

[11:39] Ethan Park: Yes, request_id should be on all responses. There was some inconsistency historically, but we’ve fixed the gateway behavior so it’s always present.

[11:52] Jake Morrison: Great.

[11:54] Priya Desai: Another concern: do you ever send the key to third parties, like telemetry.

[12:00] Monica Patel: No. The SDK doesn’t send your key anywhere besides the Redwood API endpoint you configure. We don’t have client-side analytics that exfiltrate secrets.

[12:09] Marcus Lin: And if you enable any optional telemetry hooks, they are designed to never include Authorization or prompt data. But again, that’s opt-in.

[12:19] Priya Desai: Please put that in writing.

[12:22] Monica Patel: Yep.

[12:24] Elena Kovacs: Data retention is separate, but from audit perspective: can we get a list of API keys and when used.

[12:33] Ethan Park: In the console you can see keys and usage aggregated. For per-request audit logs, hosted has limits; private deployment provides more direct audit log access. We can discuss requirements.

[12:48] Elena Kovacs: Okay, we’re likely hosted initially.

[12:50] Monica Patel: Makes sense.

[12:52] Priya Desai: Can you walk through an example of an error. Like invalid key. What does the SDK show.

[13:01] Monica Patel: Sure. You’d get a typed error like `AuthenticationError` with fields: `status_code=401`, `request_id=...`, `code=...` maybe `invalid_api_key`, and message like “Authentication failed. Check REDWOOD_API_KEY or client configuration.” No token.

[13:21] Priya Desai: Ok.

[13:23] Jake Morrison: Can you include the host and path? That’s useful for debugging.

[13:27] Monica Patel: Host and path, yes. But still no headers.

[13:32] Priya Desai: That’s fine.

[13:34] Tom Randall: How do you handle multiple auth sources if both API key and bearer token env vars are set.

[13:41] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Good catch. The rule is: if explicit auth is provided, that wins. If not, we check for bearer token first only if configured to do so; but the simplest is: prefer API key if `REDWOOD_API_KEY` is set, otherwise bearer token. We’re finalizing that exact precedence to avoid surprises.

[14:02] Priya Desai: We want deterministic, documented.

[14:05] Monica Patel: Agreed. Our standard doc will specify it.

[14:08] Marcus Lin: And we generally recommend not setting both simultaneously.

[14:12] Priya Desai: Right.

[14:14] Elena Kovacs: Are there any scenarios where a secret could appear in a stack trace indirectly, like through HTTP client exceptions.

[14:22] Monica Patel: In some languages, the underlying HTTP library can include the request object in certain exceptions if you log it. We wrap those and produce our own typed errors; we do not propagate the raw request dump.

[14:38] Marcus Lin: We also have guardrails: if an exception message contains something that matches token patterns, we scrub it. Not perfect, but it’s an additional layer.

[14:50] Priya Desai: That’s interesting. How do you match.

[14:53] Marcus Lin: Pattern-based. Like typical key prefixes, length, and entropy-ish checks. Again, the main defense is: don’t include headers in the first place.

[15:05] Jake Morrison: That’s reasonable.

[15:07] Tom Randall: On our side, we may run with `HTTP_PROXY` set. Any risk the proxy sees the header obviously.

[15:15] Ethan Park: If you route through a corporate proxy, yes the proxy will see outbound traffic including Authorization. That’s your network path. If that’s a concern, we can discuss Private/VPC options later, but that’s separate.

[15:29] Priya Desai: Yep.

[15:31] Monica Patel: I want to make sure we hit your checklist. Priya, anything else on env vars.

[15:37] Priya Desai: Two more. One: do you ever cache the token anywhere, like write to disk.

[15:43] Kaitlyn Nguyen: No. We keep it in memory as part of the client configuration.

[15:47] Priya Desai: Two: do you support reading from a file path env var. Like `REDWOOD_API_KEY_FILE`.

[15:54] Monica Patel: Not today. Some customers wrap that themselves, reading the file and passing the string into the client.

[16:02] Tom Randall: That’s fine.

[16:05] Elena Kovacs: We’ll need a written statement about secret handling in SDK logging. Also evidence of secure development practice.

[16:14] Marcus Lin: We can provide our SOC 2 report under NDA and the specific SDK secret handling guidance.

[16:20] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[16:22] Jake Morrison: Another audit point: request_id — is it returned in a response header for all endpoints including errors.

[16:29] Ethan Park: Yes. `x-request-id` header. And the body `request_id` field for JSON errors.

[16:37] Jake Morrison: For streaming, is it in headers only.

[16:40] Ethan Park: Typically yes. The SDK will capture it.

[16:44] Jake Morrison: Okay.

[16:45] Priya Desai: Can you provide sample logging snippet across languages. We want our devs to log request_id on failures.

[16:53] Monica Patel: Yes, we can send snippets for Python, TS, Go.

[17:00] Priya Desai: Also, clarify that they should never log the client config.

[17:03] Monica Patel: Yep.

[17:05] Tom Randall: One more: We use OpenAI and Anthropic. Their SDKs sometimes have weird env var names. We want one consistent pattern. Your `REDWOOD_API_KEY` is fine.

[17:17] Monica Patel: Great.

[17:19] Priya Desai: But please make sure there aren’t like three different env vars in different SDKs.

[17:23] Monica Patel: Understood. That’s literally the parity initiative.

[17:28] Marcus Lin: And from security, we’re enforcing the same redaction behavior across all three.

[17:34] Elena Kovacs: This call is being recorded by your tool, right.

[17:37] Monica Patel: Yes, Fireflies.

[17:39] Elena Kovacs: Ok.

[17:41] Jake Morrison: Can we talk about what you log server-side. Do you log Authorization.

[17:47] Ethan Park: We do not log Authorization headers in application logs. At the edge, some infrastructure can see it transiently for request processing, but it’s not stored in standard logs.

[17:59] Marcus Lin: That’s correct. We have controls to avoid storing secrets.

[18:03] Priya Desai: And support won’t ask for it.

[18:06] Monica Patel: Correct. Support asks for request_id, SDK version, and timestamps.

[18:12] Jake Morrison: That’s good.

[18:14] Tom Randall: In the SDK, if debug is enabled, does it print curl commands. Some libs do.

[18:21] Monica Patel: No curl command printing by default. If you implement your own interceptor and print curl, that’s on you.

[18:30] Priya Desai: Okay.

[18:32] Elena Kovacs: Next steps: we’ll need the doc, plus confirmation on env var precedence especially when multiple are set.

[18:42] Monica Patel: Yep. We’ll send a follow-up email with: the auth resolution order, env var names, redaction guarantees, and sample safe logging snippets.

[18:55] Priya Desai: Can you also include guidance on using Vault injection. Like “set env var at runtime and restart on rotation”.

[19:03] Monica Patel: Sure.

[19:05] Jake Morrison: And from SRE, we’d like to see request_id included in every error object, not only thrown exceptions.

[19:12] Monica Patel: It will be.

[19:14] Ethan Park: Also, if you send `x-correlation-id`, log it on your side; we’ll echo where possible.

[19:22] Tom Randall: Ok.

[19:24] Priya Desai: One more clarifier: do you ever accept auth in query params.

[19:28] Kaitlyn Nguyen: No.

[19:30] Priya Desai: Great.

[19:32] Monica Patel: Okay, if there are no more questions, we can end a bit early. Anything else from Acorn.

[19:39] Elena Kovacs: Nothing else.

[19:41] Jake Morrison: I’m good.

[19:42] Tom Randall: Yep.

[19:44] Priya Desai: Thanks.

[19:46] Monica Patel: Perfect. We’ll send the docs and propose a follow-up for request_id and log correlation plumbing. Thanks everyone.

Call ended (auto)

Notes
- Several moments of light crosstalk; speaker attribution may be imperfect.
- Customer requested written confirmation of: env var names/precedence, no secret logging, and request_id propagation for auditing.

title: Northstar x Redwood - Security review: Optimize quality gates + audit trail
preview: Northstar requested a security/controls deep dive focused on Redwood Optimize making cost-saving config changes (batching/caching/quantization) and how Redwood prevents silent quality regressions. Redwood walked through the “quality gate” enforcement model: proposed config diffs trigger static validation + eval-harness runs (smoke gate then full gate) against a baseline; only passing results can be applied. Overrides are time-bound, require an authorized approver, and emit audit log events. Rollout controller blocks canary promotion when gates fail and can auto-rollback if online quality signals degrade. Northstar asked about audit log contents, export to SIEM, RBAC, and whether customer prompts are stored; Redwood described artifact pointers, configurable retention, and enterprise log export options. Next steps: Redwood to share audit event schema/examples, SOC2 package, and a short written description of the override policy and roles; Northstar to confirm internal approver group and desired log retention/export path.
