title: Helio Health Systems - Security review: audit logging + RBAC + change management (Optimization configs)
preview:
Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-05
Time: 10:00 AM PT
Duration: 57 minutes
Call type: Security / compliance review

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Colin O'Donnell, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Paula Mendes
Helio Health Systems: Dana Kim, Priya Shah, Luis Martinez, Evan Ross

Summary (auto-generated)
- Helio reviewed requirements for audit logging, RBAC, retention, and operational change controls for Redwood Hosted/Dedicated and Private.
- Redwood walked through planned/available safe apply + rollback mechanisms for Optimization Config changes (batching/caching/quantization profiles), including versioning and gating.
- Open questions centered on: audit event field completeness, retention periods, SIEM export options, and approval workflows for high-risk changes.

Topics
- Audit log coverage and event taxonomy (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback)
- RBAC and SSO/SAML mapping for “optimization change operator” roles
- Retention and export to SIEM (Hosted vs Private)
- Change management controls: staged rollout, canaries, gating signals, emergency rollback

Action Items (auto-generated)
- Redwood to provide example audit log payloads and confirm retention options.
- Helio to confirm SIEM integration and required fields.

---

[00:00] Colin O'Donnell: Alright, we’re at the top. Thanks everyone for making time. This is the security review you all requested, focused on audit logging and change management, especially around the optimization tuning stuff.

[00:11] Dana Kim: Yep. Thanks Colin. We have a questionnaire that basically, it’s like… 40% audit logging and 40% change control and then the rest is like encryption and SDLC. We’ll stick to the logging and change control today.

[00:25] Priya Shah: And retention. Retention is a big one for us.

[00:29] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Marcus and Kevin are here from product security and security engineering. Paula is the PM driving the optimization config versioning + rollback project.

[00:41] Paula Mendes: Hi everyone.

[00:43] Kevin Osei: Hey.

[00:44] Marcus Lin: Hello.

[00:47] Luis Martinez: Hey all.

[00:49] Evan Ross: Hi, Evan here, just listening mostly.

[00:54] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. Dana, do you want to start with the must-haves?

[01:00] Dana Kim: Sure. So for any production changes—config changes included—we need audit records with: who did it, what changed, when, where it applied, and ideally before/after or at least a diff. And we need immutable audit logs, retention for minimum one year, preferably seven for certain systems, but one is the baseline.

[01:20] Dana Kim: Separately we need RBAC. Like a person who can view but not apply, a person who can apply to a canary but not global. And we need to see “break glass” actions.

[01:33] Colin O'Donnell: Got it. That aligns pretty well with what we’ve built and what’s in-flight for optimization config. Paula, do you want to set context on the config changes we’re talking about?

[01:43] Paula Mendes: Yes. So the specific surface area is what we call Optimization Config. It governs batching heuristics, caching toggles, and quantization profiles. Historically those knobs were mutable and that created operational risk—harder to know what changed and to roll it back.

[01:59] Paula Mendes: The project we’re delivering is immutable versioning plus safe apply workflows—validate, staged rollout, gated progression—and one-click rollback. Those lifecycle operations generate audit events.

[02:13] Priya Shah: When you say immutable versioning, can you explain what is immutable? Like the whole object, or just fields?

[02:19] Paula Mendes: The versioned record itself is immutable. So version “v17” of a config is a frozen snapshot with metadata. You can create a new version “v18” with changes, but you can’t edit “v17” in place. Then there’s an “active” pointer by scope—tenant/model/region—indicating which version is currently applied.

[02:43] Luis Martinez: Scope meaning like per model and region? Not just global.

[02:47] Paula Mendes: Exactly. We support scoping to tenant, model, region, and also environment (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) conceptually.

[02:55] Dana Kim: Okay. So audit logs—do we get an event when the pointer changes, and when the version is created, and when it’s validated?

[03:04] Kevin Osei: Yes, that’s the intent. Think of events across the lifecycle: optimization_config.version_created, validation_requested / validation_failed / validation_succeeded, rollout_started, rollout_paused, rollout_promoted, rollback_requested, rollback_completed.

[03:23] Kevin Osei: Names might differ slightly, but the taxonomy includes create/apply/promote/rollback. Each event includes the actor identity, the target scope (tenant/model/region), the config_version IDs, and correlation IDs.

[03:36] Dana Kim: Actor identity meaning SSO subject or email?

[03:40] Marcus Lin: For Console actions, it’s the authenticated user—SSO subject mapped to a user profile, plus email when available. For API actions, it’s the API key principal or service account, plus the key ID.

[03:54] Marcus Lin: We also include the “authn_method” field—SAML SSO vs token—and the “actor_type” (human vs service).

[04:04] Priya Shah: We specifically require source IP, user agent, and session ID when it’s a human.

[04:11] Kevin Osei: Source IP and user agent are available for Console requests; we log request metadata. Session ID—we have a session identifier internally; we can include it as “session_id” if that’s required. I want to confirm with our privacy posture, but for enterprise audit it’s typical.

[04:29] Dana Kim: And diff. Do you store a diff in the audit event?

[04:33] Kevin Osei: We can include either: a pointer to a diff artifact or include a small structured diff. Usually we avoid huge payloads in the audit log stream. What we do today for similar config objects is include “before_version” and “after_version” and a hash, and then the diff can be retrieved via an API with proper permissions.

[04:54] Luis Martinez: So the audit log shows that v18 replaced v17, but if I want the exact field change I call another endpoint.

[05:01] Kevin Osei: Correct. We can also include “changed_fields” as a list of top-level keys, like batching.max_batch_tokens, cache.prefix_cache_enabled, quant.profile_id—stuff like that.

[05:14] Dana Kim: That’s acceptable if the retrieval is also audited.

[05:17] Kevin Osei: Yep, reads can be audited too. We already support audit events for sensitive read operations in some modules; we can extend that to config diff retrieval.

[05:28] Priya Shah: Retention. What’s your default retention and can we configure it?

[05:33] Marcus Lin: For Hosted/Dedicated, audit logs are retained in Redwood’s control plane for a default period—currently 400 days for enterprise tier. We also support export to customer SIEM endpoints so you can retain longer on your side.

[05:49] Priya Shah: 400 days is okay for baseline. We need seven years in our SIEM for certain logs.

[05:55] Marcus Lin: That’s common. We’ll want to confirm the export mechanism you prefer. We can do streaming to an HTTPS webhook and we also have options like pushing to an object store destination. For Private deployments, since you operate the control plane components, the retention is in your cluster/logging stack.

[06:14] Luis Martinez: For Private, does Redwood ship an audit log service? Or do we just rely on Kubernetes logs?

[06:20] Colin O'Donnell: Good question. The Private package includes the same audit event emitter and an audit log sink that can write to your configured backend. Most customers route to their log aggregator—Splunk, Datadog, or OpenSearch. We document a Helm values pattern.

[06:39] Dana Kim: We’re mostly Splunk HEC, and sometimes S3 with Athena. Webhook is fine but it needs retries and dead-letter handling.

[06:49] Kevin Osei: Understood. Our event delivery supports at-least-once semantics. For webhook, we sign payloads, do exponential backoff, and we include an event_id for dedupe. For Private you’d configure the same mechanism or run a collector that ships to Splunk.

[07:09] Priya Shah: At least once is okay. But you need immutability. How do you prevent tampering?

[07:15] Kevin Osei: For Hosted, events are written to an append-only store and then exported. Internally we have integrity controls—write-only service accounts, restricted admin access, and we can provide evidence via SOC 2 report controls. For Private, immutability is generally the customer’s responsibility—WORM buckets or Splunk immutability settings.

[07:39] Dana Kim: Okay. RBAC next. We need granular permissions. Specifically: who can create a version, who can validate, who can apply to canary, who can promote to 100%, who can roll back.

[07:52] Marcus Lin: Our RBAC model supports separate permissions for each lifecycle action. We’ve been introducing an “optimization change operator” role. It can be split into: optimization_config.create, optimization_config.validate, optimization_config.apply, optimization_config.promote, optimization_config.rollback.

[08:11] Marcus Lin: Additionally, there are scope constraints. For example, apply/promote can be limited to a specific tenant or region. The enforcement is server-side.

[08:21] Luis Martinez: Can it be limited to “canary only”? Like you can start a rollout but can’t promote it.

[08:27] Marcus Lin: Yes, by separating apply and promote. Apply can start a rollout at 1% or to a canary shard; promote is the progression to higher percentages or full.

[08:38] Dana Kim: Do you support dual control? Like two-person approval.

[08:43] Marcus Lin: Not universally in-product today. We can integrate with your internal process via change tickets and approvals, but native two-person approval on the button click is on our longer-term roadmap.

[08:56] Priya Shah: That might be okay if we can demonstrate compensating controls: limited role assignment and audit logs.

[09:03] Dana Kim: Also break glass. If on-call does a rollback during a Sev 1, we need that to be clearly marked.

[09:11] Kevin Osei: We can mark events with a “reason” and “incident_id” field. Console rollback requires a confirmation modal; we can include a required text field “reason for rollback” and optionally link a ticket.

[09:27] Paula Mendes: The UX includes a blast radius summary and type-to-confirm for high-scope rollbacks. We can require a reason for certain scopes.

[09:36] Luis Martinez: Nice. What about “automatic rollback”? Do you do that?

[09:41] Paula Mendes: For initial GA, we’re not doing fully automated rollback for all customers. Internally and for selected accounts we can auto-pause or recommend rollback when gates fail. But the actual rollback action is human-confirmed.

[09:59] Dana Kim: Let’s talk about gates. We need assurance that you have guardrails on risky config changes.

[10:05] Paula Mendes: Safe apply uses staged rollout. You can do tenant/model/region scoping. Then within that we can do percent-based canary. Progression is gated on SLO metrics: 5xx error rate, p95 and p99 latency, saturation signals (GPU utilization, queue depth). For quantization profiles there’s a quality gate from the eval harness.

[10:30] Priya Shah: Eval harness meaning… you run a set of prompts and compare?

[10:34] Paula Mendes: Yes. It’s a fixed prompt set or customer-provided prompt set in some cases, and we compare quality metrics. It can be exact match, model-based grading, or task-specific metrics. For security review, the key is: rollout can be blocked if quality regresses beyond threshold.

[10:54] Dana Kim: Are these gates configurable per customer?

[10:58] Paula Mendes: We have defaults and allow overrides within allowed ranges. We don’t allow you to fully disable all safety checks for production scopes, unless you’re in an internal testing environment.

[11:12] Luis Martinez: We’ve had issues with other vendors where an optimization change is “instant global.” So staged is good.

[11:20] Colin O'Donnell: And this ties into the one-click rollback in Console and via API.

[11:26] Dana Kim: Rollback semantics. If I click rollback, is it instant? And what does it mean in a distributed fleet?

[11:33] Paula Mendes: It’s not “instant everywhere at the same millisecond,” but the system is designed to converge quickly. The rollback action flips the active pointer back to a prior known-good version for that scope. Workers poll / subscribe and apply the change idempotently. We have safeguards to avoid a mixed state for long.

[11:57] Paula Mendes: We also checkpoint rollout state. If apply and rollback race, rollback wins and the controller reconciles.

[12:06] Luis Martinez: So it’s eventually consistent but controlled.

[12:10] Paula Mendes: Exactly. And we emit audit events for rollback_requested and rollback_completed, and we record how many workers have applied. In Console you see progress.

[12:23] Dana Kim: Do you expose config_version in request logs? Like can I tie a latency spike to a config version.

[12:29] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, we can surface the active optimization config version in response headers optionally. Also it’s included in internal metrics tags. For customers, in the Console you’ll see version annotations on the dashboard.

[12:45] Luis Martinez: That’s important for debugging.

[12:49] Priya Shah: Now, for audit events, we need certain fields. Let me list them and you can tell me yes/no.

[12:55] Priya Shah: Event timestamp with timezone, event name, actor, actor role, source IP, user agent, action outcome (success/fail), object type, object ID, scope, before/after pointers, and correlation ID.

[13:13] Kevin Osei: Mostly yes. Timestamp is ISO. Event name yes. Actor yes. Actor role—we can include role IDs assigned at time of action. Source IP and user agent yes for Console and some API contexts (API calls have client IP). Outcome yes. Object type/ID yes. Scope yes. Before/after pointers yes. Correlation ID yes.

[13:39] Dana Kim: We also need request ID. Like a per-request unique identifier.

[13:44] Kevin Osei: We have request_id and trace_id internally; we can include both.

[13:49] Luis Martinez: For Private we’d want those too.

[13:52] Kevin Osei: Yes.

[13:54] Dana Kim: Okay. What about encryption and key management for audit logs?

[13:59] Marcus Lin: Hosted audit logs are encrypted at rest. For Dedicated and Private, keys can be managed via KMS integration depending on the deployment mode. For Private, you can use your own KMS/HSM and manage encryption on your storage backend.

[14:18] Priya Shah: Can you do customer-managed keys for Hosted?

[14:22] Marcus Lin: For pure Hosted multi-tenant, we do not support per-customer CMK yet. For Dedicated and Private, yes. This might be more relevant for data logs than audit logs, but still.

[14:35] Dana Kim: Understood.

[14:37] Luis Martinez: I want to go back to export. Webhook is okay but Splunk HEC is the preferred. Do you support HEC directly?

[14:45] Kevin Osei: Not a direct “native HEC client” in Hosted today. Most customers use the webhook receiver as an intermediary, or we can push to an S3 bucket and you ingest. For Private, you can run a collector that formats for HEC.

[15:03] Dana Kim: That’s probably fine. We have an ingestion layer.

[15:06] Priya Shah: We also need to know if Redwood staff can trigger changes in our environment and if that is audited.

[15:13] Colin O'Donnell: For Hosted/Dedicated, Redwood SRE has limited break-glass access, and actions are audited. For Private, Redwood doesn’t have access by default; it’s your cluster.

[15:25] Marcus Lin: To be precise: support access is time-bound, approval-based, and logged. We can provide policy details under NDA.

[15:34] Priya Shah: Okay.

[15:36] Dana Kim: Change management: do you have something like “change tickets” built in?

[15:41] Paula Mendes: Not as a full ITSM system. But the workflow includes required fields in the Console—description, risk level, and optional links to Jira/ServiceNow. Those show up in the config version metadata and in audit logs.

[15:58] Luis Martinez: So if we paste a ServiceNow change ID, it persists.

[16:02] Paula Mendes: Exactly.

[16:04] Dana Kim: We need approvals for prod changes. If you can’t do two-person approval, we’d probably restrict apply permissions to our change manager group.

[16:13] Marcus Lin: That’s a common pattern. We can set RBAC so only that group can promote or rollback. Or make rollback available to on-call but require reason + incident ID.

[16:26] Dana Kim: Wait, you said “rollback available to on-call,” but we need to ensure that is not blocked during an incident.

[16:33] Paula Mendes: We specifically designed rollback to be one-click with confirmation. There’s also an API endpoint. So your incident automation can do it if you choose.

[16:45] Luis Martinez: But still RBAC controlled.

[16:47] Paula Mendes: Yes.

[16:49] Priya Shah: For retention, you said 400 days. Is that configurable up?

[16:54] Marcus Lin: We can offer extended retention as an add-on for some tiers, but the most robust option is exporting to your SIEM and keeping it there. For Private, again, you control it.

[17:10] Priya Shah: Okay. Another requirement: we need to see failed attempts. Like permission denied, or validation failed.

[17:17] Kevin Osei: Absolutely. We emit audit events for both success and failure, including “denied” for RBAC. It includes the permission that was missing.

[17:27] Dana Kim: Good.

[17:29] Luis Martinez: The mis-scope scenario—like someone applies a config intended for one region to another region. Do you have guardrails?

[17:38] Paula Mendes: Yes. The apply UI and API require explicit scope, and the confirmation modal summarizes blast radius: tenant(s), model(s), region(s), and percentage. And we validate semantic constraints like region compatibility if the config references region-specific capabilities.

[17:58] Paula Mendes: Also, the history view shows scope clearly. That’s partly to prevent wrong-region mistakes.

[18:06] Dana Kim: Is there a way to “pin” a version to prevent accidental changes?

[18:10] Paula Mendes: You can pin an active version for a scope, yes. It prevents auto-promotion or other changes without elevated permission.

[18:20] Luis Martinez: That’s useful.

[18:22] Colin O'Donnell: I want to check if we covered the big questionnaire items: audit logs, RBAC, retention, export, change control, rollback.

[18:30] Dana Kim: Almost. One more: log schema stability. If we build parsers, we can’t have fields changing randomly.

[18:37] Kevin Osei: Understood. We version the event schema. We aim for backwards compatible changes only. If a breaking change is needed, it’s a new event version and communicated.

[18:48] Priya Shah: Do you have examples you can share?

[18:50] Kevin Osei: Yes, I’ll send a set of example payloads after the call. They’ll include optimization config create/apply/promote/rollback plus RBAC denial.

[19:01] Dana Kim: Please include the correlation IDs and request IDs.

[19:04] Kevin Osei: Will do.

[19:06] Luis Martinez: Another small thing: time sync. Are timestamps based on server time? Any drift issues?

[19:12] Kevin Osei: Server-side. Our infra uses NTP sync; timestamps are generated centrally. For Private, you’ll rely on your cluster time; we recommend NTP.

[19:24] Dana Kim: Good.

[19:26] Priya Shah: And access to audit logs: do we need separate permissions to view them?

[19:30] Marcus Lin: Yes. There’s an audit_log.read permission. And we can constrain it by tenant. Viewing audit logs is also logged.

[19:41] Dana Kim: Great.

[19:44] Luis Martinez: Can we filter audit events by model or region?

[19:48] Kevin Osei: Yes, because scope is structured. You can query by tenant_id, model, region, config_version.

[19:57] Paula Mendes: And in Console, the history screen can filter by those dimensions.

[20:03] Dana Kim: Okay. Shifting to operational change controls: do you have documented runbooks for rollback.

[20:09] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, there’s an internal and a customer-facing runbook. It includes decision criteria (latency increase, 5xx) and verification steps post-rollback.

[20:19] Dana Kim: We’ll want the customer-facing one.

[20:21] Colin O'Donnell: We can share a draft.

[20:24] Priya Shah: Also, do you do game days? Like testing rollback.

[20:28] Paula Mendes: Internally yes. We have a gameday plan where we simulate a bad config rollout and validate rollback and audit events.

[20:38] Dana Kim: That’s good.

[20:40] Luis Martinez: On Private, is rollback one-click too? Or is it like Helm rollback.

[20:45] Colin O'Donnell: It’s operator-friendly. Typically you can run a command or update a Helm value to pin back to a prior version. The same version model applies.

[20:55] Luis Martinez: Okay. That might be acceptable.

[21:00] Dana Kim: I’m going to ask a slightly adversarial question. If Redwood has a bug and your audit events are missing, how do we know?

[21:09] Kevin Osei: Fair. We have monitoring on the audit pipeline and alerting if event rates drop unexpectedly. Also, the apply/rollback actions themselves are transactional; if the audit emission fails in certain critical paths, we can fail the operation rather than proceed silently.

[21:28] Dana Kim: That’s what we want.

[21:30] Priya Shah: And can we see “audit log pipeline health”?

[21:34] Colin O'Donnell: In Dedicated we can expose status; in Hosted it’s more internal, but we can provide SLA statements and can share operational metrics on request.

[21:45] Luis Martinez: Makes sense.

[21:47] Dana Kim: Alright. I think we have enough. Colin, can you recap next steps.

[21:53] Colin O'Donnell: Yep. Kevin will send example audit log payloads including required fields and event names. Marcus will follow up with RBAC permission mapping and what we can and cannot do for dual-approval. Paula will send a brief statement on safe apply gates and rollback semantics.

[22:12] Colin O'Donnell: And Luis, you’ll confirm your SIEM ingestion preference and any required fields we missed. Also, we’ll schedule a Private deployment deep dive.

[22:23] Luis Martinez: Yep, I’ll send that.

[22:25] Priya Shah: And I’ll forward the questionnaire section.

[22:28] Dana Kim: Thanks.

[22:30] Colin O'Donnell: Great. We’ll end early unless anyone has more.

[22:34] Evan Ross: Nothing from me.

[22:36] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. Thanks everyone.

(Note: Fireflies transcription continued to capture minor crosstalk and silence; meeting ended around 57 minutes with additional off-topic procurement scheduling not transcribed in detail.)
