Meeting Header
Date: 2026-02-05
Time: 10:00 AM PT
Duration: 57 minutes
Call type: Security / compliance review

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Colin O'Donnell, Marcus Lin, Kevin Osei, Paula Mendes
Helio Health Systems: Dana Kim, Priya Shah, Luis Martinez, Evan Ross

Summary (auto-generated)
- Helio reviewed requirements for audit logging, RBAC, retention, and operational change controls for Redwood Hosted/Dedicated and Private.
- Redwood walked through planned/available safe apply + rollback mechanisms for Optimization Config changes (batching/caching/quantization profiles), including versioning and gating.
- Open questions centered on: audit event field completeness, retention periods, SIEM export options, and approval workflows for high-risk changes.

Topics
- Audit log coverage and event taxonomy (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback)
- RBAC and SSO/SAML mapping for “optimization change operator” roles
- Retention and export to SIEM (Hosted vs Private)
- Change management controls: staged rollout, canaries, gating signals, emergency rollback

Action Items (auto-generated)
- Redwood to provide example audit log payloads and confirm retention options.
- Helio to confirm SIEM integration and required fields.

---

[00:00] Colin O'Donnell: Alright, we’re at the top. Thanks everyone for making time. This is the security review you all requested, focused on audit logging and change management, especially around the optimization tuning stuff.

[00:11] Dana Kim: Yep. Thanks Colin. We have a questionnaire that basically, it’s like… 40% audit logging and 40% change control and then the rest is like encryption and SDLC. We’ll stick to the logging and change control today.

[00:25] Priya Shah: And retention. Retention is a big one for us.

[00:29] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Marcus and Kevin are here from product security and security engineering. Paula is the PM driving the optimization config versioning + rollback project.

[00:41] Paula Mendes: Hi everyone.

[00:43] Kevin Osei: Hey.

[00:44] Marcus Lin: Hello.

[00:47] Luis Martinez: Hey all.

[00:49] Evan Ross: Hi, Evan here, just listening mostly.

[00:54] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. Dana, do you want to start with the must-haves?

[01:00] Dana Kim: Sure. So for any production changes—config changes included—we need audit records with: who did it, what changed, when, where it applied, and ideally before/after or at least a diff. And we need immutable audit logs, retention for minimum one year, preferably seven for certain systems, but one is the baseline.

[01:20] Dana Kim: Separately we need RBAC. Like a person who can view but not apply, a person who can apply to a canary but not global. And we need to see “break glass” actions.

[01:33] Colin O'Donnell: Got it. That aligns pretty well with what we’ve built and what’s in-flight for optimization config. Paula, do you want to set context on the config changes we’re talking about?

[01:43] Paula Mendes: Yes. So the specific surface area is what we call Optimization Config. It governs batching heuristics, caching toggles, and quantization profiles. Historically those knobs were mutable and that created operational risk—harder to know what changed and to roll it back.

[01:59] Paula Mendes: The project we’re delivering is immutable versioning plus safe apply workflows—validate, staged rollout, gated progression—and one-click rollback. Those lifecycle operations generate audit events.

[02:13] Priya Shah: When you say immutable versioning, can you explain what is immutable? Like the whole object, or just fields?

[02:19] Paula Mendes: The versioned record itself is immutable. So version “v17” of a config is a frozen snapshot with metadata. You can create a new version “v18” with changes, but you can’t edit “v17” in place. Then there’s an “active” pointer by scope—tenant/model/region—indicating which version is currently applied.

[02:43] Luis Martinez: Scope meaning like per model and region? Not just global.

[02:47] Paula Mendes: Exactly. We support scoping to tenant, model, region, and also environment (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private) conceptually.

[02:55] Dana Kim: Okay. So audit logs—do we get an event when the pointer changes, and when the version is created, and when it’s validated?

[03:04] Kevin Osei: Yes, that’s the intent. Think of events across the lifecycle: optimization_config.version_created, validation_requested / validation_failed / validation_succeeded, rollout_started, rollout_paused, rollout_promoted, rollback_requested, rollback_completed.

[03:23] Kevin Osei: Names might differ slightly, but the taxonomy includes create/apply/promote/rollback. Each event includes the actor identity, the target scope (tenant/model/region), the config_version IDs, and correlation IDs.

[03:36] Dana Kim: Actor identity meaning SSO subject or email?

[03:40] Marcus Lin: For Console actions, it’s the authenticated user—SSO subject mapped to a user profile, plus email when available. For API actions, it’s the API key principal or service account, plus the key ID.

[03:54] Marcus Lin: We also include the “authn_method” field—SAML SSO vs token—and the “actor_type” (human vs service).

[04:04] Priya Shah: We specifically require source IP, user agent, and session ID when it’s a human.

[04:11] Kevin Osei: Source IP and user agent are available for Console requests; we log request metadata. Session ID—we have a session identifier internally; we can include it as “session_id” if that’s required. I want to confirm with our privacy posture, but for enterprise audit it’s typical.

[04:29] Dana Kim: And diff. Do you store a diff in the audit event?

[04:33] Kevin Osei: We can include either: a pointer to a diff artifact or include a small structured diff. Usually we avoid huge payloads in the audit log stream. What we do today for similar config objects is include “before_version” and “after_version” and a hash, and then the diff can be retrieved via an API with proper permissions.

[04:54] Luis Martinez: So the audit log shows that v18 replaced v17, but if I want the exact field change I call another endpoint.

[05:01] Kevin Osei: Correct. We can also include “changed_fields” as a list of top-level keys, like batching.max_batch_tokens, cache.prefix_cache_enabled, quant.profile_id—stuff like that.

[05:14] Dana Kim: That’s acceptable if the retrieval is also audited.

[05:17] Kevin Osei: Yep, reads can be audited too. We already support audit events for sensitive read operations in some modules; we can extend that to config diff retrieval.

[05:28] Priya Shah: Retention. What’s your default retention and can we configure it?

[05:33] Marcus Lin: For Hosted/Dedicated, audit logs are retained in Redwood’s control plane for a default period—currently 400 days for enterprise tier. We also support export to customer SIEM endpoints so you can retain longer on your side.

[05:49] Priya Shah: 400 days is okay for baseline. We need seven years in our SIEM for certain logs.

[05:55] Marcus Lin: That’s common. We’ll want to confirm the export mechanism you prefer. We can do streaming to an HTTPS webhook and we also have options like pushing to an object store destination. For Private deployments, since you operate the control plane components, the retention is in your cluster/logging stack.

[06:14] Luis Martinez: For Private, does Redwood ship an audit log service? Or do we just rely on Kubernetes logs?

[06:20] Colin O'Donnell: Good question. The Private package includes the same audit event emitter and an audit log sink that can write to your configured backend. Most customers route to their log aggregator—Splunk, Datadog, or OpenSearch. We document a Helm values pattern.

[06:39] Dana Kim: We’re mostly Splunk HEC, and sometimes S3 with Athena. Webhook is fine but it needs retries and dead-letter handling.

[06:49] Kevin Osei: Understood. Our event delivery supports at-least-once semantics. For webhook, we sign payloads, do exponential backoff, and we include an event_id for dedupe. For Private you’d configure the same mechanism or run a collector that ships to Splunk.

[07:09] Priya Shah: At least once is okay. But you need immutability. How do you prevent tampering?

[07:15] Kevin Osei: For Hosted, events are written to an append-only store and then exported. Internally we have integrity controls—write-only service accounts, restricted admin access, and we can provide evidence via SOC 2 report controls. For Private, immutability is generally the customer’s responsibility—WORM buckets or Splunk immutability settings.

[07:39] Dana Kim: Okay. RBAC next. We need granular permissions. Specifically: who can create a version, who can validate, who can apply to canary, who can promote to 100%, who can roll back.

[07:52] Marcus Lin: Our RBAC model supports separate permissions for each lifecycle action. We’ve been introducing an “optimization change operator” role. It can be split into: optimization_config.create, optimization_config.validate, optimization_config.apply, optimization_config.promote, optimization_config.rollback.

[08:11] Marcus Lin: Additionally, there are scope constraints. For example, apply/promote can be limited to a specific tenant or region. The enforcement is server-side.

[08:21] Luis Martinez: Can it be limited to “canary only”? Like you can start a rollout but can’t promote it.

[08:27] Marcus Lin: Yes, by separating apply and promote. Apply can start a rollout at 1% or to a canary shard; promote is the progression to higher percentages or full.

[08:38] Dana Kim: Do you support dual control? Like two-person approval.

[08:43] Marcus Lin: Not universally in-product today. We can integrate with your internal process via change tickets and approvals, but native two-person approval on the button click is on our longer-term roadmap.

[08:56] Priya Shah: That might be okay if we can demonstrate compensating controls: limited role assignment and audit logs.

[09:03] Dana Kim: Also break glass. If on-call does a rollback during a Sev 1, we need that to be clearly marked.

[09:11] Kevin Osei: We can mark events with a “reason” and “incident_id” field. Console rollback requires a confirmation modal; we can include a required text field “reason for rollback” and optionally link a ticket.

[09:27] Paula Mendes: The UX includes a blast radius summary and type-to-confirm for high-scope rollbacks. We can require a reason for certain scopes.

[09:36] Luis Martinez: Nice. What about “automatic rollback”? Do you do that?

[09:41] Paula Mendes: For initial GA, we’re not doing fully automated rollback for all customers. Internally and for selected accounts we can auto-pause or recommend rollback when gates fail. But the actual rollback action is human-confirmed.

[09:59] Dana Kim: Let’s talk about gates. We need assurance that you have guardrails on risky config changes.

[10:05] Paula Mendes: Safe apply uses staged rollout. You can do tenant/model/region scoping. Then within that we can do percent-based canary. Progression is gated on SLO metrics: 5xx error rate, p95 and p99 latency, saturation signals (GPU utilization, queue depth). For quantization profiles there’s a quality gate from the eval harness.

[10:30] Priya Shah: Eval harness meaning… you run a set of prompts and compare?

[10:34] Paula Mendes: Yes. It’s a fixed prompt set or customer-provided prompt set in some cases, and we compare quality metrics. It can be exact match, model-based grading, or task-specific metrics. For security review, the key is: rollout can be blocked if quality regresses beyond threshold.

[10:54] Dana Kim: Are these gates configurable per customer?

[10:58] Paula Mendes: We have defaults and allow overrides within allowed ranges. We don’t allow you to fully disable all safety checks for production scopes, unless you’re in an internal testing environment.

[11:12] Luis Martinez: We’ve had issues with other vendors where an optimization change is “instant global.” So staged is good.

[11:20] Colin O'Donnell: And this ties into the one-click rollback in Console and via API.

[11:26] Dana Kim: Rollback semantics. If I click rollback, is it instant? And what does it mean in a distributed fleet?

[11:33] Paula Mendes: It’s not “instant everywhere at the same millisecond,” but the system is designed to converge quickly. The rollback action flips the active pointer back to a prior known-good version for that scope. Workers poll / subscribe and apply the change idempotently. We have safeguards to avoid a mixed state for long.

[11:57] Paula Mendes: We also checkpoint rollout state. If apply and rollback race, rollback wins and the controller reconciles.

[12:06] Luis Martinez: So it’s eventually consistent but controlled.

[12:10] Paula Mendes: Exactly. And we emit audit events for rollback_requested and rollback_completed, and we record how many workers have applied. In Console you see progress.

[12:23] Dana Kim: Do you expose config_version in request logs? Like can I tie a latency spike to a config version.

[12:29] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, we can surface the active optimization config version in response headers optionally. Also it’s included in internal metrics tags. For customers, in the Console you’ll see version annotations on the dashboard.

[12:45] Luis Martinez: That’s important for debugging.

[12:49] Priya Shah: Now, for audit events, we need certain fields. Let me list them and you can tell me yes/no.

[12:55] Priya Shah: Event timestamp with timezone, event name, actor, actor role, source IP, user agent, action outcome (success/fail), object type, object ID, scope, before/after pointers, and correlation ID.

[13:13] Kevin Osei: Mostly yes. Timestamp is ISO. Event name yes. Actor yes. Actor role—we can include role IDs assigned at time of action. Source IP and user agent yes for Console and some API contexts (API calls have client IP). Outcome yes. Object type/ID yes. Scope yes. Before/after pointers yes. Correlation ID yes.

[13:39] Dana Kim: We also need request ID. Like a per-request unique identifier.

[13:44] Kevin Osei: We have request_id and trace_id internally; we can include both.

[13:49] Luis Martinez: For Private we’d want those too.

[13:52] Kevin Osei: Yes.

[13:54] Dana Kim: Okay. What about encryption and key management for audit logs?

[13:59] Marcus Lin: Hosted audit logs are encrypted at rest. For Dedicated and Private, keys can be managed via KMS integration depending on the deployment mode. For Private, you can use your own KMS/HSM and manage encryption on your storage backend.

[14:18] Priya Shah: Can you do customer-managed keys for Hosted?

[14:22] Marcus Lin: For pure Hosted multi-tenant, we do not support per-customer CMK yet. For Dedicated and Private, yes. This might be more relevant for data logs than audit logs, but still.

[14:35] Dana Kim: Understood.

[14:37] Luis Martinez: I want to go back to export. Webhook is okay but Splunk HEC is the preferred. Do you support HEC directly?

[14:45] Kevin Osei: Not a direct “native HEC client” in Hosted today. Most customers use the webhook receiver as an intermediary, or we can push to an S3 bucket and you ingest. For Private, you can run a collector that formats for HEC.

[15:03] Dana Kim: That’s probably fine. We have an ingestion layer.

[15:06] Priya Shah: We also need to know if Redwood staff can trigger changes in our environment and if that is audited.

[15:13] Colin O'Donnell: For Hosted/Dedicated, Redwood SRE has limited break-glass access, and actions are audited. For Private, Redwood doesn’t have access by default; it’s your cluster.

[15:25] Marcus Lin: To be precise: support access is time-bound, approval-based, and logged. We can provide policy details under NDA.

[15:34] Priya Shah: Okay.

[15:36] Dana Kim: Change management: do you have something like “change tickets” built in?

[15:41] Paula Mendes: Not as a full ITSM system. But the workflow includes required fields in the Console—description, risk level, and optional links to Jira/ServiceNow. Those show up in the config version metadata and in audit logs.

[15:58] Luis Martinez: So if we paste a ServiceNow change ID, it persists.

[16:02] Paula Mendes: Exactly.

[16:04] Dana Kim: We need approvals for prod changes. If you can’t do two-person approval, we’d probably restrict apply permissions to our change manager group.

[16:13] Marcus Lin: That’s a common pattern. We can set RBAC so only that group can promote or rollback. Or make rollback available to on-call but require reason + incident ID.

[16:26] Dana Kim: Wait, you said “rollback available to on-call,” but we need to ensure that is not blocked during an incident.

[16:33] Paula Mendes: We specifically designed rollback to be one-click with confirmation. There’s also an API endpoint. So your incident automation can do it if you choose.

[16:45] Luis Martinez: But still RBAC controlled.

[16:47] Paula Mendes: Yes.

[16:49] Priya Shah: For retention, you said 400 days. Is that configurable up?

[16:54] Marcus Lin: We can offer extended retention as an add-on for some tiers, but the most robust option is exporting to your SIEM and keeping it there. For Private, again, you control it.

[17:10] Priya Shah: Okay. Another requirement: we need to see failed attempts. Like permission denied, or validation failed.

[17:17] Kevin Osei: Absolutely. We emit audit events for both success and failure, including “denied” for RBAC. It includes the permission that was missing.

[17:27] Dana Kim: Good.

[17:29] Luis Martinez: The mis-scope scenario—like someone applies a config intended for one region to another region. Do you have guardrails?

[17:38] Paula Mendes: Yes. The apply UI and API require explicit scope, and the confirmation modal summarizes blast radius: tenant(s), model(s), region(s), and percentage. And we validate semantic constraints like region compatibility if the config references region-specific capabilities.

[17:58] Paula Mendes: Also, the history view shows scope clearly. That’s partly to prevent wrong-region mistakes.

[18:06] Dana Kim: Is there a way to “pin” a version to prevent accidental changes?

[18:10] Paula Mendes: You can pin an active version for a scope, yes. It prevents auto-promotion or other changes without elevated permission.

[18:20] Luis Martinez: That’s useful.

[18:22] Colin O'Donnell: I want to check if we covered the big questionnaire items: audit logs, RBAC, retention, export, change control, rollback.

[18:30] Dana Kim: Almost. One more: log schema stability. If we build parsers, we can’t have fields changing randomly.

[18:37] Kevin Osei: Understood. We version the event schema. We aim for backwards compatible changes only. If a breaking change is needed, it’s a new event version and communicated.

[18:48] Priya Shah: Do you have examples you can share?

[18:50] Kevin Osei: Yes, I’ll send a set of example payloads after the call. They’ll include optimization config create/apply/promote/rollback plus RBAC denial.

[19:01] Dana Kim: Please include the correlation IDs and request IDs.

[19:04] Kevin Osei: Will do.

[19:06] Luis Martinez: Another small thing: time sync. Are timestamps based on server time? Any drift issues?

[19:12] Kevin Osei: Server-side. Our infra uses NTP sync; timestamps are generated centrally. For Private, you’ll rely on your cluster time; we recommend NTP.

[19:24] Dana Kim: Good.

[19:26] Priya Shah: And access to audit logs: do we need separate permissions to view them?

[19:30] Marcus Lin: Yes. There’s an audit_log.read permission. And we can constrain it by tenant. Viewing audit logs is also logged.

[19:41] Dana Kim: Great.

[19:44] Luis Martinez: Can we filter audit events by model or region?

[19:48] Kevin Osei: Yes, because scope is structured. You can query by tenant_id, model, region, config_version.

[19:57] Paula Mendes: And in Console, the history screen can filter by those dimensions.

[20:03] Dana Kim: Okay. Shifting to operational change controls: do you have documented runbooks for rollback.

[20:09] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, there’s an internal and a customer-facing runbook. It includes decision criteria (latency increase, 5xx) and verification steps post-rollback.

[20:19] Dana Kim: We’ll want the customer-facing one.

[20:21] Colin O'Donnell: We can share a draft.

[20:24] Priya Shah: Also, do you do game days? Like testing rollback.

[20:28] Paula Mendes: Internally yes. We have a gameday plan where we simulate a bad config rollout and validate rollback and audit events.

[20:38] Dana Kim: That’s good.

[20:40] Luis Martinez: On Private, is rollback one-click too? Or is it like Helm rollback.

[20:45] Colin O'Donnell: It’s operator-friendly. Typically you can run a command or update a Helm value to pin back to a prior version. The same version model applies.

[20:55] Luis Martinez: Okay. That might be acceptable.

[21:00] Dana Kim: I’m going to ask a slightly adversarial question. If Redwood has a bug and your audit events are missing, how do we know?

[21:09] Kevin Osei: Fair. We have monitoring on the audit pipeline and alerting if event rates drop unexpectedly. Also, the apply/rollback actions themselves are transactional; if the audit emission fails in certain critical paths, we can fail the operation rather than proceed silently.

[21:28] Dana Kim: That’s what we want.

[21:30] Priya Shah: And can we see “audit log pipeline health”?

[21:34] Colin O'Donnell: In Dedicated we can expose status; in Hosted it’s more internal, but we can provide SLA statements and can share operational metrics on request.

[21:45] Luis Martinez: Makes sense.

[21:47] Dana Kim: Alright. I think we have enough. Colin, can you recap next steps.

[21:53] Colin O'Donnell: Yep. Kevin will send example audit log payloads including required fields and event names. Marcus will follow up with RBAC permission mapping and what we can and cannot do for dual-approval. Paula will send a brief statement on safe apply gates and rollback semantics.

[22:12] Colin O'Donnell: And Luis, you’ll confirm your SIEM ingestion preference and any required fields we missed. Also, we’ll schedule a Private deployment deep dive.

[22:23] Luis Martinez: Yep, I’ll send that.

[22:25] Priya Shah: And I’ll forward the questionnaire section.

[22:28] Dana Kim: Thanks.

[22:30] Colin O'Donnell: Great. We’ll end early unless anyone has more.

[22:34] Evan Ross: Nothing from me.

[22:36] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. Thanks everyone.

(Note: Fireflies transcription continued to capture minor crosstalk and silence; meeting ended around 57 minutes with additional off-topic procurement scheduling not transcribed in detail.)

title: Summit Insurance — RFP Technical Deep Dive (Private deployment, SSO/RBAC, audit logging)
preview:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Summit Insurance — RFP Technical Deep Dive (Private deployment, SSO/RBAC, audit logging)
Date: Dec 12, 2025
Start time: 9:00 AM PT
Duration: 63 min
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Lukas Meyer (AE), Colin O'Donnell (Solutions Eng, Private deployments), Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)
Summit Insurance: Priya Nair (Security Architect), Ben Crawford (IAM Lead), Mei Tan (Platform Engineering), Alyssa Grant (Vendor Risk)

Auto-summary (generated)
- Summit is evaluating Redwood Private for regulated workloads and needs RFP-ready answers on identity controls, role-based access, and audit logging.
- Redwood reviewed SSO (SAML) coverage for Console, SCIM provisioning options, and how API/service access is handled (API keys/service accounts) in Private.
- Redwood walked through audit log event types, retention controls, and export patterns (S3/SIEM) with deployment-mode caveats.
- Summit emphasized requirements: immutable audit trail, least-privilege admin separation, and no customer prompt data used for training.

Topics
1) Redwood Private architecture + shared responsibility
2) SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning
3) RBAC model (Console + API) and separation of duties
4) Audit logs: event taxonomy, export, retention, and integrity
5) RFP wording guardrails (no roadmap promises)

Action items (captured)
- Colin O'Donnell (Redwood): Send “Private deployment security controls” write-up + shared responsibility bullets for RFP language (ETA: early next week)
- Marcus Lin (Redwood): Provide approved audit logging language (event list + export options) and what’s “available today” vs “not supported” (ETA: early next week)
- Lukas Meyer (Redwood): Send SOC 2 evidence request steps (NDA + intake) and link to subprocessor/DPA request process (ETA: today)
- Ben Crawford (Summit): Confirm Summit’s IdP (Okta vs Azure AD) and whether SCIM is required on Day 1 (ETA: by Tue)
- Mei Tan (Summit): Share SIEM target (Splunk HEC details or S3 path) + retention requirement (365d vs 7y) (ETA: by Tue)

Questions (noted)
- Does SSO cover all admin access for Private, including break-glass scenarios?
- Can audit logs be exported near-real-time to Splunk, and are events tamper-evident?
- What is the RBAC granularity for model deployment actions vs billing/usage views?
- Is any customer content used to train models by default?

---

Transcript

[00:00] Lukas Meyer: Alright, thanks everyone for making time. The goal today is the technical deep dive for the RFP—specifically Private deployment security controls, SSO and RBAC, and then audit logging. Colin’s going to lead. Marcus is here in case we get into the weeds on controls.

[00:15] Priya Nair: Sounds good. For context, our vendor risk team is basically blocked until we can answer those sections with specificity. “We support audit logs” doesn’t cut it.

[00:25] Colin O'Donnell: Yep, totally get it. And we’re going to be careful to separate what’s available today from anything we’re still working on. If we can’t do something, we’ll say it.

[00:35] Alyssa Grant: Thank you. That helps.

[00:38] Colin O'Donnell: Quick agenda: I’ll do a two-minute overview of Redwood Private and shared responsibility. Then identity: SAML SSO, SCIM, and how service access works. Then RBAC. Then audit logs—events, retention, and export paths.

[00:55] Mei Tan: Perfect.

[00:57] Colin O'Donnell: Redwood Private—think of it as our control plane running in a private context with customer-managed networking. The inference data plane (where prompts/responses are processed) is deployed into your environment—typically your VPC. In on-prem, similar story but installed into your cluster. The key point: customer request payloads do not traverse the public internet, and we can restrict any egress.

[01:25] Priya Nair: So no callouts to your hosted API at inference time.

[01:28] Colin O'Donnell: Correct. For Private, inference happens inside your environment. There can be optional telemetry, but it’s configurable and we can scope it to operational metrics rather than content.

[01:41] Marcus Lin: And to be explicit—customer content isn’t used for training our models by default. For the RFP question, the clean statement is: we do not train on customer prompts or outputs unless there is an explicit, written agreement to do so. Default is no.

[01:56] Ben Crawford: Good. That one comes up a lot.

[02:00] Lukas Meyer: And we’ll send the exact approved wording so it stays consistent.

[02:06] Colin O'Donnell: Shared responsibility: we provide the software, hardening guidance, and security patches; you control the cloud account boundaries, network, and underlying cluster posture. Depending on the contract, Redwood can run the deployment with you (managed) or you can run it (self-operated). The controls like RBAC and audit logging are in the product either way.

[02:35] Mei Tan: For this RFP, assume customer-operated in our AWS account, EKS.

[02:40] Colin O'Donnell: Great.

[02:42] Colin O'Donnell: Identity—SSO. The Redwood Console supports SAML 2.0 SSO for interactive users. Typical IdPs: Okta, Azure AD, Ping, etc. We have customer-by-customer configuration, domain allowlists, and we can require SSO.

[03:03] Ben Crawford: When you say “Console”, is that also how we manage models, endpoints, and view logs?

[03:08] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. Admin actions happen either through the Console UI or our admin APIs. For Private, Console access can be private-routed, like behind your VPN/PrivateLink, depending on deployment pattern.

[03:23] Ben Crawford: And SCIM. We need automatic provisioning and deprovisioning.

[03:27] Colin O'Donnell: SCIM—yes, we support SCIM for user provisioning for the Console. Some folks only do SAML + JIT provisioning; if you require SCIM for lifecycle management, we can support that. I’ll caveat: the exact fields and group-to-role mapping can vary; we’ll provide the “supported today” mapping in writing.

[03:51] Ben Crawford: Group-to-role mapping is the big one. We don’t want manual role assignment.

[03:56] Marcus Lin: The current behavior is: users can be provisioned via SCIM, and roles can be assigned based on groups depending on configuration. Where customers want very strict separation, we recommend a small admin group and then read-only or developer roles for broader access.

[04:16] Priya Nair: Is MFA required?

[04:18] Colin O'Donnell: MFA is enforced at the IdP level for SSO users. We don’t implement a separate Redwood MFA prompt in front of SAML—so the control is “use SSO + MFA at IdP.”

[04:33] Priya Nair: That’s fine; we standardize that.

[04:36] Colin O'Donnell: Now, non-interactive access: API keys and service accounts. For automation (deploying endpoints, rotating tokens, pulling usage), you can issue API keys scoped by role. We recommend storing them in your secrets manager and rotating.

[04:55] Mei Tan: Are those keys tied to a human user?

[04:57] Colin O'Donnell: They can be issued under a service account. We can avoid tying them to an employee identity, which helps for audits.

[05:05] Ben Crawford: Do you support OIDC for API access instead of static keys?

[05:09] Colin O'Donnell: For API access, the standard is API keys today. We should not claim OIDC token exchange unless it’s part of your specific contract scope—so for RFP, answer as API keys/service accounts. If OIDC is a hard requirement, we can flag it as a gap.

[05:28] Priya Nair: Okay, call it out as not supported, that’s okay. Better than fuzzy.

[05:33] Colin O'Donnell: Exactly.

[05:35] Colin O'Donnell: RBAC—roles and permissions. We have roles like Org Admin, Security Admin (if enabled), Developer/Operator, and Read-only. The important part for your RFP: we can separate who can deploy models/endpoints from who can view billing/usage, and from who can manage identity settings.

[05:58] Alyssa Grant: Does “Security Admin” exist as a distinct role, or is that marketing?

[06:02] Marcus Lin: It’s a real role concept. The precise naming in the UI can vary by version, but the permission boundary exists: managing auth settings, audit logs access, and policy settings can be restricted. We’ll provide a role-permission summary for the RFP response library.

[06:22] Ben Crawford: We also need “break glass.” Like if Okta is down.

[06:26] Colin O'Donnell: Yep. There’s typically a break-glass local admin account controlled by you. In Private, that can be a local credential stored in your vault with restricted access. For RFP: “supported via customer-controlled break-glass admin; access can be logged.”

[06:45] Priya Nair: But is that still logged if SSO is down?

[06:48] Marcus Lin: Yes, login events are audit logged locally. Obviously, if your logging backend is down too, nothing can write. But under normal operation, break-glass actions show up as events.

[07:03] Mei Tan: On RBAC granularity—can you restrict specific actions like “create endpoint” vs “update endpoint config” vs “delete endpoint”?

[07:12] Colin O'Donnell: We have action-level permissions for major admin actions. I don’t want to over-promise per-field granularity for every setting, but the standard operations are separated: create/update/delete endpoint, manage API keys, manage users/roles, view logs.

[07:31] Lukas Meyer: We can attach the RBAC capability summary as an appendix so you can map it directly to the questionnaire.

[07:38] Priya Nair: Great.

[07:40] Colin O'Donnell: Audit logs. There are two categories people care about: admin/audit events and request logs. For admin audit events: who logged in, who created/rotated keys, who changed roles, who deployed a model or changed an endpoint config, who changed retention settings, that kind of thing.

[08:05] Priya Nair: We need those. Also, we need to know if prompt/response is included in logs.

[08:10] Colin O'Donnell: By default, we do not recommend logging prompt/response content in audit logs. Audit logs are focused on control-plane events. Request telemetry—latency, token counts—can be collected without content. In Private, since it’s your environment, you can choose to capture more, but Redwood’s audit log stream itself is not meant to store customer content.

[08:33] Marcus Lin: And that’s an important RFP nuance: “audit logs do not contain customer prompts/outputs by default.” That’s usually the safest answer.

[08:43] Ben Crawford: What fields do you provide in audit events?

[08:46] Colin O'Donnell: Typical: timestamp, actor (user/service account), actor IP (where available), action type, object (endpoint/model/user), outcome (success/failure), and sometimes request ID for correlation.

[09:05] Mei Tan: IP “where available” meaning?

[09:08] Colin O'Donnell: In Private, if you’re behind proxies or NAT, we’ll capture the source as seen by the service; you can also pass through forwarded headers depending on your ingress config. We’ll document what’s captured.

[09:22] Priya Nair: Export. We need to pipe to Splunk. Near-real-time.

[09:27] Colin O'Donnell: Export options: (1) ship to an S3 bucket in your account, then you forward to Splunk; (2) direct webhook-style export to a SIEM endpoint, depending on your network policy; (3) pull via API. For Private, the most common is S3 because it’s simple, durable, and keeps data in your account.

[09:54] Mei Tan: Do you have a Splunk HEC integration out of the box?

[09:57] Colin O'Donnell: Not “one-click Splunk” as a product checkbox. It’s usually done via forwarding from S3 or a small connector. We can provide examples, but for RFP answer: “export supported via S3/API; SIEM integration via customer-controlled forwarding.”

[10:16] Priya Nair: Okay. We can work with that.

[10:19] Alyssa Grant: Tamper-evident logs: do you sign them?

[10:23] Marcus Lin: We don’t claim cryptographic signing of every audit record as a standard feature today. Tamper resistance is typically handled by writing to an append-only destination you control—S3 with Object Lock / WORM, or your SIEM with immutability settings. We can help configure patterns, but we should not say “signed logs” unless it’s true.

[10:47] Priya Nair: That’s exactly the kind of precision I need.

[10:50] Colin O'Donnell: Retention: in the product, audit logs have a default retention, and for enterprise/compliance use cases, retention can be configured—especially if you export. In Private, since it’s your storage, you can enforce 1-year, 7-years, whatever your policy is, by your bucket lifecycle + object lock.

[11:15] Mei Tan: So Redwood doesn’t force-delete exported logs.

[11:18] Colin O'Donnell: Correct. Exported logs live where you put them.

[11:22] Ben Crawford: For the Console, can we restrict who can view audit logs?

[11:26] Colin O'Donnell: Yes—viewing audit logs is a permission.

[11:30] Priya Nair: Do you log when someone views audit logs?

[11:33] Marcus Lin: Viewing the audit log UI is not always a separate event today; the safer statement is: “administrative actions are logged; access to audit logs is restricted by RBAC.” If you need “audit the auditors” specifically, we should treat that as a requirement and verify the current behavior before claiming it.

[11:55] Lukas Meyer: We can follow up with the exact answer once Marcus double-checks the event taxonomy.

[12:01] Priya Nair: Please.

[12:03] Colin O'Donnell: Also, incident response questions often come along with audit logs. Redwood has an incident response program, but for RFP, we typically provide the high-level process and then SOC 2 report under NDA covers more.

[12:18] Alyssa Grant: Yes, we’ll ask for SOC 2.

[12:21] Lukas Meyer: Totally. We have a standard evidence request process—NDA first, then we provide the report. I’ll send the steps after this call.

[12:30] Ben Crawford: Quick one on SSO: can you enforce that local passwords are disabled?

[12:35] Colin O'Donnell: In SSO-required mode, we can require SAML for standard users. Break-glass is the exception, controlled by you. So the right language is “SSO can be enforced for all users except customer-controlled break-glass admin.”

[12:55] Priya Nair: Works.

[12:58] Mei Tan: Network: any outbound connectivity required from the inference cluster?

[13:03] Colin O'Donnell: For pure inference, no outbound required besides what you configure for model artifact access (if you’re pulling model weights from your registry) and for any export endpoints you choose (like S3). We can run with restricted egress. If you want zero egress, we can do that, but it changes how updates and monitoring work. We’ll document the tradeoffs.

[13:30] Priya Nair: For our regulated environment, outbound is heavily restricted. So we’ll probably do private endpoints and no general internet.

[13:39] Colin O'Donnell: That’s common.

[13:41] Ben Crawford: Encryption—are you using TLS end-to-end? And at rest, KMS?

[13:47] Colin O'Donnell: In transit: TLS for API calls; internal service-to-service can be TLS depending on your service mesh and deployment. At rest: data stored by the system—configs, logs, etc.—is encrypted at rest, typically via your cloud provider primitives. If you use AWS, it’s KMS-backed. For keys: in Private, keys are in your account.

[14:15] Priya Nair: Any HSM support?

[14:18] Colin O'Donnell: If you use KMS with CloudHSM-backed keys, that’s on the AWS side. Redwood integrates at the platform layer; we don’t ship our own HSM.

[14:30] Marcus Lin: Again, for RFP language: “supports customer-managed encryption keys via cloud KMS in Private deployments.” Keep it simple.

[14:41] Mei Tan: Logging again—do you have request IDs so we can correlate application logs with your audit logs?

[14:47] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, we return request IDs, and they appear in operational logs and sometimes audit trails depending on event type. For token usage dashboards, you can correlate at the route/model level.

[15:05] Priya Nair: We also have a requirement around “administrative access to production” for vendors. Do Redwood employees have access into our Private cluster?

[15:14] Colin O'Donnell: Default is no. It’s your environment. If you choose a managed option where Redwood assists, access is time-bound and approved, and should be auditable through your IAM + our logs. For the RFP, the safest default statement is “no standing access; any support access is customer-authorized and logged.”

[15:38] Alyssa Grant: That will help.

[15:41] Lukas Meyer: One more thing I’m hearing across deals: data residency. You’re US-only, correct?

[15:47] Priya Nair: For Summit, yes, US. But we still require the statement that data stays in our account.

[15:53] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, the statement is straightforward: customer content is processed and stored within your environment, subject to your configuration.

[16:04] Ben Crawford: Okay.

[16:06] (crosstalk)

[16:07] Mei Tan: Sorry—what about system metrics? Do they ever get sent back to you?

[16:12] Colin O'Donnell: Optional. Some customers allow anonymized operational metrics to Redwood for support—like CPU/GPU utilization, error rates. We can configure that off, or route it through your own observability stack. We’ll put that in the shared responsibility notes.

[16:35] Priya Nair: Please include it, because “telemetry” triggers questions for us.

[16:39] Marcus Lin: We can also include a line that Redwood does not require customer prompt content for support troubleshooting; we can work off metrics and customer-provided samples when needed.

[16:52] Priya Nair: Great.

[16:54] Lukas Meyer: Do you want to spend the rest of the time on audit log specifics? Or RBAC mapping to your roles.

[17:00] Ben Crawford: RBAC mapping. We have three personas: platform admins, app developers, and auditors.

[17:06] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Platform admins: can manage deployment config, endpoints, and system settings. App developers: can create API keys for their apps (if allowed), view usage/performance dashboards, but not change org-wide security settings. Auditors: read-only access to audit logs and config state. That maps cleanly.

[17:34] Priya Nair: Can “auditor” see prompts?

[17:37] Colin O'Donnell: No, not unless you build separate logging in your app that captures prompts. Redwood’s audit logs are control-plane.

[17:46] Mei Tan: That’s preferred.

[17:48] Ben Crawford: User lifecycle—if someone is removed from Okta, do they lose access immediately?

[17:54] Colin O'Donnell: With SAML, access is tied to IdP authentication. With SCIM, deprovisioning happens when SCIM pushes the update; typical is near real-time but depends on IdP. For RFP: “deprovisioning supported via SCIM; access can be revoked via IdP; API keys should be rotated.”

[18:20] Priya Nair: Make sure you mention key rotation; that’s another checkbox.

[18:24] Colin O'Donnell: Yep.

[18:26] Marcus Lin: On audit logs: we log API key creation and revocation events.

[18:32] Ben Crawford: And failed logins?

[18:34] Marcus Lin: Yes, authentication failures are captured as security events.

[18:39] Priya Nair: Good.

[18:41] Lukas Meyer: I’m going to ask a potentially annoying question: do you need us to answer HIPAA or PCI sections? Or is it general security.

[18:49] Alyssa Grant: General security plus SOC 2 is the main thing. We do have a “HIPAA applicable?” question, but we can mark N/A. We’re not doing PHI in this workload.

[19:01] Lukas Meyer: Great. We won’t position a BAA or anything unless requested.

[19:06] Colin O'Donnell: Back to audit log export—Mei, you mentioned Splunk HEC. If you can share whether you prefer direct HEC or S3-based forwarding, we’ll tailor guidance.

[19:18] Mei Tan: Likely S3 + our existing forwarder.

[19:22] Colin O'Donnell: Perfect.

[19:24] Priya Nair: One more control: vulnerability management. Do you do pen tests? Frequency?

[19:30] Marcus Lin: We do regular vulnerability scanning and periodic third-party penetration testing. For exact frequency, we provide approved language rather than making up a number on a call. The SOC 2 report and/or pen test attestation under NDA is the right channel.

[19:50] Priya Nair: That’s fine—just don’t say “annual” if it’s not always annual.

[19:54] Marcus Lin: Exactly.

[19:56] Colin O'Donnell: Any other must-have RFP items we haven’t hit?

[20:00] Alyssa Grant: Data retention. Specifically: “How long do you retain customer data?”

[20:06] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, customer content retention is governed by you because content stays in your environment. For Hosted/Dedicated, there are defined retention policies for logs and operational metadata; but since you’re evaluating Private, the correct answer is: “customer controls retention; Redwood does not retain prompts/outputs outside the customer environment by default.”

[20:28] Priya Nair: Perfect.

[20:30] Ben Crawford: Do you have audit log retention controls in-product or is it just “ship it out”?

[20:36] Colin O'Donnell: Both. There’s in-product retention for the console view, and export for long-term retention. In your scenario, we expect export to be the durable source.

[20:49] Mei Tan: Okay.

[20:51] (brief silence)

[20:52] Lukas Meyer: We’re at the 20-minute mark in the transcript; we can keep going on any open questions.

[20:57] Priya Nair: I want to understand the boundary between “audit log” and “application log.” Our auditors sometimes ask for “every API call log.”

[21:06] Colin O'Donnell: Great point. We do have request logs/metrics that can include endpoint name, timestamp, latency, token counts, status code. We do not frame that as “audit logs” because it’s not about admin actions. And we don’t recommend including payloads. If your auditors need per-request traceability, we can support correlation IDs, and you can capture request logs at your ingress layer.

[21:35] Priya Nair: That’s the answer I need. We’ll implement at ingress.

[21:39] Marcus Lin: And we’ll provide written language: “Redwood provides admin audit events; request-level telemetry is available; payload logging is customer-controlled.”

[21:51] Ben Crawford: Are the audit logs accessible via API? We have an internal GRC tool that pulls.

[21:56] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, there’s an API for audit events export/pull, subject to permissions.

[22:04] Mei Tan: Rate limits?

[22:06] Colin O'Donnell: There are reasonable rate limits; for bulk export we recommend S3 or scheduled pulls. We can tune if needed, but again, won’t promise unlimited.

[22:20] Priya Nair: Okay.

[22:22] Lukas Meyer: Competitors—just for context, are you comparing this to Bedrock private endpoints or Azure OpenAI in your tenant?

[22:31] Priya Nair: We looked at Bedrock. The control story is okay but the model selection and performance tuning isn’t great for our workloads. Azure we already use for other stuff but same issue.

[22:44] Colin O'Donnell: Makes sense.

[22:46] Mei Tan: Also Together is in the mix, but Private deployment was less mature.

[22:50] Lukas Meyer: Helpful.

[22:52] Colin O'Donnell: Anything else on SSO? Ben, you asked about Okta vs Azure AD.

[22:58] Ben Crawford: It’s Okta for workforce. We might use Azure AD for some app-to-app. But for admin console, Okta.

[23:08] Colin O'Donnell: Great—Okta SAML and SCIM is a standard setup.

[23:13] Priya Nair: Can you do device posture? Like “only managed devices.”

[23:19] Colin O'Donnell: That’s enforced at your IdP / access proxy layer. Redwood doesn’t have a device agent. So for RFP: “supported via IdP conditional access.”

[23:33] Priya Nair: Great.

[23:35] Marcus Lin: And network-level restrictions can also help: private routing, IP allowlists, etc.

[23:41] Mei Tan: Yep.

[23:43] (Fireflies note: audio overlap)

[23:45] Priya Nair: Last thing—do you have a standard “shared responsibility model” write-up? We always attach those.

[23:52] Colin O'Donnell: We have internal docs; we’ll send a customer-facing version that’s RFP friendly.

[24:00] Lukas Meyer: We’ll make sure it’s the approved one.

[24:03] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. I think we covered the core. Marcus, anything you want to add on controls language?

[24:10] Marcus Lin: Only that we’ll provide approved statements for: SOC 2 availability under NDA, vulnerability management without stating an incorrect cadence, and audit log integrity guidance (S3 Object Lock, SIEM immutability) without claiming “cryptographic signing.”

[24:30] Priya Nair: Thank you. That’s exactly what we need.

[24:34] Lukas Meyer: Before we wrap, recap next steps: we’ll send the Private deployment appendix and RBAC summary, plus audit log export/retention language. You’ll confirm IdP details (Okta) and whether SCIM is day-one, and share Splunk forwarding preference.

[24:53] Ben Crawford: Yep.

[24:54] Mei Tan: Yep, S3 forwarding.

[24:56] Alyssa Grant: And send the SOC 2 request instructions.

[25:00] Lukas Meyer: Will do today.

[25:02] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Thanks all.

[25:05] (Meeting ended in transcript; remainder of time was post-call note capture / Fireflies processing)
text:
Fireflies.ai Transcript

Meeting: Summit Insurance — RFP Technical Deep Dive (Private deployment, SSO/RBAC, audit logging)
Date: Dec 12, 2025
Start time: 9:00 AM PT
Duration: 63 min
Recorded by: Fireflies.ai

Attendees
Redwood Inference: Lukas Meyer (AE), Colin O'Donnell (Solutions Eng, Private deployments), Marcus Lin (Security Engineering)
Summit Insurance: Priya Nair (Security Architect), Ben Crawford (IAM Lead), Mei Tan (Platform Engineering), Alyssa Grant (Vendor Risk)

Auto-summary (generated)
- Summit is evaluating Redwood Private for regulated workloads and needs RFP-ready answers on identity controls, role-based access, and audit logging.
- Redwood reviewed SSO (SAML) coverage for Console, SCIM provisioning options, and how API/service access is handled (API keys/service accounts) in Private.
- Redwood walked through audit log event types, retention controls, and export patterns (S3/SIEM) with deployment-mode caveats.
- Summit emphasized requirements: immutable audit trail, least-privilege admin separation, and no customer prompt data used for training.

Topics
1) Redwood Private architecture + shared responsibility
2) SSO/SAML and SCIM provisioning
3) RBAC model (Console + API) and separation of duties
4) Audit logs: event taxonomy, export, retention, and integrity
5) RFP wording guardrails (no roadmap promises)

Action items (captured)
- Colin O'Donnell (Redwood): Send “Private deployment security controls” write-up + shared responsibility bullets for RFP language (ETA: early next week)
- Marcus Lin (Redwood): Provide approved audit logging language (event list + export options) and what’s “available today” vs “not supported” (ETA: early next week)
- Lukas Meyer (Redwood): Send SOC 2 evidence request steps (NDA + intake) and link to subprocessor/DPA request process (ETA: today)
- Ben Crawford (Summit): Confirm Summit’s IdP (Okta vs Azure AD) and whether SCIM is required on Day 1 (ETA: by Tue)
- Mei Tan (Summit): Share SIEM target (Splunk HEC details or S3 path) + retention requirement (365d vs 7y) (ETA: by Tue)

Questions (noted)
- Does SSO cover all admin access for Private, including break-glass scenarios?
- Can audit logs be exported near-real-time to Splunk, and are events tamper-evident?
- What is the RBAC granularity for model deployment actions vs billing/usage views?
- Is any customer content used to train models by default?

---

Transcript

[00:00] Lukas Meyer: Alright, thanks everyone for making time. The goal today is the technical deep dive for the RFP—specifically Private deployment security controls, SSO and RBAC, and then audit logging. Colin’s going to lead. Marcus is here in case we get into the weeds on controls.

[00:15] Priya Nair: Sounds good. For context, our vendor risk team is basically blocked until we can answer those sections with specificity. “We support audit logs” doesn’t cut it.

[00:25] Colin O'Donnell: Yep, totally get it. And we’re going to be careful to separate what’s available today from anything we’re still working on. If we can’t do something, we’ll say it.

[00:35] Alyssa Grant: Thank you. That helps.

[00:38] Colin O'Donnell: Quick agenda: I’ll do a two-minute overview of Redwood Private and shared responsibility. Then identity: SAML SSO, SCIM, and how service access works. Then RBAC. Then audit logs—events, retention, and export paths.

[00:55] Mei Tan: Perfect.

[00:57] Colin O'Donnell: Redwood Private—think of it as our control plane running in a private context with customer-managed networking. The inference data plane (where prompts/responses are processed) is deployed into your environment—typically your VPC. In on-prem, similar story but installed into your cluster. The key point: customer request payloads do not traverse the public internet, and we can restrict any egress.

[01:25] Priya Nair: So no callouts to your hosted API at inference time.

[01:28] Colin O'Donnell: Correct. For Private, inference happens inside your environment. There can be optional telemetry, but it’s configurable and we can scope it to operational metrics rather than content.

[01:41] Marcus Lin: And to be explicit—customer content isn’t used for training our models by default. For the RFP question, the clean statement is: we do not train on customer prompts or outputs unless there is an explicit, written agreement to do so. Default is no.

[01:56] Ben Crawford: Good. That one comes up a lot.

[02:00] Lukas Meyer: And we’ll send the exact approved wording so it stays consistent.

[02:06] Colin O'Donnell: Shared responsibility: we provide the software, hardening guidance, and security patches; you control the cloud account boundaries, network, and underlying cluster posture. Depending on the contract, Redwood can run the deployment with you (managed) or you can run it (self-operated). The controls like RBAC and audit logging are in the product either way.

[02:35] Mei Tan: For this RFP, assume customer-operated in our AWS account, EKS.

[02:40] Colin O'Donnell: Great.

[02:42] Colin O'Donnell: Identity—SSO. The Redwood Console supports SAML 2.0 SSO for interactive users. Typical IdPs: Okta, Azure AD, Ping, etc. We have customer-by-customer configuration, domain allowlists, and we can require SSO.

[03:03] Ben Crawford: When you say “Console”, is that also how we manage models, endpoints, and view logs?

[03:08] Colin O'Donnell: Yes. Admin actions happen either through the Console UI or our admin APIs. For Private, Console access can be private-routed, like behind your VPN/PrivateLink, depending on deployment pattern.

[03:23] Ben Crawford: And SCIM. We need automatic provisioning and deprovisioning.

[03:27] Colin O'Donnell: SCIM—yes, we support SCIM for user provisioning for the Console. Some folks only do SAML + JIT provisioning; if you require SCIM for lifecycle management, we can support that. I’ll caveat: the exact fields and group-to-role mapping can vary; we’ll provide the “supported today” mapping in writing.

[03:51] Ben Crawford: Group-to-role mapping is the big one. We don’t want manual role assignment.

[03:56] Marcus Lin: The current behavior is: users can be provisioned via SCIM, and roles can be assigned based on groups depending on configuration. Where customers want very strict separation, we recommend a small admin group and then read-only or developer roles for broader access.

[04:16] Priya Nair: Is MFA required?

[04:18] Colin O'Donnell: MFA is enforced at the IdP level for SSO users. We don’t implement a separate Redwood MFA prompt in front of SAML—so the control is “use SSO + MFA at IdP.”

[04:33] Priya Nair: That’s fine; we standardize that.

[04:36] Colin O'Donnell: Now, non-interactive access: API keys and service accounts. For automation (deploying endpoints, rotating tokens, pulling usage), you can issue API keys scoped by role. We recommend storing them in your secrets manager and rotating.

[04:55] Mei Tan: Are those keys tied to a human user?

[04:57] Colin O'Donnell: They can be issued under a service account. We can avoid tying them to an employee identity, which helps for audits.

[05:05] Ben Crawford: Do you support OIDC for API access instead of static keys?

[05:09] Colin O'Donnell: For API access, the standard is API keys today. We should not claim OIDC token exchange unless it’s part of your specific contract scope—so for RFP, answer as API keys/service accounts. If OIDC is a hard requirement, we can flag it as a gap.

[05:28] Priya Nair: Okay, call it out as not supported, that’s okay. Better than fuzzy.

[05:33] Colin O'Donnell: Exactly.

[05:35] Colin O'Donnell: RBAC—roles and permissions. We have roles like Org Admin, Security Admin (if enabled), Developer/Operator, and Read-only. The important part for your RFP: we can separate who can deploy models/endpoints from who can view billing/usage, and from who can manage identity settings.

[05:58] Alyssa Grant: Does “Security Admin” exist as a distinct role, or is that marketing?

[06:02] Marcus Lin: It’s a real role concept. The precise naming in the UI can vary by version, but the permission boundary exists: managing auth settings, audit logs access, and policy settings can be restricted. We’ll provide a role-permission summary for the RFP response library.

[06:22] Ben Crawford: We also need “break glass.” Like if Okta is down.

[06:26] Colin O'Donnell: Yep. There’s typically a break-glass local admin account controlled by you. In Private, that can be a local credential stored in your vault with restricted access. For RFP: “supported via customer-controlled break-glass admin; access can be logged.”

[06:45] Priya Nair: But is that still logged if SSO is down?

[06:48] Marcus Lin: Yes, login events are audit logged locally. Obviously, if your logging backend is down too, nothing can write. But under normal operation, break-glass actions show up as events.

[07:03] Mei Tan: On RBAC granularity—can you restrict specific actions like “create endpoint” vs “update endpoint config” vs “delete endpoint”?

[07:12] Colin O'Donnell: We have action-level permissions for major admin actions. I don’t want to over-promise per-field granularity for every setting, but the standard operations are separated: create/update/delete endpoint, manage API keys, manage users/roles, view logs.

[07:31] Lukas Meyer: We can attach the RBAC capability summary as an appendix so you can map it directly to the questionnaire.

[07:38] Priya Nair: Great.

[07:40] Colin O'Donnell: Audit logs. There are two categories people care about: admin/audit events and request logs. For admin audit events: who logged in, who created/rotated keys, who changed roles, who deployed a model or changed an endpoint config, who changed retention settings, that kind of thing.

[08:05] Priya Nair: We need those. Also, we need to know if prompt/response is included in logs.

[08:10] Colin O'Donnell: By default, we do not recommend logging prompt/response content in audit logs. Audit logs are focused on control-plane events. Request telemetry—latency, token counts—can be collected without content. In Private, since it’s your environment, you can choose to capture more, but Redwood’s audit log stream itself is not meant to store customer content.

[08:33] Marcus Lin: And that’s an important RFP nuance: “audit logs do not contain customer prompts/outputs by default.” That’s usually the safest answer.

[08:43] Ben Crawford: What fields do you provide in audit events?

[08:46] Colin O'Donnell: Typical: timestamp, actor (user/service account), actor IP (where available), action type, object (endpoint/model/user), outcome (success/failure), and sometimes request ID for correlation.

[09:05] Mei Tan: IP “where available” meaning?

[09:08] Colin O'Donnell: In Private, if you’re behind proxies or NAT, we’ll capture the source as seen by the service; you can also pass through forwarded headers depending on your ingress config. We’ll document what’s captured.

[09:22] Priya Nair: Export. We need to pipe to Splunk. Near-real-time.

[09:27] Colin O'Donnell: Export options: (1) ship to an S3 bucket in your account, then you forward to Splunk; (2) direct webhook-style export to a SIEM endpoint, depending on your network policy; (3) pull via API. For Private, the most common is S3 because it’s simple, durable, and keeps data in your account.

[09:54] Mei Tan: Do you have a Splunk HEC integration out of the box?

[09:57] Colin O'Donnell: Not “one-click Splunk” as a product checkbox. It’s usually done via forwarding from S3 or a small connector. We can provide examples, but for RFP answer: “export supported via S3/API; SIEM integration via customer-controlled forwarding.”

[10:16] Priya Nair: Okay. We can work with that.

[10:19] Alyssa Grant: Tamper-evident logs: do you sign them?

[10:23] Marcus Lin: We don’t claim cryptographic signing of every audit record as a standard feature today. Tamper resistance is typically handled by writing to an append-only destination you control—S3 with Object Lock / WORM, or your SIEM with immutability settings. We can help configure patterns, but we should not say “signed logs” unless it’s true.

[10:47] Priya Nair: That’s exactly the kind of precision I need.

[10:50] Colin O'Donnell: Retention: in the product, audit logs have a default retention, and for enterprise/compliance use cases, retention can be configured—especially if you export. In Private, since it’s your storage, you can enforce 1-year, 7-years, whatever your policy is, by your bucket lifecycle + object lock.

[11:15] Mei Tan: So Redwood doesn’t force-delete exported logs.

[11:18] Colin O'Donnell: Correct. Exported logs live where you put them.

[11:22] Ben Crawford: For the Console, can we restrict who can view audit logs?

[11:26] Colin O'Donnell: Yes—viewing audit logs is a permission.

[11:30] Priya Nair: Do you log when someone views audit logs?

[11:33] Marcus Lin: Viewing the audit log UI is not always a separate event today; the safer statement is: “administrative actions are logged; access to audit logs is restricted by RBAC.” If you need “audit the auditors” specifically, we should treat that as a requirement and verify the current behavior before claiming it.

[11:55] Lukas Meyer: We can follow up with the exact answer once Marcus double-checks the event taxonomy.

[12:01] Priya Nair: Please.

[12:03] Colin O'Donnell: Also, incident response questions often come along with audit logs. Redwood has an incident response program, but for RFP, we typically provide the high-level process and then SOC 2 report under NDA covers more.

[12:18] Alyssa Grant: Yes, we’ll ask for SOC 2.

[12:21] Lukas Meyer: Totally. We have a standard evidence request process—NDA first, then we provide the report. I’ll send the steps after this call.

[12:30] Ben Crawford: Quick one on SSO: can you enforce that local passwords are disabled?

[12:35] Colin O'Donnell: In SSO-required mode, we can require SAML for standard users. Break-glass is the exception, controlled by you. So the right language is “SSO can be enforced for all users except customer-controlled break-glass admin.”

[12:55] Priya Nair: Works.

[12:58] Mei Tan: Network: any outbound connectivity required from the inference cluster?

[13:03] Colin O'Donnell: For pure inference, no outbound required besides what you configure for model artifact access (if you’re pulling model weights from your registry) and for any export endpoints you choose (like S3). We can run with restricted egress. If you want zero egress, we can do that, but it changes how updates and monitoring work. We’ll document the tradeoffs.

[13:30] Priya Nair: For our regulated environment, outbound is heavily restricted. So we’ll probably do private endpoints and no general internet.

[13:39] Colin O'Donnell: That’s common.

[13:41] Ben Crawford: Encryption—are you using TLS end-to-end? And at rest, KMS?

[13:47] Colin O'Donnell: In transit: TLS for API calls; internal service-to-service can be TLS depending on your service mesh and deployment. At rest: data stored by the system—configs, logs, etc.—is encrypted at rest, typically via your cloud provider primitives. If you use AWS, it’s KMS-backed. For keys: in Private, keys are in your account.

[14:15] Priya Nair: Any HSM support?

[14:18] Colin O'Donnell: If you use KMS with CloudHSM-backed keys, that’s on the AWS side. Redwood integrates at the platform layer; we don’t ship our own HSM.

[14:30] Marcus Lin: Again, for RFP language: “supports customer-managed encryption keys via cloud KMS in Private deployments.” Keep it simple.

[14:41] Mei Tan: Logging again—do you have request IDs so we can correlate application logs with your audit logs?

[14:47] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, we return request IDs, and they appear in operational logs and sometimes audit trails depending on event type. For token usage dashboards, you can correlate at the route/model level.

[15:05] Priya Nair: We also have a requirement around “administrative access to production” for vendors. Do Redwood employees have access into our Private cluster?

[15:14] Colin O'Donnell: Default is no. It’s your environment. If you choose a managed option where Redwood assists, access is time-bound and approved, and should be auditable through your IAM + our logs. For the RFP, the safest default statement is “no standing access; any support access is customer-authorized and logged.”

[15:38] Alyssa Grant: That will help.

[15:41] Lukas Meyer: One more thing I’m hearing across deals: data residency. You’re US-only, correct?

[15:47] Priya Nair: For Summit, yes, US. But we still require the statement that data stays in our account.

[15:53] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, the statement is straightforward: customer content is processed and stored within your environment, subject to your configuration.

[16:04] Ben Crawford: Okay.

[16:06] (crosstalk)

[16:07] Mei Tan: Sorry—what about system metrics? Do they ever get sent back to you?

[16:12] Colin O'Donnell: Optional. Some customers allow anonymized operational metrics to Redwood for support—like CPU/GPU utilization, error rates. We can configure that off, or route it through your own observability stack. We’ll put that in the shared responsibility notes.

[16:35] Priya Nair: Please include it, because “telemetry” triggers questions for us.

[16:39] Marcus Lin: We can also include a line that Redwood does not require customer prompt content for support troubleshooting; we can work off metrics and customer-provided samples when needed.

[16:52] Priya Nair: Great.

[16:54] Lukas Meyer: Do you want to spend the rest of the time on audit log specifics? Or RBAC mapping to your roles.

[17:00] Ben Crawford: RBAC mapping. We have three personas: platform admins, app developers, and auditors.

[17:06] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Platform admins: can manage deployment config, endpoints, and system settings. App developers: can create API keys for their apps (if allowed), view usage/performance dashboards, but not change org-wide security settings. Auditors: read-only access to audit logs and config state. That maps cleanly.

[17:34] Priya Nair: Can “auditor” see prompts?

[17:37] Colin O'Donnell: No, not unless you build separate logging in your app that captures prompts. Redwood’s audit logs are control-plane.

[17:46] Mei Tan: That’s preferred.

[17:48] Ben Crawford: User lifecycle—if someone is removed from Okta, do they lose access immediately?

[17:54] Colin O'Donnell: With SAML, access is tied to IdP authentication. With SCIM, deprovisioning happens when SCIM pushes the update; typical is near real-time but depends on IdP. For RFP: “deprovisioning supported via SCIM; access can be revoked via IdP; API keys should be rotated.”

[18:20] Priya Nair: Make sure you mention key rotation; that’s another checkbox.

[18:24] Colin O'Donnell: Yep.

[18:26] Marcus Lin: On audit logs: we log API key creation and revocation events.

[18:32] Ben Crawford: And failed logins?

[18:34] Marcus Lin: Yes, authentication failures are captured as security events.

[18:39] Priya Nair: Good.

[18:41] Lukas Meyer: I’m going to ask a potentially annoying question: do you need us to answer HIPAA or PCI sections? Or is it general security.

[18:49] Alyssa Grant: General security plus SOC 2 is the main thing. We do have a “HIPAA applicable?” question, but we can mark N/A. We’re not doing PHI in this workload.

[19:01] Lukas Meyer: Great. We won’t position a BAA or anything unless requested.

[19:06] Colin O'Donnell: Back to audit log export—Mei, you mentioned Splunk HEC. If you can share whether you prefer direct HEC or S3-based forwarding, we’ll tailor guidance.

[19:18] Mei Tan: Likely S3 + our existing forwarder.

[19:22] Colin O'Donnell: Perfect.

[19:24] Priya Nair: One more control: vulnerability management. Do you do pen tests? Frequency?

[19:30] Marcus Lin: We do regular vulnerability scanning and periodic third-party penetration testing. For exact frequency, we provide approved language rather than making up a number on a call. The SOC 2 report and/or pen test attestation under NDA is the right channel.

[19:50] Priya Nair: That’s fine—just don’t say “annual” if it’s not always annual.

[19:54] Marcus Lin: Exactly.

[19:56] Colin O'Donnell: Any other must-have RFP items we haven’t hit?

[20:00] Alyssa Grant: Data retention. Specifically: “How long do you retain customer data?”

[20:06] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, customer content retention is governed by you because content stays in your environment. For Hosted/Dedicated, there are defined retention policies for logs and operational metadata; but since you’re evaluating Private, the correct answer is: “customer controls retention; Redwood does not retain prompts/outputs outside the customer environment by default.”

[20:28] Priya Nair: Perfect.

[20:30] Ben Crawford: Do you have audit log retention controls in-product or is it just “ship it out”?

[20:36] Colin O'Donnell: Both. There’s in-product retention for the console view, and export for long-term retention. In your scenario, we expect export to be the durable source.

[20:49] Mei Tan: Okay.

[20:51] (brief silence)

[20:52] Lukas Meyer: We’re at the 20-minute mark in the transcript; we can keep going on any open questions.

[20:57] Priya Nair: I want to understand the boundary between “audit log” and “application log.” Our auditors sometimes ask for “every API call log.”

[21:06] Colin O'Donnell: Great point. We do have request logs/metrics that can include endpoint name, timestamp, latency, token counts, status code. We do not frame that as “audit logs” because it’s not about admin actions. And we don’t recommend including payloads. If your auditors need per-request traceability, we can support correlation IDs, and you can capture request logs at your ingress layer.

[21:35] Priya Nair: That’s the answer I need. We’ll implement at ingress.

[21:39] Marcus Lin: And we’ll provide written language: “Redwood provides admin audit events; request-level telemetry is available; payload logging is customer-controlled.”

[21:51] Ben Crawford: Are the audit logs accessible via API? We have an internal GRC tool that pulls.

[21:56] Colin O'Donnell: Yes, there’s an API for audit events export/pull, subject to permissions.

[22:04] Mei Tan: Rate limits?

[22:06] Colin O'Donnell: There are reasonable rate limits; for bulk export we recommend S3 or scheduled pulls. We can tune if needed, but again, won’t promise unlimited.

[22:20] Priya Nair: Okay.

[22:22] Lukas Meyer: Competitors—just for context, are you comparing this to Bedrock private endpoints or Azure OpenAI in your tenant?

[22:31] Priya Nair: We looked at Bedrock. The control story is okay but the model selection and performance tuning isn’t great for our workloads. Azure we already use for other stuff but same issue.

[22:44] Colin O'Donnell: Makes sense.

[22:46] Mei Tan: Also Together is in the mix, but Private deployment was less mature.

[22:50] Lukas Meyer: Helpful.

[22:52] Colin O'Donnell: Anything else on SSO? Ben, you asked about Okta vs Azure AD.

[22:58] Ben Crawford: It’s Okta for workforce. We might use Azure AD for some app-to-app. But for admin console, Okta.

[23:08] Colin O'Donnell: Great—Okta SAML and SCIM is a standard setup.

[23:13] Priya Nair: Can you do device posture? Like “only managed devices.”

[23:19] Colin O'Donnell: That’s enforced at your IdP / access proxy layer. Redwood doesn’t have a device agent. So for RFP: “supported via IdP conditional access.”

[23:33] Priya Nair: Great.

[23:35] Marcus Lin: And network-level restrictions can also help: private routing, IP allowlists, etc.

[23:41] Mei Tan: Yep.

[23:43] (Fireflies note: audio overlap)

[23:45] Priya Nair: Last thing—do you have a standard “shared responsibility model” write-up? We always attach those.

[23:52] Colin O'Donnell: We have internal docs; we’ll send a customer-facing version that’s RFP friendly.

[24:00] Lukas Meyer: We’ll make sure it’s the approved one.

[24:03] Colin O'Donnell: Cool. I think we covered the core. Marcus, anything you want to add on controls language?

[24:10] Marcus Lin: Only that we’ll provide approved statements for: SOC 2 availability under NDA, vulnerability management without stating an incorrect cadence, and audit log integrity guidance (S3 Object Lock, SIEM immutability) without claiming “cryptographic signing.”

[24:30] Priya Nair: Thank you. That’s exactly what we need.

[24:34] Lukas Meyer: Before we wrap, recap next steps: we’ll send the Private deployment appendix and RBAC summary, plus audit log export/retention language. You’ll confirm IdP details (Okta) and whether SCIM is day-one, and share Splunk forwarding preference.

[24:53] Ben Crawford: Yep.

[24:54] Mei Tan: Yep, S3 forwarding.

[24:56] Alyssa Grant: And send the SOC 2 request instructions.

[25:00] Lukas Meyer: Will do today.

[25:02] Colin O'Donnell: Great. Thanks all.

[25:05] (Meeting ended in transcript; remainder of time was post-call note capture / Fireflies processing)
