title: Acme Bank - Redwood Private VPC technical deep dive (endpoints, logging, KMS, connectivity)
preview:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: Acme Bank - Redwood Private VPC technical deep dive (endpoints, logging, KMS, connectivity)
Date: 2025-06-12
Start time: 2:00 PM ET
Duration: 62 minutes

Attendees
- Markus Klein (Redwood Inference)
- Colin O'Donnell (Redwood Inference)
- Victor Alvarez (Redwood Inference)
- Marcus Lin (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Shah (Acme Bank)
- Dan Miller (Acme Bank)
- Elena Kovacs (Acme Bank)
- Tom Reyes (Acme Bank)

----
Auto-generated Summary (may be incomplete)
- The group reviewed Redwood Private’s networking prerequisites for an AWS VPC deployment, focusing on minimizing internet egress via VPC endpoints and clarifying DNS behavior.
- Acme asked for defaults around VPC Flow Logs, audit logging, and SIEM export, plus how Redwood supports customer-managed KMS keys.
- Redwood outlined the alpha Terraform module approach: opinionated defaults with controlled overrides, plus installer preflight checks for missing endpoints (notably ECR).
- Connectivity options (no egress / proxy-only / Direct Connect) were discussed along with extension points for TGW and shared-services DNS.

Topics
- Required VPC endpoints and private DNS considerations (ECR, S3, STS, CloudWatch Logs, KMS)
- NAT vs no-NAT designs, proxy egress constraints
- Audit logging, flow logs, destinations and retention
- IAM roles / permission boundaries for installer execution
- KMS key ownership and key policy expectations
- Design partner pilot next steps

Action items
- Redwood (Markus) to send endpoint list + DNS notes, including “must-have” vs “nice-to-have” endpoints for the installer.
- Redwood (Victor) to send a reference diagram for a “no-internet egress” VPC pattern using interface endpoints + gateway endpoint for S3.
- Redwood (Marcus) to send security posture notes: default logging, encryption, and how customers can route logs to SIEM.
- Acme (Dan) to confirm if private DNS can be enabled for interface endpoints in their shared-services VPC, and how they handle split-horizon DNS.
- Acme (Tom) to share their standard KMS CMK policy template constraints (who can administer vs use, rotation, deletion protections).

Questions
- Does Redwood require outbound internet for image pulls or package downloads? (Answer: designed to work with VPC endpoints; NAT not strictly required if endpoints/DNS are correct.)
- Can flow logs go to customer-owned S3 and be shipped to Splunk? (Answer: yes; modules support S3/CloudWatch; shipping is an integration pattern.)
- What is the minimal IAM needed for the installer execution role? (Answer: Redwood provides a scaffold with permission boundary; final can be constrained further.)

----
Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for making time. This is Markus from Redwood. The goal today is basically to go a layer deeper on the VPC pieces for Redwood Private, especially around endpoints, logging, and KMS. We’ve got Victor who’s been building the network modules and Marcus from security.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:19] Dan Miller: Yep.

[00:20] Markus Klein: Quick roll call, Priya you’re cloud security, Dan network, Elena platform, Tom IAM/governance. Anything else we should know? Any other stakeholders missing that will block? Like, the DNS team sometimes.

[00:33] Elena Kovacs: DNS is kind of under shared services. I can pull them in later if needed, but we’ll start.

[00:39] Markus Klein: Great. Colin is also here, he’s been on the discovery side with you. Colin, anything to add?

[00:45] Colin O'Donnell: Nope, just that we keep seeing the same failure modes in private pilots, so we want to be explicit on endpoints + DNS so your POC doesn’t hit the potholes.

[00:56] Dan Miller: Yeah, we read that note you sent about ECR endpoints being a common thing.

[01:01] Markus Klein: Exactly. Maybe we start there. Victor, can you outline the “must-have” endpoints for a locked-down VPC?

[01:09] Victor Alvarez: Sure. So for a typical EKS-based private deployment where there’s no public egress, we need interface endpoints for at least ECR API and ECR DKR. People sometimes only add one. If you only add the API endpoint, image pulls still fail. Then STS is usually required for role assumption flows. CloudWatch Logs if you’re shipping node and app logs there. KMS if you’re using AWS KMS and need the nodes or control plane to talk to it.

[01:39] Victor Alvarez: And then S3 is special: you typically use a gateway endpoint for S3 rather than interface, because it’s cheaper and works well for artifact pulls. Some customers also add EC2 endpoint, autoscaling endpoint, and EKS endpoint depending on how they bootstrap and what the cluster needs.

[01:56] Priya Shah: When you say “need,” is that for the Redwood software itself, or just for EKS to function?

[02:02] Victor Alvarez: Both. The installer will pull container images, so it’s ECR. The runtime and the surrounding services will emit logs/metrics depending on your chosen sink. EKS itself can be set up as private endpoint, but then your admin access path matters.

[02:18] Elena Kovacs: We do private EKS endpoints by default. We access via a jump host in a management subnet.

[02:23] Dan Miller: And we have no direct internet egress from application subnets. There is a proxy path in shared services, but we try to avoid it.

[02:33] Markus Klein: That aligns with what we see in regulated environments. The question we typically get is: can Redwood operate without NAT gateways at all.

[02:41] Priya Shah: Yeah, that’s my big one. “No NAT” is basically our standard for these workloads.

[02:46] Victor Alvarez: In most cases, yes. The alpha modules assume a NAT-per-AZ pattern as a default because it’s the broadest compatibility, but there’s a supported toggle to run “private-only” with endpoints. The gotcha is DNS: interface endpoints rely on private DNS to be easy.

[03:06] Dan Miller: Private DNS for endpoints is sometimes controversial internally. We’ve got shared services VPC doing resolver rules to on-prem. If we turn on private DNS on endpoints in the workload VPC, will it override our forwarding?

[03:18] Victor Alvarez: It can, depending on the domain. For example, enabling private DNS for ecr.* can cause resolution inside the VPC to go to the endpoint, which is what you want, but it’s different from forwarding to on-prem. The module supports enabling private DNS per endpoint, not a global “all on.”

[03:36] Elena Kovacs: We have split-horizon for a few zones. Not for AWS service zones, obviously, but we have custom zones.

[03:44] Colin O'Donnell: The common failure mode we saw in another pilot was: endpoints exist, but private DNS was off, and then the nodes tried to resolve public ECR and obviously couldn’t reach it.

[03:54] Dan Miller: Right.

[03:56] Markus Klein: Priya, would your policy allow private DNS for AWS service endpoints? Some banks are okay with it because it stays inside VPC.

[04:05] Priya Shah: We can allow it if we can scope it. If it’s per endpoint and not messing with our internal zones, it’s fine. But we’ll need it documented.

[04:16] Markus Klein: Yup. We have docs in the alpha guide that call out: ECR endpoints plus private DNS are effectively mandatory if no NAT.

[04:26] Tom Reyes: On IAM, when you say STS endpoint required, is that because the nodes assume roles? Or the installer assumes roles? Both?

[04:33] Victor Alvarez: Potentially both. The installer role will call AWS APIs. The cluster nodes commonly call STS for IRSA flows depending on your setup. If you don’t have STS endpoint and no NAT, you’ll see weird auth failures.

[04:50] Marcus Lin: And from security perspective, we like STS endpoint anyway because it keeps token vending inside the VPC boundary versus going out.

[04:58] Priya Shah: Ok.

[05:00] Markus Klein: Switching to logging. Marcus, can you cover what we do by default for audit logs and VPC flow logs?

[05:06] Marcus Lin: Sure. So, two buckets: AWS-level logging and Redwood application-level audit/ops logs. AWS-level: we recommend VPC Flow Logs enabled for the VPC and subnets that carry cluster traffic. The module supports destination to CloudWatch Logs or to S3. In regulated environments, S3 is common because it’s easy to set retention/immutability with object lock, plus export to SIEM.

[05:34] Marcus Lin: We also recommend CloudTrail in the account, but that’s usually an org-level thing you already have. On Redwood side, the installer and services emit structured logs. We don’t force them to CloudWatch; you can ship from your collectors to Splunk or whatever. But we do want an audit trail of administrative actions, like changes to config, upgrades, access attempts.

[05:57] Priya Shah: We need all admin actions retained one year minimum. Flow logs we keep 90 days hot, one year cold. Can you do that?

[06:06] Marcus Lin: Yes, if you choose S3 as destination, lifecycle policies can do that. If CloudWatch, retention can be set too, but cost can blow up.

[06:16] Dan Miller: Do the modules create the S3 bucket? Because we have to use pre-approved buckets with specific policies.

[06:22] Victor Alvarez: The module can either create or reference an existing bucket. Default in alpha is “customer-owned destination,” meaning you can pass in bucket ARN. We try not to force creation because of exactly this.

[06:36] Elena Kovacs: For CloudWatch Logs, do you create the log group? Again, we have naming and encryption requirements.

[06:41] Victor Alvarez: Same pattern. We can create, but you can also pass in. And we support KMS encryption for log groups.

[06:49] Tom Reyes: That ties to KMS. Let’s talk keys. We require customer-managed CMKs, no AWS-managed keys for most things.

[06:57] Markus Klein: Yup. This is the KMS segment.

[07:01] Marcus Lin: We support customer-managed KMS keys for at-rest encryption: EBS volumes, S3 buckets for logs, CloudWatch log groups, secrets storage. The main thing we need is key policy that allows the right principals to use Encrypt/Decrypt and GenerateDataKey. We can share a template.

[07:21] Tom Reyes: Our policy has a strict separation: key admins can’t use keys; workloads can’t administer. Also rotation on.

[07:28] Marcus Lin: That’s fine. Our docs assume that. The module outputs the IAM role ARNs that need key usage grants. We generally recommend you manage the key and pass key ARN into module.

[07:43] Priya Shah: Do you need KMS endpoint? You mentioned it earlier.

[07:46] Victor Alvarez: If you’re fully private with no NAT, yes. Because when instances call KMS APIs, it’s an AWS API endpoint. So you need the interface endpoint for KMS.

[07:58] Dan Miller: Okay, and CloudWatch Logs endpoint too if logs go to CloudWatch.

[08:02] Victor Alvarez: Correct.

[08:04] Elena Kovacs: What about ECR + S3 only. If we send logs to S3 and don’t use CloudWatch, do we still need CloudWatch endpoints?

[08:13] Victor Alvarez: Not strictly. But EKS itself sometimes uses CloudWatch for control plane logs if enabled. If you disable it, you can avoid.

[08:22] Markus Klein: The alpha “minimal endpoint set” we recommend for no-NAT is usually: ECR API, ECR DKR, STS, S3 gateway. Then add KMS if you use CMKs and add CloudWatch if you use it. Plus whatever your DNS design needs.

[08:41] Dan Miller: DNS design is actually where we get stuck. We have TGW hub, and DNS is centralized. If we create endpoints in the workload VPC but resolution is being forwarded to centralized resolvers, do we need Route 53 resolver endpoints?

[08:56] Victor Alvarez: Possibly. If your VPC uses the default AmazonProvidedDNS and the VPC has interface endpoints with private DNS, resolution inside that VPC should work without extra resolver endpoints. But if you have conditional forwarding that overrides, it gets tricky. A pattern is: keep AWS service domains resolved locally, and only forward internal corp domains.

[09:19] Dan Miller: We do the opposite sometimes because of policy. Like, “all DNS through central.” But maybe we can carve out AWS zones.

[09:28] Priya Shah: We can explore. It’s not impossible.

[09:31] Markus Klein: This is exactly why we’re building the “validated defaults” in Terraform. We’ll document supported patterns and where we say “we can’t guarantee.”

[09:40] Colin O'Donnell: Also, installer preflight checks: we now detect if endpoints exist and if private DNS is off, we print a warning that’s pretty explicit. It’s not perfect but it reduces time-to-fix.

[09:53] Elena Kovacs: That’s good. We hate black-box failures.

[09:57] Markus Klein: Connectivity constraints next. Dan, you mentioned TGW hub. Do you have Direct Connect into the hub VPC?

[10:04] Dan Miller: Yes. DX comes into shared services, then TGW to workload VPCs. No site-to-site VPN in most cases.

[10:13] Markus Klein: Any requirement for Redwood to connect back to Redwood cloud? Like control plane or updates?

[10:18] Priya Shah: For private deployment, no outbound to vendor clouds. That’s a hard requirement. If there is any “phone home” it’s a non-starter.

[10:27] Markus Klein: Understood. Redwood Private is designed to run with a private control plane in your VPC / on-prem. For alpha, we do have some optional support channels and telemetry that can be disabled. The default for regulated is off, and we can document what endpoints would be used if you opted in.

[10:45] Marcus Lin: From security perspective we treat outbound telemetry as optional, and we’ll provide a statement of what is collected if enabled. But again, can be off.

[10:55] Tom Reyes: On updates, how do you deliver patches? If no outbound internet, how do you get images?

[11:01] Colin O'Donnell: Great question. Typically we either mirror images into your ECR (your account) or you pull from a pre-approved registry path reachable via endpoints. For strict environments, we provide an artifact bundle or a process to sync images through your controlled pipeline.

[11:20] Elena Kovacs: We can do image mirroring. We have a pipeline that pulls from vendor, scans, then pushes into our ECR.

[11:27] Markus Klein: Perfect. Then endpoints for ECR are still needed inside the workload VPC.

[11:34] Priya Shah: Does the installer require access to GitHub or something for charts?

[11:39] Colin O'Donnell: Not if you use the packaged installer flow. If you use Helm directly and pull charts from public, that would need egress. But in private deployments we do offline-friendly packaging.

[11:54] Dan Miller: Good.

[11:56] Markus Klein: Let’s talk IAM boundaries because that’s another question you asked in email. Tom, what’s your standard model? Do you allow vendor-provided roles?

[12:05] Tom Reyes: We don’t allow “vendor creates admin roles.” We can allow a scoped role with permission boundary, and we need explicit list of actions.

[12:14] Marcus Lin: That’s aligned to what we’re doing in the Terraform modules. We create an installer execution role scaffold with a permission boundary. The boundary is a ceiling; then inline policy is the minimum set. You can tighten further.

[12:32] Tom Reyes: Can we pre-create the role and just give you ARN? We don’t want modules creating IAM.

[12:38] Victor Alvarez: Yes. Modules support “create_iam = false” and you pass role ARN. We still output the required policy document so your team can attach it.

[12:49] Elena Kovacs: That’s important for our internal process.

[12:52] Markus Klein: On audit trails for IAM actions, we assume you have CloudTrail. But do you need application audit logs to go to SIEM?

[13:00] Priya Shah: Yes. We are Splunk-heavy. Ideally logs land in S3 then we have forwarders.

[13:07] Marcus Lin: Great. For VPC Flow Logs, S3 is easy. For application logs, it depends on your logging stack in EKS. Most customers use Fluent Bit or similar to ship to S3, Kinesis, or Splunk HEC. Our requirement is: logs must be accessible for incident response and compliance. We’ll provide a recommended config but we don’t force a specific SIEM.

[13:31] Dan Miller: For flow logs: do you turn on “all traffic” or only rejects?

[13:36] Victor Alvarez: Default in alpha is “REJECT” plus “ALL” optionally. We got feedback that “ALL” is expensive but helpful during pilots. We can set it to REJECT by default and have a variable.

[13:51] Priya Shah: In prod we do REJECT plus sampling. But POC, we can do more.

[13:56] Markus Klein: Makes sense.

[14:00] Elena Kovacs: One question: do you need VPC endpoints for SSM? We use SSM Session Manager, no bastions.

[14:07] Victor Alvarez: If you want SSM in private subnets with no NAT, yes you’d need endpoints for SSM, EC2 messages, SSM messages. But that’s your admin plane, not Redwood-specific. Our modules don’t force it but we can document as an extension.

[14:25] Dan Miller: Okay.

[14:27] Markus Klein: Let’s ground this in your topology. Dan, do you have a rough CIDR plan for the pilot VPC?

[14:33] Dan Miller: Likely a /20 in us-east-1, three AZs. Private subnets only. We can have a small management subnet if required, but prefer not.

[14:44] Victor Alvarez: /20 is fine. Our reference uses three AZs with private subnets per AZ and optional public subnets if NAT gateways are used. For no-NAT, you can omit public.

[14:57] Elena Kovacs: EKS requires something for load balancers. If it’s internal, it can still be in private subnets.

[15:03] Victor Alvarez: Correct. For internal NLB/ALB, private subnets are okay as long as tags are right.

[15:11] Priya Shah: Ingress: we’ll do internal only. No public endpoints.

[15:16] Markus Klein: That’s supported. Redwood Private is typically internal ingress with your own connectivity controls.

[15:22] Colin O'Donnell: We’ll want to confirm the installer expectations: DNS, TLS, internal cert chain. But we can cover that separately.

[15:31] Elena Kovacs: We use ACM Private CA for internal certs.

[15:35] Marcus Lin: Great, that works.

[15:38] Markus Klein: On KMS again, Tom: do you require keys in a separate account or same account?

[15:44] Tom Reyes: Ideally a security account, but for pilot we can do same account with guardrails.

[15:51] Marcus Lin: That’s fine. Cross-account KMS is doable but adds complexity; for alpha we can keep it simple.

[16:02] Dan Miller: Another constraint: we’re required to log DNS queries in some environments. Do you support Route 53 Resolver query logs?

[16:10] Victor Alvarez: If you’re using Route 53 Resolver, yes, query logs can be enabled and delivered to CloudWatch or S3. Our alpha modules don’t automatically set that up yet, but we have it on the roadmap and can provide a pattern.

[16:25] Priya Shah: We’d want it for the pilot if possible.

[16:28] Markus Klein: We can do it as an add-on pattern even if not in the core module. The key is: we don’t want alpha to be unmaintainable, but we can help in pilot.

[16:39] Elena Kovacs: That’s acceptable. For pilot we can do a little custom.

[16:45] Markus Klein: Ok. What about data residency: us-east-1 only?

[16:49] Priya Shah: Yes, for now.

[16:52] Markus Klein: Great.

[16:54] Dan Miller: On endpoints, do you require “Interface endpoints in every subnet” or can we centralize them?

[17:01] Victor Alvarez: Technically interface endpoints are ENIs in specific subnets. Best practice is put them in each AZ where you have workloads to avoid cross-AZ traffic and to keep it resilient. We usually create one subnet per AZ for endpoints, or reuse private subnets, but security groups matter.

[17:20] Dan Miller: We’d prefer dedicated endpoint subnets, but that’s another CIDR.

[17:24] Victor Alvarez: Yeah. Our module supports either. Default is using the private subnets with a locked-down endpoint SG.

[17:33] Priya Shah: Endpoint SG: can it be strict, like only 443 from node security group?

[17:38] Victor Alvarez: Yes. That’s part of the opinionated security group patterns. We try to avoid 0.0.0.0/0 internal sprawl.

[17:46] Marcus Lin: And we also recommend NACLs sparingly because they cause debug pain, but if you have them, we can work within them.

[17:55] Dan Miller: We have NACL baselines. We can share.

[17:58] Markus Klein: Perfect.

[18:01] Elena Kovacs: This might be dumb: for S3 gateway endpoint, does it work with KMS-encrypted buckets?

[18:06] Victor Alvarez: Yes, gateway endpoint is just routing. KMS is separate call path, so you still need KMS endpoint if no NAT.

[18:15] Elena Kovacs: Got it.

[18:17] Colin O'Donnell: Another “gotcha”: if you’re mirroring images into ECR, your EKS nodes will still talk to ECR API/DKR. So endpoints must exist and DNS must resolve to them.

[18:28] Dan Miller: Yep.

[18:30] Markus Klein: Let’s talk pilot. We’re looking at you as a design partner for the alpha modules. That means: you’d use our Terraform modules as a baseline, we’d do weekly check-ins, and we’d incorporate feedback into the defaults/extension points.

[18:45] Priya Shah: What’s the timeline? We’re trying to do an internal proof by end of July.

[18:50] Markus Klein: That’s feasible. Typically: 1–2 weeks to set up infra with Terraform, then run installer preflights, then 1–2 weeks to validate logging, access, and run a small workload. We’ll need a sandbox account.

[19:05] Elena Kovacs: We can use our dev security account.

[19:09] Tom Reyes: We still need approvals for IAM and KMS.

[19:12] Markus Klein: Understood. To speed that, we’ll send the exact IAM policy and boundary docs.

[19:18] Marcus Lin: And we can provide a short “security defaults” memo: what logs are enabled, what’s encrypted, what doesn’t leave the VPC.

[19:27] Priya Shah: That will help.

[19:30] Dan Miller: For connectivity, we want to avoid provisioning NAT gateways. Are you okay if we start with endpoints only?

[19:37] Victor Alvarez: Yes. We’ll want to validate the endpoints list and DNS early. The integration test harness we have internally is basically the same: stand up VPC, endpoints, flow logs, then verify outputs.

[19:51] Colin O'Donnell: Also we can run installer preflight without installing everything, just to validate “network prerequisites” quickly.

[20:00] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[20:02] Markus Klein: Any other concerns? Competitor-wise, are you comparing to Bedrock private endpoints or Azure OpenAI private link? Just so we understand the bar.

[20:12] Priya Shah: We’re looking at multiple. Bedrock is easy in AWS but model selection and control is… limited. Azure is complicated because we’re not all-in on Azure.

[20:23] Markus Klein: Understood.

[20:25] Dan Miller: The bar is: private connectivity and auditability. If we can’t prove no egress and we can’t get logs into Splunk, it’s dead.

[20:34] Markus Klein: Clear.

[20:36] Marcus Lin: On “no egress,” we can help you validate with flow logs and route tables. Also we can provide a checklist: no IGW, no NAT, only endpoints, and restrict SG egress to endpoints.

[20:51] Priya Shah: That checklist is what I want.

[20:54] Markus Klein: Great.

[20:56] Elena Kovacs: One more: do you need a VPC endpoint for Secrets Manager?

[21:00] Victor Alvarez: If you use Secrets Manager and have no NAT, yes. But depending on how secrets are stored. Many clusters do.

[21:10] Colin O'Donnell: For Redwood Private, we can integrate with Secrets Manager or use Kubernetes secrets with envelope encryption; either way there’s a KMS aspect.

[21:21] Elena Kovacs: Ok, we’ll plan for Secrets Manager endpoint too.

[21:25] Dan Miller: The endpoints list is growing.

[21:27] Victor Alvarez: Yeah, that’s why we split into “required” and “recommended.” Required for the common path: ECR, STS, S3. Recommended: KMS, Logs, Secrets Manager, EC2 depending. We’ll send the matrix.

[21:44] Markus Klein: Okay, we’re at about halfway. Let’s do a quick recap and make sure we’re aligned.

[21:50] Markus Klein: What I’m hearing: Acme wants a no-NAT pilot VPC in us-east-1 with private EKS endpoint, internal ingress only, interface endpoints with private DNS allowed for AWS service domains, logs to S3 with 1-year retention, CMK everywhere, and IAM roles pre-created with boundaries.

[22:10] Priya Shah: Correct.

[22:12] Dan Miller: Yeah.

[22:13] Tom Reyes: Yes.

[22:15] Markus Klein: Great. Next, we’ll send: endpoint list + DNS notes, logging defaults doc, IAM boundary doc, and a minimal Terraform example. Then you’ll confirm DNS policy and KMS policy constraints.

[22:28] Elena Kovacs: Works.

[22:30] Markus Klein: Any final questions before we drop?

[22:33] Priya Shah: Can you provide a statement about where audit logs live? Like, are they only in the cluster or can we guarantee export?

[22:41] Marcus Lin: We can guarantee that audit events are emitted. Export depends on your sink. In the pilot we’ll configure export to your S3/Splunk path and validate delivery.

[22:53] Priya Shah: Ok.

[22:55] Dan Miller: The installer preflight - does it check route tables too? Or just endpoints.

[23:01] Colin O'Donnell: Right now it checks endpoints presence and DNS resolution for some services (ECR mainly). Route table validation is on the roadmap; we can also provide a manual checklist.

[23:15] Dan Miller: Fine.

[23:16] Markus Klein: Perfect. We’ll follow up with a calendar invite for a 90-minute working session on topology next week. Thanks all.

[23:25] Priya Shah: Thanks.

[23:26] Elena Kovacs: Thank you.

[23:27] Dan Miller: Bye.

[23:28] Tom Reyes: Bye.

----
Note: Transcript may contain errors due to automated transcription (acronyms, speaker attribution, and punctuation).
text:
Fireflies.ai - Meeting Transcript

Title: Acme Bank - Redwood Private VPC technical deep dive (endpoints, logging, KMS, connectivity)
Date: 2025-06-12
Start time: 2:00 PM ET
Duration: 62 minutes

Attendees
- Markus Klein (Redwood Inference)
- Colin O'Donnell (Redwood Inference)
- Victor Alvarez (Redwood Inference)
- Marcus Lin (Redwood Inference)
- Priya Shah (Acme Bank)
- Dan Miller (Acme Bank)
- Elena Kovacs (Acme Bank)
- Tom Reyes (Acme Bank)

----
Auto-generated Summary (may be incomplete)
- The group reviewed Redwood Private’s networking prerequisites for an AWS VPC deployment, focusing on minimizing internet egress via VPC endpoints and clarifying DNS behavior.
- Acme asked for defaults around VPC Flow Logs, audit logging, and SIEM export, plus how Redwood supports customer-managed KMS keys.
- Redwood outlined the alpha Terraform module approach: opinionated defaults with controlled overrides, plus installer preflight checks for missing endpoints (notably ECR).
- Connectivity options (no egress / proxy-only / Direct Connect) were discussed along with extension points for TGW and shared-services DNS.

Topics
- Required VPC endpoints and private DNS considerations (ECR, S3, STS, CloudWatch Logs, KMS)
- NAT vs no-NAT designs, proxy egress constraints
- Audit logging, flow logs, destinations and retention
- IAM roles / permission boundaries for installer execution
- KMS key ownership and key policy expectations
- Design partner pilot next steps

Action items
- Redwood (Markus) to send endpoint list + DNS notes, including “must-have” vs “nice-to-have” endpoints for the installer.
- Redwood (Victor) to send a reference diagram for a “no-internet egress” VPC pattern using interface endpoints + gateway endpoint for S3.
- Redwood (Marcus) to send security posture notes: default logging, encryption, and how customers can route logs to SIEM.
- Acme (Dan) to confirm if private DNS can be enabled for interface endpoints in their shared-services VPC, and how they handle split-horizon DNS.
- Acme (Tom) to share their standard KMS CMK policy template constraints (who can administer vs use, rotation, deletion protections).

Questions
- Does Redwood require outbound internet for image pulls or package downloads? (Answer: designed to work with VPC endpoints; NAT not strictly required if endpoints/DNS are correct.)
- Can flow logs go to customer-owned S3 and be shipped to Splunk? (Answer: yes; modules support S3/CloudWatch; shipping is an integration pattern.)
- What is the minimal IAM needed for the installer execution role? (Answer: Redwood provides a scaffold with permission boundary; final can be constrained further.)

----
Transcript

[00:00] Markus Klein: Alright, cool. Thanks everyone for making time. This is Markus from Redwood. The goal today is basically to go a layer deeper on the VPC pieces for Redwood Private, especially around endpoints, logging, and KMS. We’ve got Victor who’s been building the network modules and Marcus from security.

[00:18] Priya Shah: Sounds good.

[00:19] Dan Miller: Yep.

[00:20] Markus Klein: Quick roll call, Priya you’re cloud security, Dan network, Elena platform, Tom IAM/governance. Anything else we should know? Any other stakeholders missing that will block? Like, the DNS team sometimes.

[00:33] Elena Kovacs: DNS is kind of under shared services. I can pull them in later if needed, but we’ll start.

[00:39] Markus Klein: Great. Colin is also here, he’s been on the discovery side with you. Colin, anything to add?

[00:45] Colin O'Donnell: Nope, just that we keep seeing the same failure modes in private pilots, so we want to be explicit on endpoints + DNS so your POC doesn’t hit the potholes.

[00:56] Dan Miller: Yeah, we read that note you sent about ECR endpoints being a common thing.

[01:01] Markus Klein: Exactly. Maybe we start there. Victor, can you outline the “must-have” endpoints for a locked-down VPC?

[01:09] Victor Alvarez: Sure. So for a typical EKS-based private deployment where there’s no public egress, we need interface endpoints for at least ECR API and ECR DKR. People sometimes only add one. If you only add the API endpoint, image pulls still fail. Then STS is usually required for role assumption flows. CloudWatch Logs if you’re shipping node and app logs there. KMS if you’re using AWS KMS and need the nodes or control plane to talk to it.

[01:39] Victor Alvarez: And then S3 is special: you typically use a gateway endpoint for S3 rather than interface, because it’s cheaper and works well for artifact pulls. Some customers also add EC2 endpoint, autoscaling endpoint, and EKS endpoint depending on how they bootstrap and what the cluster needs.

[01:56] Priya Shah: When you say “need,” is that for the Redwood software itself, or just for EKS to function?

[02:02] Victor Alvarez: Both. The installer will pull container images, so it’s ECR. The runtime and the surrounding services will emit logs/metrics depending on your chosen sink. EKS itself can be set up as private endpoint, but then your admin access path matters.

[02:18] Elena Kovacs: We do private EKS endpoints by default. We access via a jump host in a management subnet.

[02:23] Dan Miller: And we have no direct internet egress from application subnets. There is a proxy path in shared services, but we try to avoid it.

[02:33] Markus Klein: That aligns with what we see in regulated environments. The question we typically get is: can Redwood operate without NAT gateways at all.

[02:41] Priya Shah: Yeah, that’s my big one. “No NAT” is basically our standard for these workloads.

[02:46] Victor Alvarez: In most cases, yes. The alpha modules assume a NAT-per-AZ pattern as a default because it’s the broadest compatibility, but there’s a supported toggle to run “private-only” with endpoints. The gotcha is DNS: interface endpoints rely on private DNS to be easy.

[03:06] Dan Miller: Private DNS for endpoints is sometimes controversial internally. We’ve got shared services VPC doing resolver rules to on-prem. If we turn on private DNS on endpoints in the workload VPC, will it override our forwarding?

[03:18] Victor Alvarez: It can, depending on the domain. For example, enabling private DNS for ecr.* can cause resolution inside the VPC to go to the endpoint, which is what you want, but it’s different from forwarding to on-prem. The module supports enabling private DNS per endpoint, not a global “all on.”

[03:36] Elena Kovacs: We have split-horizon for a few zones. Not for AWS service zones, obviously, but we have custom zones.

[03:44] Colin O'Donnell: The common failure mode we saw in another pilot was: endpoints exist, but private DNS was off, and then the nodes tried to resolve public ECR and obviously couldn’t reach it.

[03:54] Dan Miller: Right.

[03:56] Markus Klein: Priya, would your policy allow private DNS for AWS service endpoints? Some banks are okay with it because it stays inside VPC.

[04:05] Priya Shah: We can allow it if we can scope it. If it’s per endpoint and not messing with our internal zones, it’s fine. But we’ll need it documented.

[04:16] Markus Klein: Yup. We have docs in the alpha guide that call out: ECR endpoints plus private DNS are effectively mandatory if no NAT.

[04:26] Tom Reyes: On IAM, when you say STS endpoint required, is that because the nodes assume roles? Or the installer assumes roles? Both?

[04:33] Victor Alvarez: Potentially both. The installer role will call AWS APIs. The cluster nodes commonly call STS for IRSA flows depending on your setup. If you don’t have STS endpoint and no NAT, you’ll see weird auth failures.

[04:50] Marcus Lin: And from security perspective, we like STS endpoint anyway because it keeps token vending inside the VPC boundary versus going out.

[04:58] Priya Shah: Ok.

[05:00] Markus Klein: Switching to logging. Marcus, can you cover what we do by default for audit logs and VPC flow logs?

[05:06] Marcus Lin: Sure. So, two buckets: AWS-level logging and Redwood application-level audit/ops logs. AWS-level: we recommend VPC Flow Logs enabled for the VPC and subnets that carry cluster traffic. The module supports destination to CloudWatch Logs or to S3. In regulated environments, S3 is common because it’s easy to set retention/immutability with object lock, plus export to SIEM.

[05:34] Marcus Lin: We also recommend CloudTrail in the account, but that’s usually an org-level thing you already have. On Redwood side, the installer and services emit structured logs. We don’t force them to CloudWatch; you can ship from your collectors to Splunk or whatever. But we do want an audit trail of administrative actions, like changes to config, upgrades, access attempts.

[05:57] Priya Shah: We need all admin actions retained one year minimum. Flow logs we keep 90 days hot, one year cold. Can you do that?

[06:06] Marcus Lin: Yes, if you choose S3 as destination, lifecycle policies can do that. If CloudWatch, retention can be set too, but cost can blow up.

[06:16] Dan Miller: Do the modules create the S3 bucket? Because we have to use pre-approved buckets with specific policies.

[06:22] Victor Alvarez: The module can either create or reference an existing bucket. Default in alpha is “customer-owned destination,” meaning you can pass in bucket ARN. We try not to force creation because of exactly this.

[06:36] Elena Kovacs: For CloudWatch Logs, do you create the log group? Again, we have naming and encryption requirements.

[06:41] Victor Alvarez: Same pattern. We can create, but you can also pass in. And we support KMS encryption for log groups.

[06:49] Tom Reyes: That ties to KMS. Let’s talk keys. We require customer-managed CMKs, no AWS-managed keys for most things.

[06:57] Markus Klein: Yup. This is the KMS segment.

[07:01] Marcus Lin: We support customer-managed KMS keys for at-rest encryption: EBS volumes, S3 buckets for logs, CloudWatch log groups, secrets storage. The main thing we need is key policy that allows the right principals to use Encrypt/Decrypt and GenerateDataKey. We can share a template.

[07:21] Tom Reyes: Our policy has a strict separation: key admins can’t use keys; workloads can’t administer. Also rotation on.

[07:28] Marcus Lin: That’s fine. Our docs assume that. The module outputs the IAM role ARNs that need key usage grants. We generally recommend you manage the key and pass key ARN into module.

[07:43] Priya Shah: Do you need KMS endpoint? You mentioned it earlier.

[07:46] Victor Alvarez: If you’re fully private with no NAT, yes. Because when instances call KMS APIs, it’s an AWS API endpoint. So you need the interface endpoint for KMS.

[07:58] Dan Miller: Okay, and CloudWatch Logs endpoint too if logs go to CloudWatch.

[08:02] Victor Alvarez: Correct.

[08:04] Elena Kovacs: What about ECR + S3 only. If we send logs to S3 and don’t use CloudWatch, do we still need CloudWatch endpoints?

[08:13] Victor Alvarez: Not strictly. But EKS itself sometimes uses CloudWatch for control plane logs if enabled. If you disable it, you can avoid.

[08:22] Markus Klein: The alpha “minimal endpoint set” we recommend for no-NAT is usually: ECR API, ECR DKR, STS, S3 gateway. Then add KMS if you use CMKs and add CloudWatch if you use it. Plus whatever your DNS design needs.

[08:41] Dan Miller: DNS design is actually where we get stuck. We have TGW hub, and DNS is centralized. If we create endpoints in the workload VPC but resolution is being forwarded to centralized resolvers, do we need Route 53 resolver endpoints?

[08:56] Victor Alvarez: Possibly. If your VPC uses the default AmazonProvidedDNS and the VPC has interface endpoints with private DNS, resolution inside that VPC should work without extra resolver endpoints. But if you have conditional forwarding that overrides, it gets tricky. A pattern is: keep AWS service domains resolved locally, and only forward internal corp domains.

[09:19] Dan Miller: We do the opposite sometimes because of policy. Like, “all DNS through central.” But maybe we can carve out AWS zones.

[09:28] Priya Shah: We can explore. It’s not impossible.

[09:31] Markus Klein: This is exactly why we’re building the “validated defaults” in Terraform. We’ll document supported patterns and where we say “we can’t guarantee.”

[09:40] Colin O'Donnell: Also, installer preflight checks: we now detect if endpoints exist and if private DNS is off, we print a warning that’s pretty explicit. It’s not perfect but it reduces time-to-fix.

[09:53] Elena Kovacs: That’s good. We hate black-box failures.

[09:57] Markus Klein: Connectivity constraints next. Dan, you mentioned TGW hub. Do you have Direct Connect into the hub VPC?

[10:04] Dan Miller: Yes. DX comes into shared services, then TGW to workload VPCs. No site-to-site VPN in most cases.

[10:13] Markus Klein: Any requirement for Redwood to connect back to Redwood cloud? Like control plane or updates?

[10:18] Priya Shah: For private deployment, no outbound to vendor clouds. That’s a hard requirement. If there is any “phone home” it’s a non-starter.

[10:27] Markus Klein: Understood. Redwood Private is designed to run with a private control plane in your VPC / on-prem. For alpha, we do have some optional support channels and telemetry that can be disabled. The default for regulated is off, and we can document what endpoints would be used if you opted in.

[10:45] Marcus Lin: From security perspective we treat outbound telemetry as optional, and we’ll provide a statement of what is collected if enabled. But again, can be off.

[10:55] Tom Reyes: On updates, how do you deliver patches? If no outbound internet, how do you get images?

[11:01] Colin O'Donnell: Great question. Typically we either mirror images into your ECR (your account) or you pull from a pre-approved registry path reachable via endpoints. For strict environments, we provide an artifact bundle or a process to sync images through your controlled pipeline.

[11:20] Elena Kovacs: We can do image mirroring. We have a pipeline that pulls from vendor, scans, then pushes into our ECR.

[11:27] Markus Klein: Perfect. Then endpoints for ECR are still needed inside the workload VPC.

[11:34] Priya Shah: Does the installer require access to GitHub or something for charts?

[11:39] Colin O'Donnell: Not if you use the packaged installer flow. If you use Helm directly and pull charts from public, that would need egress. But in private deployments we do offline-friendly packaging.

[11:54] Dan Miller: Good.

[11:56] Markus Klein: Let’s talk IAM boundaries because that’s another question you asked in email. Tom, what’s your standard model? Do you allow vendor-provided roles?

[12:05] Tom Reyes: We don’t allow “vendor creates admin roles.” We can allow a scoped role with permission boundary, and we need explicit list of actions.

[12:14] Marcus Lin: That’s aligned to what we’re doing in the Terraform modules. We create an installer execution role scaffold with a permission boundary. The boundary is a ceiling; then inline policy is the minimum set. You can tighten further.

[12:32] Tom Reyes: Can we pre-create the role and just give you ARN? We don’t want modules creating IAM.

[12:38] Victor Alvarez: Yes. Modules support “create_iam = false” and you pass role ARN. We still output the required policy document so your team can attach it.

[12:49] Elena Kovacs: That’s important for our internal process.

[12:52] Markus Klein: On audit trails for IAM actions, we assume you have CloudTrail. But do you need application audit logs to go to SIEM?

[13:00] Priya Shah: Yes. We are Splunk-heavy. Ideally logs land in S3 then we have forwarders.

[13:07] Marcus Lin: Great. For VPC Flow Logs, S3 is easy. For application logs, it depends on your logging stack in EKS. Most customers use Fluent Bit or similar to ship to S3, Kinesis, or Splunk HEC. Our requirement is: logs must be accessible for incident response and compliance. We’ll provide a recommended config but we don’t force a specific SIEM.

[13:31] Dan Miller: For flow logs: do you turn on “all traffic” or only rejects?

[13:36] Victor Alvarez: Default in alpha is “REJECT” plus “ALL” optionally. We got feedback that “ALL” is expensive but helpful during pilots. We can set it to REJECT by default and have a variable.

[13:51] Priya Shah: In prod we do REJECT plus sampling. But POC, we can do more.

[13:56] Markus Klein: Makes sense.

[14:00] Elena Kovacs: One question: do you need VPC endpoints for SSM? We use SSM Session Manager, no bastions.

[14:07] Victor Alvarez: If you want SSM in private subnets with no NAT, yes you’d need endpoints for SSM, EC2 messages, SSM messages. But that’s your admin plane, not Redwood-specific. Our modules don’t force it but we can document as an extension.

[14:25] Dan Miller: Okay.

[14:27] Markus Klein: Let’s ground this in your topology. Dan, do you have a rough CIDR plan for the pilot VPC?

[14:33] Dan Miller: Likely a /20 in us-east-1, three AZs. Private subnets only. We can have a small management subnet if required, but prefer not.

[14:44] Victor Alvarez: /20 is fine. Our reference uses three AZs with private subnets per AZ and optional public subnets if NAT gateways are used. For no-NAT, you can omit public.

[14:57] Elena Kovacs: EKS requires something for load balancers. If it’s internal, it can still be in private subnets.

[15:03] Victor Alvarez: Correct. For internal NLB/ALB, private subnets are okay as long as tags are right.

[15:11] Priya Shah: Ingress: we’ll do internal only. No public endpoints.

[15:16] Markus Klein: That’s supported. Redwood Private is typically internal ingress with your own connectivity controls.

[15:22] Colin O'Donnell: We’ll want to confirm the installer expectations: DNS, TLS, internal cert chain. But we can cover that separately.

[15:31] Elena Kovacs: We use ACM Private CA for internal certs.

[15:35] Marcus Lin: Great, that works.

[15:38] Markus Klein: On KMS again, Tom: do you require keys in a separate account or same account?

[15:44] Tom Reyes: Ideally a security account, but for pilot we can do same account with guardrails.

[15:51] Marcus Lin: That’s fine. Cross-account KMS is doable but adds complexity; for alpha we can keep it simple.

[16:02] Dan Miller: Another constraint: we’re required to log DNS queries in some environments. Do you support Route 53 Resolver query logs?

[16:10] Victor Alvarez: If you’re using Route 53 Resolver, yes, query logs can be enabled and delivered to CloudWatch or S3. Our alpha modules don’t automatically set that up yet, but we have it on the roadmap and can provide a pattern.

[16:25] Priya Shah: We’d want it for the pilot if possible.

[16:28] Markus Klein: We can do it as an add-on pattern even if not in the core module. The key is: we don’t want alpha to be unmaintainable, but we can help in pilot.

[16:39] Elena Kovacs: That’s acceptable. For pilot we can do a little custom.

[16:45] Markus Klein: Ok. What about data residency: us-east-1 only?

[16:49] Priya Shah: Yes, for now.

[16:52] Markus Klein: Great.

[16:54] Dan Miller: On endpoints, do you require “Interface endpoints in every subnet” or can we centralize them?

[17:01] Victor Alvarez: Technically interface endpoints are ENIs in specific subnets. Best practice is put them in each AZ where you have workloads to avoid cross-AZ traffic and to keep it resilient. We usually create one subnet per AZ for endpoints, or reuse private subnets, but security groups matter.

[17:20] Dan Miller: We’d prefer dedicated endpoint subnets, but that’s another CIDR.

[17:24] Victor Alvarez: Yeah. Our module supports either. Default is using the private subnets with a locked-down endpoint SG.

[17:33] Priya Shah: Endpoint SG: can it be strict, like only 443 from node security group?

[17:38] Victor Alvarez: Yes. That’s part of the opinionated security group patterns. We try to avoid 0.0.0.0/0 internal sprawl.

[17:46] Marcus Lin: And we also recommend NACLs sparingly because they cause debug pain, but if you have them, we can work within them.

[17:55] Dan Miller: We have NACL baselines. We can share.

[17:58] Markus Klein: Perfect.

[18:01] Elena Kovacs: This might be dumb: for S3 gateway endpoint, does it work with KMS-encrypted buckets?

[18:06] Victor Alvarez: Yes, gateway endpoint is just routing. KMS is separate call path, so you still need KMS endpoint if no NAT.

[18:15] Elena Kovacs: Got it.

[18:17] Colin O'Donnell: Another “gotcha”: if you’re mirroring images into ECR, your EKS nodes will still talk to ECR API/DKR. So endpoints must exist and DNS must resolve to them.

[18:28] Dan Miller: Yep.

[18:30] Markus Klein: Let’s talk pilot. We’re looking at you as a design partner for the alpha modules. That means: you’d use our Terraform modules as a baseline, we’d do weekly check-ins, and we’d incorporate feedback into the defaults/extension points.

[18:45] Priya Shah: What’s the timeline? We’re trying to do an internal proof by end of July.

[18:50] Markus Klein: That’s feasible. Typically: 1–2 weeks to set up infra with Terraform, then run installer preflights, then 1–2 weeks to validate logging, access, and run a small workload. We’ll need a sandbox account.

[19:05] Elena Kovacs: We can use our dev security account.

[19:09] Tom Reyes: We still need approvals for IAM and KMS.

[19:12] Markus Klein: Understood. To speed that, we’ll send the exact IAM policy and boundary docs.

[19:18] Marcus Lin: And we can provide a short “security defaults” memo: what logs are enabled, what’s encrypted, what doesn’t leave the VPC.

[19:27] Priya Shah: That will help.

[19:30] Dan Miller: For connectivity, we want to avoid provisioning NAT gateways. Are you okay if we start with endpoints only?

[19:37] Victor Alvarez: Yes. We’ll want to validate the endpoints list and DNS early. The integration test harness we have internally is basically the same: stand up VPC, endpoints, flow logs, then verify outputs.

[19:51] Colin O'Donnell: Also we can run installer preflight without installing everything, just to validate “network prerequisites” quickly.

[20:00] Elena Kovacs: Great.

[20:02] Markus Klein: Any other concerns? Competitor-wise, are you comparing to Bedrock private endpoints or Azure OpenAI private link? Just so we understand the bar.

[20:12] Priya Shah: We’re looking at multiple. Bedrock is easy in AWS but model selection and control is… limited. Azure is complicated because we’re not all-in on Azure.

[20:23] Markus Klein: Understood.

[20:25] Dan Miller: The bar is: private connectivity and auditability. If we can’t prove no egress and we can’t get logs into Splunk, it’s dead.

[20:34] Markus Klein: Clear.

[20:36] Marcus Lin: On “no egress,” we can help you validate with flow logs and route tables. Also we can provide a checklist: no IGW, no NAT, only endpoints, and restrict SG egress to endpoints.

[20:51] Priya Shah: That checklist is what I want.

[20:54] Markus Klein: Great.

[20:56] Elena Kovacs: One more: do you need a VPC endpoint for Secrets Manager?

[21:00] Victor Alvarez: If you use Secrets Manager and have no NAT, yes. But depending on how secrets are stored. Many clusters do.

[21:10] Colin O'Donnell: For Redwood Private, we can integrate with Secrets Manager or use Kubernetes secrets with envelope encryption; either way there’s a KMS aspect.

[21:21] Elena Kovacs: Ok, we’ll plan for Secrets Manager endpoint too.

[21:25] Dan Miller: The endpoints list is growing.

[21:27] Victor Alvarez: Yeah, that’s why we split into “required” and “recommended.” Required for the common path: ECR, STS, S3. Recommended: KMS, Logs, Secrets Manager, EC2 depending. We’ll send the matrix.

[21:44] Markus Klein: Okay, we’re at about halfway. Let’s do a quick recap and make sure we’re aligned.

[21:50] Markus Klein: What I’m hearing: Acme wants a no-NAT pilot VPC in us-east-1 with private EKS endpoint, internal ingress only, interface endpoints with private DNS allowed for AWS service domains, logs to S3 with 1-year retention, CMK everywhere, and IAM roles pre-created with boundaries.

[22:10] Priya Shah: Correct.

[22:12] Dan Miller: Yeah.

[22:13] Tom Reyes: Yes.

[22:15] Markus Klein: Great. Next, we’ll send: endpoint list + DNS notes, logging defaults doc, IAM boundary doc, and a minimal Terraform example. Then you’ll confirm DNS policy and KMS policy constraints.

[22:28] Elena Kovacs: Works.

[22:30] Markus Klein: Any final questions before we drop?

[22:33] Priya Shah: Can you provide a statement about where audit logs live? Like, are they only in the cluster or can we guarantee export?

[22:41] Marcus Lin: We can guarantee that audit events are emitted. Export depends on your sink. In the pilot we’ll configure export to your S3/Splunk path and validate delivery.

[22:53] Priya Shah: Ok.

[22:55] Dan Miller: The installer preflight - does it check route tables too? Or just endpoints.

[23:01] Colin O'Donnell: Right now it checks endpoints presence and DNS resolution for some services (ECR mainly). Route table validation is on the roadmap; we can also provide a manual checklist.

[23:15] Dan Miller: Fine.

[23:16] Markus Klein: Perfect. We’ll follow up with a calendar invite for a 90-minute working session on topology next week. Thanks all.

[23:25] Priya Shah: Thanks.

[23:26] Elena Kovacs: Thank you.

[23:27] Dan Miller: Bye.

[23:28] Tom Reyes: Bye.

----

title: Security review - Private upgrades: audit logs, backup encryption, rollback evidence (Cascade)
preview: Cascade walked through audit/compliance evidence requirements for Private upgrades (VPC) with an eye toward SOX-ish controls and internal auditability. Redwood reviewed planned upgrade lifecycle events (upgrade started, prechecks pass/fail, backup started/completed, migration start/end, rollback invoked/completed), and discussed required fields to make logs useful in Splunk (who, what version, where, correlation IDs). Open questions centered on retention/tamper-evidence, and how customer-managed KMS keys apply to backups and upgrade state. Redwood committed to sharing the audit event schema draft + an example Splunk mapping, and confirming support bundle contents (upgrade plan JSON, last-run logs, checksum list for artifacts).

title: Cobalt Finance — Security review: Dedicated Burst audit logging + RBAC
preview: Cobalt Finance security team reviewed Redwood’s audit logging and RBAC model for the Dedicated “Burst Capacity” feature. Customer asked for: (1) concrete audit event types for burst config changes and admin overrides, (2) who can make changes (RBAC + SSO/SAML), (3) retention options and export to their SIEM, and (4) evidence that customer admins can independently audit changes vs Redwood Support actions. Redwood explained planned audit events (enable/disable, limit updates, cooldown/ratio changes, tier changes, policy override, emergency circuit breaker), separation of duties (customer admin vs Redwood operator), read vs write permissions, and log export/retention approach (Console + API export, with longer retention for enterprise). Next steps include sharing a sample audit event payload/redacted examples and confirming Cobalt’s retention and SIEM ingestion requirements.

title: Security review - audit logging for route experiments (Helio Finance)

title: Security review: Quantization Profiles change controls + audit logs (FalconBank)
preview: FalconBank security review focused on change control for the opt-in Quantization Profile Library. Customer asked how Redwood enforces RBAC for route-level enablement, what gets written to audit logs (fields, retention, export), whether quantization can be globally disabled, and how Redwood prevents silent profile changes. Redwood confirmed: (1) route-level toggles only (no default-on), (2) deterministic resolution with compatibility guardrails and safe fallback to baseline, (3) immutable, versioned profile IDs (with signing/integrity checks) and no silent upgrades when pinning a specific profile, (4) audit events with actor, route, before/after diff, and outcome (applied/fallback/disabled), (5) global kill-switch + per-tenant override option, and (6) Console change history view and API-driven configuration for internal approvals.

Emberfin will confirm internal retention requirements and preferred export format and schedule a follow-up focused on SIEM ingestion + a short tabletop scenario for change-control evidence.
