title: Northbridge Healthcare - Private POC scoping (identity + RBAC)
preview:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-03-05
Time: 9:00 AM PT
Duration: 46 minutes
Title: Northbridge Healthcare - Private POC scoping (identity + RBAC)
Organizer: Colin O'Donnell (Redwood Inference)
Attendees: 
- Redwood: Colin O'Donnell, Vivek Kulkarni, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Marcus Lin
- Northbridge Healthcare: Sarah Nguyen, Michael Patel, Jen Alvarez

Auto-summary (Fireflies)
Summary:
- Northbridge is evaluating a Private deployment and wants to validate how Redwood RBAC v2 works with Okta (group-based access), least-privilege role templates, and auditability.
- Customer emphasized separation of duties (Security vs Platform Ops vs Auditor) and the need to scope permissions by environment/cluster.
- Redwood confirmed deny-by-default authorization, role templates, role bindings by scope, and enhanced audit logs that include authorization decision context and (optionally) denial reason codes.

Topics:
- IdP integration constraints (Okta, SAML vs OIDC, group claims)
- Group-to-role mapping and scope model (org / project / environment / cluster)
- Role templates needed for Private admin and day-2 operations
- Break-glass access expectations and monitoring
- POC timeline and success criteria

Questions captured:
- Can Redwood map IdP groups directly to roles, or is there a separate provisioning step?
- How do role bindings work across environments (dev/stage/prod) and clusters?
- What happens if a user is in multiple groups (union vs precedence)?
- Can an auditor view audit logs without being able to export them to SIEM?
- What does the “permission denied” error look like and can admins see why?

Transcript

[00:00] Colin O'Donnell: Alright, cool. I think we’re recording. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the Private POC scoping call, focus on identity and RBAC. I’m Colin, solutions on the Private pilots. Vivek is our PM for Private deployments. Kaitlyn is our authn/authz lead, Marcus is security engineering.

[00:18] Sarah Nguyen: Great. Thanks Colin. I’m Sarah, IT Security. Michael’s our platform lead, Jen’s GRC.

[00:27] Michael Patel: Hey.

[00:28] Jen Alvarez: Hi everyone.

[00:31] Colin O'Donnell: Awesome. Maybe quick framing: you all are looking at Private, so you’ll run Redwood in your VPC / your environment. The big things today: how do we connect to your IdP, how do we map groups to roles, what role templates do you need, and what’s the timeline for the POC.

[00:50] Vivek Kulkarni: And we can also cover what we consider “MVP” for POC: like admin bootstrap, separation of duties, and audit logs.

[01:00] Sarah Nguyen: Yep. The headline for us is least privilege. We’ve been burned with systems that are basically “admin” or “not admin.” We need separation: security admin manages policies and audit, ops manages deployments, and auditors are read-only.

[01:16] Marcus Lin: Makes sense. That aligns with what we’ve been building: fine-grained permissions and explicit role templates.

[01:24] Colin O'Donnell: Before we jump there, can you confirm IdP? We saw Okta in the security review notes.

[01:30] Sarah Nguyen: Yes, Okta. For most internal apps we do SAML. Some newer ones are OIDC, but security prefers SAML where possible.

[01:40] Kaitlyn Nguyen: For Private, we support SSO with either SAML or OIDC depending on your deployment configuration. The key RBAC question is: what claims do we get for groups, and what are the constraints on group list size.

[01:54] Michael Patel: Okta can send group attributes in SAML assertion but there’s a limit. It’s not infinite. We typically do a filtered list, like groups matching a regex.

[02:05] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yep, that’s common. If you can do “groups” filtered to “redwood-*” or similar, that’s ideal. Otherwise we can use a custom attribute too, but group claim is easiest.

[02:17] Sarah Nguyen: Another constraint: we can’t do SCIM provisioning into every vendor right away. We prefer “if you’re in the Okta group, you get access.”

[02:26] Colin O'Donnell: Understood. So you’d like group-based authorization, not manual per-user assignments.

[02:33] Sarah Nguyen: Exactly. And then we need the ability to tie those groups to roles, ideally at different scopes. Like prod vs dev.

[02:43] Vivek Kulkarni: Great. Our RBAC v2 model is: Permissions are atomic actions, roles are collections of permissions, and role bindings attach roles to subjects (users, groups) at a scope. Scopes can be organization-level and then narrower scopes like project/environment. In Private there’s also cluster-level operations.

[03:02] Michael Patel: When you say “environment,” is that like a namespace? Or…

[03:07] Vivek Kulkarni: Conceptually yes. It maps to “deployment environments” in our control plane: dev, staging, prod. We can align that to your separate clusters or to logical environments inside one cluster.

[03:20] Michael Patel: Okay, so scope could be “prod environment” and then a role binding to that.

[03:25] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Exactly.

[03:27] Jen Alvarez: For compliance: we need an auditor role that can view audit logs but not change retention settings. And for some tools, export to SIEM is considered privileged.

[03:38] Marcus Lin: That’s a good call-out. We generally split “view audit events” from “export audit events” and also from “configure audit sinks.” Export can be sensitive, because it can exfiltrate logs.

[03:50] Sarah Nguyen: Yes, that’s exactly the separation we want.

[03:54] Colin O'Donnell: Maybe let’s list the roles you’re thinking and we map to templates.

[04:00] Sarah Nguyen: Sure. We need:
1) Org or tenant admin for initial setup, but we want that to be limited and ideally time-bound.
2) Security admin: manage roles, role bindings, audit, maybe key management.
3) Platform operator: manage deployments, routing policies, model pinning, scaling.
4) Auditor: read-only.
And maybe a “Support” role for our internal L2 helpdesk, but not sure.

[04:28] Vivek Kulkarni: Those map well to our built-ins. For Private we’ve added operator roles that include cluster scoped actions like managing the inference pools, configuration, but do not include org/user management.

[04:40] Michael Patel: I have a question. In Private, do we need Redwood to do the “org/user creation”? Or does it auto-create users on login.

[04:48] Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s JIT (just-in-time) user creation on first login, usually. But authorization will still be deny-by-default until a role binding exists for that user or their group.

[05:01] Sarah Nguyen: That’s good. But then how do we avoid the chicken-and-egg problem? Like first person can’t login.

[05:07] Colin O'Donnell: Great question. Private installer bootstrap includes creating an initial admin binding. Typically you configure a bootstrap group in Okta, like “redwood-private-bootstrap-admins,” and the installer binds that group to the Org Admin role.

[05:25] Michael Patel: So we set that group membership in Okta, and then first login works.

[05:28] Colin O'Donnell: Yes.

[05:30] Sarah Nguyen: And that admin group is separate from day-to-day admins.

[05:33] Marcus Lin: And we can add controls around break-glass. Like separate group, alerts, and time-bound elevation if you want.

[05:42] Jen Alvarez: Time-bound is important. We have a policy that emergency admin access should be approved and expire.

[05:50] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Today, the RBAC system supports time-bound bindings, but the workflow can vary. In POC we can demonstrate: create an “elevated admin” role binding with an expiry, and log it.

[06:05] Sarah Nguyen: Okay.

[06:07] Michael Patel: Let’s talk group mapping. We have existing Okta groups. We can create new ones, but we prefer to avoid hundreds. How many do you expect?

[06:18] Vivek Kulkarni: We don’t need hundreds. Most customers do maybe 6-15 groups: per environment per function, like “redwood-prod-operators,” “redwood-prod-security-admins,” “redwood-auditors,” etc.

[06:32] Michael Patel: That sounds reasonable.

[06:34] Sarah Nguyen: We also need to map to projects. We might have multiple teams consuming the LLM platform.

[06:41] Vivek Kulkarni: Then you can have project-scoped role bindings. Like Team A operators can manage Team A project routing policies but not Team B.

[06:51] Michael Patel: So the scope dimension is org -> project -> env.

[06:55] Vivek Kulkarni: That’s right. And in Private, cluster can be an additional scope for certain installer and infrastructure operations.

[07:05] Sarah Nguyen: How do conflicts work? If someone is in both “auditor” and “operator” group.

[07:11] Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s additive: union of allowed permissions across all bindings. We don’t do “deny overrides allow” for normal RBAC. Deny-by-default means if no binding grants it, it’s denied.

[07:23] Sarah Nguyen: Okay, union makes sense.

[07:26] Jen Alvarez: What about separation of duties though? Like ensuring the same person can’t be both security admin and billing admin.

[07:33] Marcus Lin: That’s more like SoD enforcement, typically done via policy or org process. In our v2 we’re not doing hard ABAC constraints like “cannot have both roles” yet. But you can enforce that via Okta group policy and approvals.

[07:48] Jen Alvarez: That’s acceptable. We can enforce at IdP side.

[07:52] Colin O'Donnell: On the role templates: we’ll share a set that covers what you listed. We can start with built-ins and customize later if needed.

[08:01] Michael Patel: Custom roles are possible?

[08:04] Vivek Kulkarni: Yes, you can define custom roles by selecting permissions. For POC, we recommend starting with templates because it reduces risk.

[08:13] Sarah Nguyen: I want to be careful about “template drift” later. Like when you add new permissions, does the template auto-update.

[08:21] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Great question. Templates are versioned. We treat them as a bundle. If you stick to templates, you can opt into updates; but it’s not silently changing your access model. We can discuss how updates are rolled out, especially in Private.

[08:36] Michael Patel: That’s good. Silent permission expansion is a no-go for us.

[08:41] Marcus Lin: Same. We have strong rules on that internally.

[08:45] Colin O'Donnell: Switching to auditability. Jen, you mentioned export to SIEM.

[08:50] Jen Alvarez: Yes. We need audit logs for auth changes and for sensitive operations. We also need to show who did what and why. Some systems just log “403.” We need detail.

[09:04] Marcus Lin: We log authorization decisions as part of audit events. That includes subject, action, scope, policy version, decision allow/deny, and denial reasons, with some controls on what’s exposed.

[09:18] Sarah Nguyen: Do you log denies too?

[09:20] Marcus Lin: Yes, for security-relevant endpoints and admin console actions. There are some volume considerations, but for RBAC and admin operations, yes.

[09:31] Michael Patel: Can we export those to Splunk.

[09:33] Marcus Lin: Yes. We have an exporter that can map into common SIEM formats. For Private you can run it inside your environment.

[09:41] Jen Alvarez: Okay.

[09:43] Colin O'Donnell: Let’s talk timeline. What’s driving the POC schedule on your side.

[09:49] Sarah Nguyen: We have a decision gate end of April. We want a working POC in March and early April. Ideally we install in a dev VPC first.

[10:01] Michael Patel: From infra, we can stand up a Kubernetes cluster in two weeks. The question is identity and network connectivity.

[10:09] Colin O'Donnell: Great. So if we target install week of March 17th, does that align.

[10:15] Michael Patel: Probably, yes.

[10:17] Vivek Kulkarni: For POC success criteria, can we enumerate? Usually it’s: admin bootstrap, SSO login, role assignment, verify least-privileged flows, validate audit logs, and run a basic inference workload.

[10:31] Sarah Nguyen: Yep. Specifically we want:
- Okta SAML works.
- Group-based role binding works.
- Operator can deploy and change routing but cannot manage users.
- Security admin can manage roles and export audit logs.
- Auditor can view audit but cannot export.

[10:55] Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s a clean matrix.

[10:58] Michael Patel: And we need to ensure we can scope ops to dev vs prod, but in POC maybe we just do dev.

[11:06] Colin O'Donnell: Makes sense.

[11:09] Sarah Nguyen: Also, error messages. When someone gets denied, we don’t want to leak internals, but admins need to troubleshoot quickly.

[11:18] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Totally. The default response is a standardized “permission denied.” For admins, we can enable a debug header or console view that shows the denial reason code and what permission was missing. It’s controlled.

[11:33] Sarah Nguyen: That’s good.

[11:35] Jen Alvarez: Do you have evidence artifacts for SOC2. Like design docs and test coverage.

[11:41] Marcus Lin: Yes. We can share an overview and the relevant sections: authorization enforcement patterns, audit logging schema, and test approach. For the POC we can also show sample audit events.

[11:55] Michael Patel: On network: does the control plane call back to Redwood cloud.

[11:59] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, there’s an option for a private control plane within your environment. There may be optional update checks or telemetry, but we can disable or route it based on your policies. We’ll scope it per your requirements.

[12:14] Michael Patel: We likely need no outbound except allowlisted.

[12:18] Colin O'Donnell: Understood.

[12:20] Sarah Nguyen: On group mapping, one more thing: we have nested groups sometimes.

[12:24] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Okta nested groups don’t always materialize in SAML claims the way you expect. We typically rely on the final set of group names in the assertion. If nested membership is important, you might need to flatten via Okta rules.

[12:39] Sarah Nguyen: Yeah, we can do that.

[12:42] Michael Patel: What about group name length and characters. Some of ours have spaces.

[12:48] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Spaces can be okay in SAML attribute values, but for mapping we recommend stable identifiers. If needed we can map on group ID, but most setups map on group name string. We’ll confirm what’s easiest.

[13:04] Colin O'Donnell: If you can standardize “redwood-<env>-<role>” naming, it’ll save pain.

[13:10] Sarah Nguyen: Agreed.

[13:12] Vivek Kulkarni: Another question: do you need separate roles for API access vs Console.

[13:18] Michael Patel: Yes, good point. We’ll have service accounts generating tokens.

[13:23] Kaitlyn Nguyen: For service accounts / API keys, we treat them as subjects too. You can grant a key a role binding at a scope. We’ll define that in the POC: for example, a CI pipeline key can be “deployment operator” for dev environment.

[13:40] Michael Patel: Great.

[13:42] Sarah Nguyen: Do API keys rotate automatically.

[13:44] Marcus Lin: Rotation is supported and is a permissioned action. We can ensure only Security Admin can rotate keys, or you can delegate to Platform Ops depending on policy.

[13:55] Sarah Nguyen: We want Security Admin only.

[13:58] Colin O'Donnell: Noted.

[14:00] (crosstalk)
[14:01] Michael Patel: Sorry, one more: For Private, do we still have “org” concept. We’ll be one tenant.

[14:07] Vivek Kulkarni: Usually yes, still one org but you can create multiple projects/environments inside. It helps keep semantics consistent.

[14:16] Michael Patel: Okay.

[14:18] Colin O'Donnell: I think we covered main points. Maybe let’s do a quick recap and next steps.

[14:24] Colin O'Donnell: Recap: Northbridge wants Okta SAML, group-based RBAC with deny-by-default. Roles: bootstrap admin, security admin, platform operator, auditor. Scope: at least environment and project, and maybe cluster for infra ops. Need audit logs with authorization decision context and SIEM export separation. Timeline: aim for install around week of March 17th, decision gate end of April.

[14:54] Sarah Nguyen: That’s right.

[14:56] Colin O'Donnell: Next steps from us: we’ll send a checklist with specific IdP questions and role template recommendations. Kaitlyn will send examples of group mapping, Marcus will send security artifacts pointers and audit log samples. From you: sample SAML assertion / group claim, list of group naming constraints, and confirm break-glass expectations.

[15:20] Jen Alvarez: Works.

[15:22] Michael Patel: Yep.

[15:23] Sarah Nguyen: Thanks all.

[15:25] Colin O'Donnell: Cool, appreciate it. We’ll follow up in email and get the next technical session scheduled.

(Transcript ends - recording stopped a bit early; remaining time was scheduling logistics.)
text:
Meeting header
Date: 2025-03-05
Time: 9:00 AM PT
Duration: 46 minutes
Title: Northbridge Healthcare - Private POC scoping (identity + RBAC)
Organizer: Colin O'Donnell (Redwood Inference)
Attendees: 
- Redwood: Colin O'Donnell, Vivek Kulkarni, Kaitlyn Nguyen, Marcus Lin
- Northbridge Healthcare: Sarah Nguyen, Michael Patel, Jen Alvarez

Auto-summary (Fireflies)
Summary:
- Northbridge is evaluating a Private deployment and wants to validate how Redwood RBAC v2 works with Okta (group-based access), least-privilege role templates, and auditability.
- Customer emphasized separation of duties (Security vs Platform Ops vs Auditor) and the need to scope permissions by environment/cluster.
- Redwood confirmed deny-by-default authorization, role templates, role bindings by scope, and enhanced audit logs that include authorization decision context and (optionally) denial reason codes.

Topics:
- IdP integration constraints (Okta, SAML vs OIDC, group claims)
- Group-to-role mapping and scope model (org / project / environment / cluster)
- Role templates needed for Private admin and day-2 operations
- Break-glass access expectations and monitoring
- POC timeline and success criteria

Questions captured:
- Can Redwood map IdP groups directly to roles, or is there a separate provisioning step?
- How do role bindings work across environments (dev/stage/prod) and clusters?
- What happens if a user is in multiple groups (union vs precedence)?
- Can an auditor view audit logs without being able to export them to SIEM?
- What does the “permission denied” error look like and can admins see why?

Transcript

[00:00] Colin O'Donnell: Alright, cool. I think we’re recording. Thanks everyone for joining. This is the Private POC scoping call, focus on identity and RBAC. I’m Colin, solutions on the Private pilots. Vivek is our PM for Private deployments. Kaitlyn is our authn/authz lead, Marcus is security engineering.

[00:18] Sarah Nguyen: Great. Thanks Colin. I’m Sarah, IT Security. Michael’s our platform lead, Jen’s GRC.

[00:27] Michael Patel: Hey.

[00:28] Jen Alvarez: Hi everyone.

[00:31] Colin O'Donnell: Awesome. Maybe quick framing: you all are looking at Private, so you’ll run Redwood in your VPC / your environment. The big things today: how do we connect to your IdP, how do we map groups to roles, what role templates do you need, and what’s the timeline for the POC.

[00:50] Vivek Kulkarni: And we can also cover what we consider “MVP” for POC: like admin bootstrap, separation of duties, and audit logs.

[01:00] Sarah Nguyen: Yep. The headline for us is least privilege. We’ve been burned with systems that are basically “admin” or “not admin.” We need separation: security admin manages policies and audit, ops manages deployments, and auditors are read-only.

[01:16] Marcus Lin: Makes sense. That aligns with what we’ve been building: fine-grained permissions and explicit role templates.

[01:24] Colin O'Donnell: Before we jump there, can you confirm IdP? We saw Okta in the security review notes.

[01:30] Sarah Nguyen: Yes, Okta. For most internal apps we do SAML. Some newer ones are OIDC, but security prefers SAML where possible.

[01:40] Kaitlyn Nguyen: For Private, we support SSO with either SAML or OIDC depending on your deployment configuration. The key RBAC question is: what claims do we get for groups, and what are the constraints on group list size.

[01:54] Michael Patel: Okta can send group attributes in SAML assertion but there’s a limit. It’s not infinite. We typically do a filtered list, like groups matching a regex.

[02:05] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Yep, that’s common. If you can do “groups” filtered to “redwood-*” or similar, that’s ideal. Otherwise we can use a custom attribute too, but group claim is easiest.

[02:17] Sarah Nguyen: Another constraint: we can’t do SCIM provisioning into every vendor right away. We prefer “if you’re in the Okta group, you get access.”

[02:26] Colin O'Donnell: Understood. So you’d like group-based authorization, not manual per-user assignments.

[02:33] Sarah Nguyen: Exactly. And then we need the ability to tie those groups to roles, ideally at different scopes. Like prod vs dev.

[02:43] Vivek Kulkarni: Great. Our RBAC v2 model is: Permissions are atomic actions, roles are collections of permissions, and role bindings attach roles to subjects (users, groups) at a scope. Scopes can be organization-level and then narrower scopes like project/environment. In Private there’s also cluster-level operations.

[03:02] Michael Patel: When you say “environment,” is that like a namespace? Or…

[03:07] Vivek Kulkarni: Conceptually yes. It maps to “deployment environments” in our control plane: dev, staging, prod. We can align that to your separate clusters or to logical environments inside one cluster.

[03:20] Michael Patel: Okay, so scope could be “prod environment” and then a role binding to that.

[03:25] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Exactly.

[03:27] Jen Alvarez: For compliance: we need an auditor role that can view audit logs but not change retention settings. And for some tools, export to SIEM is considered privileged.

[03:38] Marcus Lin: That’s a good call-out. We generally split “view audit events” from “export audit events” and also from “configure audit sinks.” Export can be sensitive, because it can exfiltrate logs.

[03:50] Sarah Nguyen: Yes, that’s exactly the separation we want.

[03:54] Colin O'Donnell: Maybe let’s list the roles you’re thinking and we map to templates.

[04:00] Sarah Nguyen: Sure. We need:
1) Org or tenant admin for initial setup, but we want that to be limited and ideally time-bound.
2) Security admin: manage roles, role bindings, audit, maybe key management.
3) Platform operator: manage deployments, routing policies, model pinning, scaling.
4) Auditor: read-only.
And maybe a “Support” role for our internal L2 helpdesk, but not sure.

[04:28] Vivek Kulkarni: Those map well to our built-ins. For Private we’ve added operator roles that include cluster scoped actions like managing the inference pools, configuration, but do not include org/user management.

[04:40] Michael Patel: I have a question. In Private, do we need Redwood to do the “org/user creation”? Or does it auto-create users on login.

[04:48] Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s JIT (just-in-time) user creation on first login, usually. But authorization will still be deny-by-default until a role binding exists for that user or their group.

[05:01] Sarah Nguyen: That’s good. But then how do we avoid the chicken-and-egg problem? Like first person can’t login.

[05:07] Colin O'Donnell: Great question. Private installer bootstrap includes creating an initial admin binding. Typically you configure a bootstrap group in Okta, like “redwood-private-bootstrap-admins,” and the installer binds that group to the Org Admin role.

[05:25] Michael Patel: So we set that group membership in Okta, and then first login works.

[05:28] Colin O'Donnell: Yes.

[05:30] Sarah Nguyen: And that admin group is separate from day-to-day admins.

[05:33] Marcus Lin: And we can add controls around break-glass. Like separate group, alerts, and time-bound elevation if you want.

[05:42] Jen Alvarez: Time-bound is important. We have a policy that emergency admin access should be approved and expire.

[05:50] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Today, the RBAC system supports time-bound bindings, but the workflow can vary. In POC we can demonstrate: create an “elevated admin” role binding with an expiry, and log it.

[06:05] Sarah Nguyen: Okay.

[06:07] Michael Patel: Let’s talk group mapping. We have existing Okta groups. We can create new ones, but we prefer to avoid hundreds. How many do you expect?

[06:18] Vivek Kulkarni: We don’t need hundreds. Most customers do maybe 6-15 groups: per environment per function, like “redwood-prod-operators,” “redwood-prod-security-admins,” “redwood-auditors,” etc.

[06:32] Michael Patel: That sounds reasonable.

[06:34] Sarah Nguyen: We also need to map to projects. We might have multiple teams consuming the LLM platform.

[06:41] Vivek Kulkarni: Then you can have project-scoped role bindings. Like Team A operators can manage Team A project routing policies but not Team B.

[06:51] Michael Patel: So the scope dimension is org -> project -> env.

[06:55] Vivek Kulkarni: That’s right. And in Private, cluster can be an additional scope for certain installer and infrastructure operations.

[07:05] Sarah Nguyen: How do conflicts work? If someone is in both “auditor” and “operator” group.

[07:11] Kaitlyn Nguyen: It’s additive: union of allowed permissions across all bindings. We don’t do “deny overrides allow” for normal RBAC. Deny-by-default means if no binding grants it, it’s denied.

[07:23] Sarah Nguyen: Okay, union makes sense.

[07:26] Jen Alvarez: What about separation of duties though? Like ensuring the same person can’t be both security admin and billing admin.

[07:33] Marcus Lin: That’s more like SoD enforcement, typically done via policy or org process. In our v2 we’re not doing hard ABAC constraints like “cannot have both roles” yet. But you can enforce that via Okta group policy and approvals.

[07:48] Jen Alvarez: That’s acceptable. We can enforce at IdP side.

[07:52] Colin O'Donnell: On the role templates: we’ll share a set that covers what you listed. We can start with built-ins and customize later if needed.

[08:01] Michael Patel: Custom roles are possible?

[08:04] Vivek Kulkarni: Yes, you can define custom roles by selecting permissions. For POC, we recommend starting with templates because it reduces risk.

[08:13] Sarah Nguyen: I want to be careful about “template drift” later. Like when you add new permissions, does the template auto-update.

[08:21] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Great question. Templates are versioned. We treat them as a bundle. If you stick to templates, you can opt into updates; but it’s not silently changing your access model. We can discuss how updates are rolled out, especially in Private.

[08:36] Michael Patel: That’s good. Silent permission expansion is a no-go for us.

[08:41] Marcus Lin: Same. We have strong rules on that internally.

[08:45] Colin O'Donnell: Switching to auditability. Jen, you mentioned export to SIEM.

[08:50] Jen Alvarez: Yes. We need audit logs for auth changes and for sensitive operations. We also need to show who did what and why. Some systems just log “403.” We need detail.

[09:04] Marcus Lin: We log authorization decisions as part of audit events. That includes subject, action, scope, policy version, decision allow/deny, and denial reasons, with some controls on what’s exposed.

[09:18] Sarah Nguyen: Do you log denies too?

[09:20] Marcus Lin: Yes, for security-relevant endpoints and admin console actions. There are some volume considerations, but for RBAC and admin operations, yes.

[09:31] Michael Patel: Can we export those to Splunk.

[09:33] Marcus Lin: Yes. We have an exporter that can map into common SIEM formats. For Private you can run it inside your environment.

[09:41] Jen Alvarez: Okay.

[09:43] Colin O'Donnell: Let’s talk timeline. What’s driving the POC schedule on your side.

[09:49] Sarah Nguyen: We have a decision gate end of April. We want a working POC in March and early April. Ideally we install in a dev VPC first.

[10:01] Michael Patel: From infra, we can stand up a Kubernetes cluster in two weeks. The question is identity and network connectivity.

[10:09] Colin O'Donnell: Great. So if we target install week of March 17th, does that align.

[10:15] Michael Patel: Probably, yes.

[10:17] Vivek Kulkarni: For POC success criteria, can we enumerate? Usually it’s: admin bootstrap, SSO login, role assignment, verify least-privileged flows, validate audit logs, and run a basic inference workload.

[10:31] Sarah Nguyen: Yep. Specifically we want:
- Okta SAML works.
- Group-based role binding works.
- Operator can deploy and change routing but cannot manage users.
- Security admin can manage roles and export audit logs.
- Auditor can view audit but cannot export.

[10:55] Kaitlyn Nguyen: That’s a clean matrix.

[10:58] Michael Patel: And we need to ensure we can scope ops to dev vs prod, but in POC maybe we just do dev.

[11:06] Colin O'Donnell: Makes sense.

[11:09] Sarah Nguyen: Also, error messages. When someone gets denied, we don’t want to leak internals, but admins need to troubleshoot quickly.

[11:18] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Totally. The default response is a standardized “permission denied.” For admins, we can enable a debug header or console view that shows the denial reason code and what permission was missing. It’s controlled.

[11:33] Sarah Nguyen: That’s good.

[11:35] Jen Alvarez: Do you have evidence artifacts for SOC2. Like design docs and test coverage.

[11:41] Marcus Lin: Yes. We can share an overview and the relevant sections: authorization enforcement patterns, audit logging schema, and test approach. For the POC we can also show sample audit events.

[11:55] Michael Patel: On network: does the control plane call back to Redwood cloud.

[11:59] Colin O'Donnell: For Private, there’s an option for a private control plane within your environment. There may be optional update checks or telemetry, but we can disable or route it based on your policies. We’ll scope it per your requirements.

[12:14] Michael Patel: We likely need no outbound except allowlisted.

[12:18] Colin O'Donnell: Understood.

[12:20] Sarah Nguyen: On group mapping, one more thing: we have nested groups sometimes.

[12:24] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Okta nested groups don’t always materialize in SAML claims the way you expect. We typically rely on the final set of group names in the assertion. If nested membership is important, you might need to flatten via Okta rules.

[12:39] Sarah Nguyen: Yeah, we can do that.

[12:42] Michael Patel: What about group name length and characters. Some of ours have spaces.

[12:48] Kaitlyn Nguyen: Spaces can be okay in SAML attribute values, but for mapping we recommend stable identifiers. If needed we can map on group ID, but most setups map on group name string. We’ll confirm what’s easiest.

[13:04] Colin O'Donnell: If you can standardize “redwood-<env>-<role>” naming, it’ll save pain.

[13:10] Sarah Nguyen: Agreed.

[13:12] Vivek Kulkarni: Another question: do you need separate roles for API access vs Console.

[13:18] Michael Patel: Yes, good point. We’ll have service accounts generating tokens.

[13:23] Kaitlyn Nguyen: For service accounts / API keys, we treat them as subjects too. You can grant a key a role binding at a scope. We’ll define that in the POC: for example, a CI pipeline key can be “deployment operator” for dev environment.

[13:40] Michael Patel: Great.

[13:42] Sarah Nguyen: Do API keys rotate automatically.

[13:44] Marcus Lin: Rotation is supported and is a permissioned action. We can ensure only Security Admin can rotate keys, or you can delegate to Platform Ops depending on policy.

[13:55] Sarah Nguyen: We want Security Admin only.

[13:58] Colin O'Donnell: Noted.

[14:00] (crosstalk)
[14:01] Michael Patel: Sorry, one more: For Private, do we still have “org” concept. We’ll be one tenant.

[14:07] Vivek Kulkarni: Usually yes, still one org but you can create multiple projects/environments inside. It helps keep semantics consistent.

[14:16] Michael Patel: Okay.

[14:18] Colin O'Donnell: I think we covered main points. Maybe let’s do a quick recap and next steps.

[14:24] Colin O'Donnell: Recap: Northbridge wants Okta SAML, group-based RBAC with deny-by-default. Roles: bootstrap admin, security admin, platform operator, auditor. Scope: at least environment and project, and maybe cluster for infra ops. Need audit logs with authorization decision context and SIEM export separation. Timeline: aim for install around week of March 17th, decision gate end of April.

[14:54] Sarah Nguyen: That’s right.

[14:56] Colin O'Donnell: Next steps from us: we’ll send a checklist with specific IdP questions and role template recommendations. Kaitlyn will send examples of group mapping, Marcus will send security artifacts pointers and audit log samples. From you: sample SAML assertion / group claim, list of group naming constraints, and confirm break-glass expectations.

[15:20] Jen Alvarez: Works.

[15:22] Michael Patel: Yep.

[15:23] Sarah Nguyen: Thanks all.

[15:25] Colin O'Donnell: Cool, appreciate it. We’ll follow up in email and get the next technical session scheduled.

(Transcript ends - recording stopped a bit early; remaining time was scheduling logistics.)

title: Ironbank Financial - Security review (RBAC, SSO/SAML, audit logs)
preview: Enterprise security review with Ironbank Financial covering Redwood’s RBAC v2 direction (least privilege, scoped role bindings, deny-by-default), SSO/SAML integration and group-to-role mapping expectations, and audit logging requirements for evidence and SIEM ingestion. Ironbank requested concrete examples: permission catalog granularity, audit event schema including authorization decision context, and operational controls like break-glass access + review. Redwood clarified hosted vs dedicated vs private semantics and current roadmap/timing for RBAC v2 rollout and migration compatibility.
text: Enterprise security review with Ironbank Financial covering Redwood’s RBAC v2 direction (least privilege, scoped role bindings, deny-by-default), SSO/SAML integration and group-to-role mapping expectations, and audit logging requirements for evidence and SIEM ingestion. Ironbank requested concrete examples: permission catalog granularity, audit event schema including authorization decision context, and operational controls like break-glass access + review. Redwood clarified hosted vs dedicated vs private semantics and current roadmap/timing for RBAC v2 rollout and migration compatibility.

title: Lattice Search — security + production readiness review (Hosted API + roadmap)
preview: Lattice Search walked Redwood through their internal go-live/security approval requirements for adding an LLM inference provider to an enterprise search product (customer data, regulated customers). Key asks: auditability (who did what / when), SSO/SAML + RBAC, data retention and residency clarity, and a concrete “launch checklist” they can attach to their change-management packet. Redwood clarified Hosted API data handling (no prompt storage by default; limited logs), available security controls today (SOC 2, encryption, key mgmt options depending on deployment), and what’s on the near-term roadmap (richer audit logs in Console, admin controls). Discussion also covered production readiness items: retries/timeouts, rate limiting/quotas, streaming disconnect behavior, and model pinning/fallback. Agreed to share Redwood’s security artifacts + draft production launch checklist, and schedule a technical deep dive on audit logs and Private/VPC options.

Agreed to share Redwood’s security artifacts + draft production launch checklist, and schedule a technical deep dive on audit logs and Private/VPC options.

title: Photon Health x Redwood — Private deployment compliance deep dive (retention, audit logs, report pack)
preview: Photon Health drilled into Redwood Private deployment specifics: where different log/telemetry data classes are stored in Private, how retention policies are evaluated/enforced (control plane policy + data plane purge jobs), how deletions are verified, and how the baseline compliance reporting pack will be produced and delivered for customer audits. Photon emphasized requirements around PHI-adjacent prompt data, audit log completeness, evidence repeatability, and a preference for customer-managed storage/KMS with tight RBAC. Redwood clarified that in Private, data plane stores live in Photon’s account/VPC, Redwood provides the policy service + purge job framework, and the compliance pack is generated from audit log exporter outputs plus retention configuration exports, with versioned templates.
text: Photon Health drilled into Redwood Private deployment specifics: where different log/telemetry data classes are stored in Private, how retention policies are evaluated/enforced (control plane policy + data plane purge jobs), how deletions are verified, and how the baseline compliance reporting pack will be produced and delivered for customer audits. Photon emphasized requirements around PHI-adjacent prompt data, audit log completeness, evidence repeatability, and a preference for customer-managed storage/KMS with tight RBAC. Redwood clarified that in Private, data plane stores live in Photon’s account/VPC, Redwood provides the policy service + purge job framework, and the compliance pack is generated from audit log exporter outputs plus retention configuration exports, with versioned templates.

title: Nordic Bank — Security + data retention / residency review (Redwood Inference)
preview: Nordic Bank reviewed Redwood’s security posture with emphasis on EU data residency and strict retention controls. Customer asked for clear answers on what Redwood retains by default (prompts/responses, token/latency telemetry, logs) and how retention can be configured or disabled in Private/VPC. They also requested reporting by region and environment/tier (hosted vs dedicated vs private) to support governance and internal chargeback. Redwood outlined current capabilities (EU regions, private deployment options, RBAC/SSO, audit logs, configurable log retention) and noted that cost/unit-economics drilldowns by region/tier are currently internal but can be shared as aggregated summaries during pilot/QBRs. Next steps include sending security package (SOC2, ISO, pen test summary), a written retention matrix, and scheduling a follow-up focused on Redwood Private architecture and EU-only controls.

title: Northbridge Bank - Security review: retention controls + audit logs (Dedicated/Private)
preview: Northbridge Bank walked through their security review questions focused on data retention, audit logging, and evidence expectations for regulated workloads. Redwood clarified which data classes are in scope for retention controls (request/response logs where enabled, audit logs, traces/metrics, and certain artifacts), how default TTLs differ by Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, and how purge jobs are enforced and verified. Northbridge emphasized need for (a) customer-configurable retention with minimum/maximum guardrails, (b) a clear legal hold / incident hold process, (c) audit-log export coverage including SSO/RBAC/admin actions, and (d) a customer-ready evidence package aligned to SOC 2 / ISO-style controls. Redwood committed to sending the retention controls overview + baseline compliance reporting pack outline, plus a sample audit-log export (redacted) and a list of audit event types currently covered. Next steps include a follow-up deep dive on Private deployment data residency + KMS integration and confirming target retention periods for Northbridge's intended deployment mode.
text: Northbridge Bank walked through their security review questions focused on data retention, audit logging, and evidence expectations for regulated workloads. Redwood clarified which data classes are in scope for retention controls (request/response logs where enabled, audit logs, traces/metrics, and certain artifacts), how default TTLs differ by Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, and how purge jobs are enforced and verified. Northbridge emphasized need for (a) customer-configurable retention with minimum/maximum guardrails, (b) a clear legal hold / incident hold process, (c) audit-log export coverage including SSO/RBAC/admin actions, and (d) a customer-ready evidence package aligned to SOC 2 / ISO-style controls. Redwood committed to sending the retention controls overview + baseline compliance reporting pack outline, plus a sample audit-log export (redacted) and a list of audit event types currently covered. Next steps include a follow-up deep dive on Private deployment data residency + KMS integration and confirming target retention periods for Northbridge's intended deployment mode.

title: Nordholm Payments - Security review (data residency, audit logs, regional failover)
preview: Nordholm Payments reviewed Redwood Hosted controls for EU-only processing, audit logs, and failover behavior. Redwood confirmed planned/active region pinning controls (primary region + allowed region set) with deterministic enforcement at request time and blocked cross-region fallback by default. Customer requested documentation: residency policy/controls narrative, audit log event schema + samples, subprocessor region list, SOC 2 report, and retention details for logs/traces. Next steps: Redwood to send residency FAQ + internal policy excerpt, schedule a short technical follow-up on audit log export + SIEM integration, and confirm timeline for GA of hosted residency enforcement.
